topics.title: Natural rubber: source, chemical formula, molecular weight distribution, crystallisation, structure-property relationship, chemical reactivity, electrical and oxidation properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT501 - RUBBERS-SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Natural rubber: source, chemical formula, molecular weight distribution, crystallisation, structure-property relationship, chemical reactivity, electrical and oxidation properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Natural Rubber (NR)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition:**
> *Natural Rubber (NR) is a high-molecular-weight, stereoregular polymer of isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene) obtained from the latex of the **Hevea brasiliensis** tree, with a predominantly **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** configuration (≥ 98% cis content), exhibiting unique strain-induced crystallisation, high tensile strength in the unfilled state, and outstanding resilience.*

The IUPAC name of the repeating unit is **(Z)-1,4-poly(2-methylbuta-1,3-diene)**, and the empirical formula of the repeat unit is **C₅H₈**, making NR a pure hydrocarbon elastomer with no heteroatom in the backbone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Callout: "The Bouncy Tree Milk Analogy"**
> Imagine a tree (Hevea brasiliensis) as a giant, living "milking cow." When you tap the bark (a process called *tapping*), a milky white fluid called **latex** oozes out — much like sap from a maple tree. This latex is essentially natural rubber in a water-based suspension. If you let the water evaporate, you get a sticky, gummy ball that bounces — your first piece of natural rubber! The "stretchiness" comes from incredibly long, tangled polymer chains that uncoil when pulled (like a plate of spaghetti being lifted with a fork) and snap back when released, much like a spring.

### 1.3 Sources of Natural Rubber

| Source | Botanical Name | Cis-1,4 Content | Commercial Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Para Rubber Tree** (Primary) | *Hevea brasiliensis* | **≥ 98%** | **> 99% of world supply** |
| Guayule | *Parthenium argentatum* | ~ 99% | Hypoallergenic substitute (US/Mexico) |
| Russian Dandelion | *Taraxacum kok-saghyz* | ~ 95% | Strategic substitute (Russia/EU) |
| Balata | *Manilkara bidentata* | trans-1,4 (gutta-percha) | Not true NR (thermoplastic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** *Hevea brasiliensis* is native to the Amazon basin (Brazil) but is now cultivated predominantly in **Thailand, Indonesia, Malaysia, India (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka), Vietnam, and Sri Lanka**. Kerala's tropical climate and laterite soil make it ideal for Hevea cultivation.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific Gravity of NR:** **0.92 – 0.94** (one of the lowest among commercial rubbers → floats on water)
- **Refractive Index:** **1.519** (at 23 °C)
- **Mooney Viscosity (raw, unvulcanised):** **60 – 90 ML(1+4) at 100 °C**
- **Ash content (raw NR):** **≤ 0.5%**
- **Dirt content:** **≤ 0.05%**
- **Volatile matter:** **≤ 0.5%**
- **Latex total solid content:** **30 – 40%**
- **Latex dry rubber content (DRC):** **~ 30%**

### 1.5 Classification of NR Grades (KTU-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **International Commodity Grades (per ISO 2000):**
> - **TSR (Technically Specified Rubber):** TSR 5, TSR 10, TSR 20, TSR CV (Constant Viscosity), TSR L (Latex-grade)
> - **RSS (Ribbed Smoked Sheet):** RSS 1, RSS 2, RSS 3, RSS 4, RSS 5
> - **Specialty grades:** Pale Latex Crepe, Sole Crepe, epoxidised NR (ENR), deproteinised NR (DPNR), liquid NR

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chemical Structure of Natural Rubber — The cis-1,4-Polyisoprene Backbone

Natural rubber is a **linear, head-to-tail, stereoregular** polymer built by 1,4-addition polymerisation of isoprene monomer. Each repeating unit contains a **C=C double bond**, a **pendant methyl group**, and a **methylene (-CH₂-)** segment. The double bond is exclusively in the **Z (cis) configuration**, which is the geometric isomer that confers the elastic behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the C=C double bond so important?** It is the *chemical Achilles' heel* of NR — it is the reactive site for **vulcanisation (sulphur cross-linking), oxidation, ozonolysis, halogenation, epoxidation, maleinisation, and hydrogenation**. The 100% hydrocarbon (no heteroatom) backbone also explains the excellent **electrical insulation** properties.

The cis-1,4 configuration is synthesised biologically by the enzyme **rubber transferase (cis-prenyl transferase)** in the latex vessels (laticifers) of Hevea brasiliensis, using isopentenyl pyrophosphate (IPP) as the biological monomer.

### 2.2 Chemical Formula Representation

**Monomer (isoprene):**
$$ \text{CH}_2=\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2 $$

**Polymer (cis-1,4-polyisoprene):**
$$ \left[-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2-\right]_n $$

**Empirical formula of repeat unit:** **C₅H₈**
**Number-average molecular weight of repeat unit:** **M₀ = 68.12 g/mol**

### 2.3 Molecular Weight Distribution (MWD) — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MWD matters:** Natural rubber is a *polydisperse* polymer — every chain in a sample has a different length. A narrow MWD gives consistent properties and easier processing, while a broad MWD gives high green strength but poor extrusion behaviour.

**Logical Steps to Characterise MWD:**

1. **Fractionate** the polymer into many narrow-MW slices (e.g., by Gel Permeation Chromatography, GPC).
2. **Count** the number of molecules in each slice (Nᵢ) and note their molecular weight (Mᵢ).
3. **Calculate** two key averages:
   - **Number-average molecular weight (M̄ₙ):** weighted by number of molecules — sensitive to small chains.
   - **Weight-average molecular weight (M̄w):** weighted by mass — sensitive to large chains.
4. **Compute** the Polydispersity Index (PDI) — a measure of MWD breadth.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula you must memorise for KTU examinations on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Definition | Typical NR Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Degree of Polymerisation (Xₙ)** | $X_n = \dfrac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0}$ | Average number of repeat units per chain | 3 000 – 7 000 | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Number-Average MW (M̄ₙ)** | $\overline{M_n} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | First moment of MWD | 1.5 × 10⁵ – 4.0 × 10⁵ | g/mol |
| 3 | **Weight-Average MW (M̄w)** | $\overline{M_w} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Second moment of MWD | 5.0 × 10⁵ – 1.5 × 10⁶ | g/mol |
| 4 | **Z-Average MW (M̄z)** | $\overline{M_z} = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^3}{\sum N_i M_i^2}$ | Third moment — sensitive to ultra-high chains | 1.0 × 10⁶ – 3.0 × 10⁶ | g/mol |
| 5 | **Polydispersity Index (PDI / Đ)** | $PDI = \dfrac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$ | MWD breadth | 1.5 – 3.0 (NR is broad) | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Density (ρ) of NR** | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Mass per unit volume | 0.92 – 0.94 | g/cm³ |
| 7 | **Glass Transition Temp (T_g)** | $T_g = -72 °C$ (DSC) | Amorphous-phase transition | – 72 | °C |
| 8 | **Melting Point (T_m)** | $T_m \approx 28-30 °C$ (unstrained) | Crystalline phase melting | 28 – 30 | °C |
| 9 | **Avrami Equation (crystallisation kinetics)** | $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ | Fraction crystallised vs time | k, n depend on T | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Flory Rehner Equation (swelling)** | $\chi = \dfrac{V_1 \left[ \ln(1-v_2) + v_2 + \chi v_2^2 \right]}{v_2^2}$ | Cross-link density (gel) | — | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)** | Empirical (DSC, ASTM D3895) | Time to onset of exotherm at given T | depends on antioxidant | minutes |
| 12 | **Dielectric Constant (ε') of NR** | $\varepsilon' = \dfrac{C_p}{C_0}$ | Charge storage capacity | 2.4 – 2.6 (1 kHz) | dimensionless |
| 13 | **Dielectric Strength** | $E_{bd} = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{d}$ | Breakdown voltage per unit thickness | 18 – 25 | kV/mm |
| 14 | **Volume Resistivity (ρᵥ)** | $\rho_v = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{l}$ | Resistance through bulk | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ | Ω·cm |
| 15 | **Charlesby–Pinner Equation (cross-linking by radiation)** | $s + s^{1/2} = \dfrac{p_0}{q_0} + \dfrac{1}{q_0 u_1 r}$ | Sol fraction + cross-link density | — | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of MWD

- **Tyre tread compounds (NR-based):** Broad MWD (PDI ≈ 2–3) is preferred for **high green strength** and **resistance to cut growth**.
- **Latex dipped goods (gloves, balloons):** Narrow MWD is preferred for **uniform film thickness** and **clarity**.
- **Adhesive grade NR:** Low-MW liquid NR (MW ≈ 10 000) is preferred for **tack** and **wetting**.

### 2.6 Crystallisation in Natural Rubber — The Heart of Self-Reinforcement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Topic:** NR is the *only* commercial elastomer that **crystallises under strain** (strain-induced crystallisation, SIC). This is why an unfilled, unvulcanised NR gum stock can reach **30–35 MPa tensile strength** — comparable to some engineering plastics.

There are **two principal modes** of crystallisation:

**(a) Low-Temperature (Storage) Crystallisation**
- Occurs when NR is held at temperatures between **– 40 °C and + 5 °C** for extended periods (hours to weeks).
- Maximum rate at approximately **– 25 °C**.
- Crystallites are **spherulitic** (α-form and β-form polymorphs).
- Melts on warming to **28 – 30 °C**.

**(b) Strain-Induced Crystallisation (SIC)**
- Occurs when NR is **stretched above ~ 200% elongation**.
- Crystals align **along the stretch direction** as nano-fibrils, acting as **physical cross-links** and **crack stoppers**.
- Reversible: crystals melt instantly on release of strain.
- This is the **single most important reason** NR is irreplaceable in heavy-duty tyres, earthquake bearings, and aircraft engine mounts.

### 2.7 Structure–Property Relationship — The Master Map

| Structural Feature | Resulting Property | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Cis-1,4 configuration (≥ 98%)** | Low Tg, high chain flexibility | Excellent low-temperature elasticity |
| **Linear, stereoregular backbone** | Ability to strain-crystallise | Self-reinforcement, high tensile strength |
| **100% hydrocarbon (no N, O, S, Cl)** | Hydrophobic, non-polar | Excellent electrical insulator, oil resistance is poor |
| **Every repeat unit has C=C** | High unsaturation (chemical reactivity) | Easily vulcanised; easily oxidised |
| **High MW (M̄w ~ 10⁶)** | Long entangled chains | High green strength, tacky |
| **Non-crystallising pendant groups** | No bulky side groups | Chains pack efficiently under strain |
| **Amorphous at rest, crystalline under strain** | Self-reinforcing, hysteresis low | Superior fatigue & cut-growth resistance |

### 2.8 Chemical Reactivity of Natural Rubber

The **C=C double bond** in each isoprene unit is the sole reactive site. Major reactions are:

1. **Vulcanisation (Sulphur cross-linking):** The industrial backbone of rubber technology.
2. **Halogenation:** Chlorination → chlorinated NR (paint binder); Bromination → bromobutyl rubber intermediate.
3. **Hydrogenation:** Hydrogenated NR (HNR) → saturated, ozone-resistant.
4. **Epoxidation:** ENR (Epoxidised Natural Rubber) → improved oil resistance and damping.
5. **Maleinisation:** Maleic-anhydride-grafted NR → compatibiliser for polymer blends.
6. **Cyclisation:** Cyclised NR → hard, resinous material for shoe soles and printing inks.
7. **Hydrochlorination:** Rubber hydrochloride (Pliofilm) → packaging film.
8. **Oxidation / Ozonolysis:** Undesirable degradation reactions (see §2.10).

### 2.9 Electrical Properties of NR

> [!NOTE]
> Natural rubber is an **outstanding dielectric** because its backbone contains only C and H atoms — no polar groups, no ions, no conjugated π-systems. The pendant -CH₃ groups are weakly electron-donating but do not introduce significant polarity.

| Electrical Property | Value | Test Method |
|---|---|---|
| Volume resistivity, ρᵥ | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁶ Ω·cm | ASTM D257 |
| Surface resistivity, ρₛ | 10¹² – 10¹⁴ Ω/sq | ASTM D257 |
| Dielectric constant, ε' (1 kHz) | 2.4 – 2.6 | ASTM D150 |
| Dielectric loss tangent, tan δ (1 kHz) | 0.002 – 0.005 | ASTM D150 |
| Dielectric strength | 18 – 25 kV/mm | ASTM D149 |
| Tracking resistance | Class 1a (poor weather) | IEC 60112 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Caveat:** NR is a *poor* insulator in **outdoor, high-humidity, or contaminated environments** because the surface is hydrophobic but becomes conductive if coated with dirt/ionic contaminants. For high-voltage outdoor insulation, **silicone rubber or EPDM** is preferred.

### 2.10 Oxidation Properties of Natural Rubber — The Auto-Oxidation Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Question:** "Explain the auto-oxidation of natural rubber with the mechanism."

Natural rubber is highly susceptible to **oxidative degradation** because of the C=C double bond and the **allylic hydrogen** on the methylene carbon α to the double bond. The mechanism follows the classic **Bolland–Gee free-radical chain mechanism** (also called the **Bolis' cycle**):

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
$$ \text{RH} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\,h\nu,\,\text{mech}} \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{H}^{\cdot} $$

**Step 2 — Propagation (oxygen addition):**
$$ \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\cdot} $$
$$ \text{ROO}^{\cdot} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\cdot} $$

**Step 3 — Branching (hydroperoxide decomposition):**
$$ \text{ROOH} \rightarrow \text{RO}^{\cdot} + {}^{\cdot}\text{OH} $$

**Step 4 — Termination (radical combination):**
$$ \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{R}^{\cdot} \rightarrow \text{R-R} $$
$$ \text{ROO}^{\cdot} + \text{ROO}^{\cdot} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consequences of Oxidation:** Chain scission → softening, tackiness, surface cracking; Cross-linking → hardening, embrittlement; Discolouration → brown, then tacky residue. **Activation energy (Eₐ) for NR oxidation ≈ 90–105 kJ/mol** in the uninhibited state. Antioxidants (e.g., **6PPD, IPPD, TMQ, BHT**) lower Eₐ to > 130 kJ/mol and extend service life by 10–100×.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Number-Average and Weight-Average Molecular Weights

Consider a polymer sample fractionated into discrete slices. Let slice *i* contain **Nᵢ** molecules, each with molecular weight **Mᵢ**.

#### Derivation 3.1.1 — Number-Average Molecular Weight (M̄ₙ)

The **total number of molecules** in the sample is:

$$ N_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i $$

The **total mass** of the sample is:

$$ m_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i M_i $$

The **number-average molecular weight** is defined as the total mass divided by the total number of molecules:

$$ \overline{M_n} = \frac{m_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i} N_i} $$

> **Textual logic:** Every molecule — whether short or long — contributes equally to the *count* in the denominator. Hence M̄ₙ is biased toward the **low-molecular-weight chains** because there are simply more of them.

#### Derivation 3.1.2 — Weight-Average Molecular Weight (M̄w)

Define the **mass fraction** of slice *i* as:

$$ w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j} $$

The weight-average is then the mass-fraction-weighted sum of Mᵢ:

$$ \overline{M_w} = \sum_i w_i M_i = \sum_i \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_j N_j M_j} M_i = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2}{\sum_i N_i M_i} $$

> **Textual logic:** A heavy chain contributes *twice* to M̄w — once to the mass and once to the Mᵢ multiplier. Therefore M̄w is biased toward the **high-molecular-weight chains**.

#### Derivation 3.1.3 — Polydispersity Index (PDI)

By definition:

$$ PDI = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{\sum_i N_i M_i^2 \big/ \sum_i N_i M_i}{\sum_i N_i M_i \big/ \sum_i N_i} = \frac{\left(\sum_i N_i M_i^2\right)\left(\sum_i N_i\right)}{\left(\sum_i N_i M_i\right)^2} $$

> **Textual logic:** A *monodisperse* sample (all chains identical) gives PDI = 1.0. The further PDI deviates above 1, the broader the MWD. For commercial NR, **PDI ≈ 1.5–3.0**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — MWD Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Style Question (worked out):**
> A natural rubber sample is fractionated into 5 fractions with the following data:
>
> | Fraction *i* | Nᵢ (× 10¹⁶) | Mᵢ (× 10⁵ g/mol) |
> |---|---|---|
> | 1 | 2.0 | 1.0 |
> | 2 | 4.0 | 2.5 |
> | 3 | 3.0 | 5.0 |
> | 4 | 1.5 | 8.0 |
> | 5 | 0.5 | 12.0 |
>
> Calculate M̄ₙ, M̄w, and PDI.

**Solution — Step 1: Build the master table**

| i | Nᵢ | Mᵢ | NᵢMᵢ | NᵢMᵢ² |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 1.0 | 2.0 × 10¹⁶ | 2.0 × 10¹⁶ |
| 2 | 4.0 | 2.5 | 10.0 × 10¹⁶ | 25.0 × 10¹⁶ |
| 3 | 3.0 | 5.0 | 15.0 × 10¹⁶ | 75.0 × 10¹⁶ |
| 4 | 1.5 | 8.0 | 12.0 × 10¹⁶ | 96.0 × 10¹⁶ |
| 5 | 0.5 | 12.0 | 6.0 × 10¹⁶ | 72.0 × 10¹⁶ |
| **Σ** | **11.0 × 10¹⁶** | — | **45.0 × 10¹⁶** | **270.0 × 10¹⁶** |

**Solution — Step 2: Calculate M̄ₙ**

$$ \overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} = \frac{45.0 \times 10^{16}}{11.0 \times 10^{16}} = 4.09 \times 10^5 \ \text{g/mol} $$

**Solution — Step 3: Calculate M̄w**

$$ \overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} = \frac{270.0 \times 10^{16}}{45.0 \times 10^{16}} = 6.00 \times 10^5 \ \text{g/mol} $$

**Solution — Step 4: Calculate PDI**

$$ PDI = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} = \frac{6.00 \times 10^5}{4.09 \times 10^5} = 1.466 $$

**Solution — Step 5: Calculate Degree of Polymerisation**

$$ X_n = \frac{\overline{M_n}}{M_0} = \frac{4.09 \times 10^5}{68.12} \approx 6004 $$

> **Textual Interpretation:** The PDI of 1.47 is a moderately narrow MWD — close to that of a well-controlled synthetic polyisoprene (PDI ≈ 1.05–1.10), but broader than commercial NR (typical PDI = 1.5–3.0). This suggests the sample is either a fractionated NR cut or a narrow-distribution synthetic analogue.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Avrami Equation for Crystallisation Kinetics

The **Avrami equation** describes the time evolution of the crystalline fraction **X(t)** in a polymer melt or rubber.

**Assumptions:**
- Crystallisation occurs by **nucleation** (instantaneous or sporadic) and **3-D growth**.
- The transformed regions are *spherical* (or fibrillar in the case of SIC).

**Derivation — Step 1: Probability of an untransformed point**

The probability **P** that a randomly chosen point in the material is *not yet* in a transformed region at time *t* is:

$$ P = \exp(-V_{ext}) $$

where **V_ext** is the expected total extended volume of all growing nuclei at time *t*.

**Derivation — Step 2: Extended volume for a single nucleus**

For a single nucleus growing from time τ to time t with 3-D linear growth rate G:

$$ v_{ext}(\tau) = \frac{4\pi}{3} G^3 (t-\tau)^3 $$

**Derivation — Step 3: Integrate over nucleation rate**

If nucleation is **sporadic** with rate $\dot{N}$ (nuclei per unit volume per unit time):

$$ V_{ext} = \int_0^t \frac{4\pi}{3} G^3 (t-\tau)^3 \dot{N}\, d\tau = \frac{\pi}{3} \dot{N} G^3 t^4 $$

**Derivation — Step 4: Crystalline fraction**

$$ X(t) = 1 - P = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\pi}{3} \dot{N} G^3 t^4\right) $$

**Derivation — Step 5: Generalised Avrami form**

$$ \boxed{X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)} $$

where:
- $k = \dfrac{\pi}{3} \dot{N} G^3$ (rate constant)
- $n = 4$ for sporadic 3-D nucleation
- $n = 3$ for instantaneous 3-D nucleation (athermal)

> **For Natural Rubber:** During **strain-induced crystallisation**, $n \approx 1$ to $2$ (1-D fibrillar growth along stretch axis), and $k$ depends strongly on **strain, temperature, and cross-link density**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property ↔ Structure ↔ Engineering Use

| # | Property | Controlling Structure | Engineering Use | KTU Typical Answer Phrase |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | High tensile strength (unfilled) | Strain-induced crystallisation | Tyre carcass, conveyor belts | "NR crystallises upon stretching" |
| 2 | Low hysteresis | Entropy-driven elasticity | Engine mounts, springs | "Low energy loss per cycle" |
| 3 | Excellent tack | High MW, broad MWD | Tyre building, plies | "Self-adhesion of uncured layers" |
| 4 | Poor oil resistance | Non-polar backbone | Limit use in fuels | "Hydrocarbon solubility" |
| 5 | Easy vulcanisation | Allylic C-H, C=C | Sulphur/accelerator cure | "Sulphur cross-links form readily" |
| 6 | Good electrical insulator | No polar groups | Cable sheaths, gloves | "Volume resistivity ~ 10¹⁵ Ω·cm" |
| 7 | Poor ozone resistance | C=C + allylic H | Use wax or antioxidant | "Surface cracking" |
| 8 | Low-temperature flexibility | Low Tg (– 72 °C) | Aircraft seals | "Tg below – 70 °C" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complete NR Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hevea brasiliensis Tree] --> B[Tapping Bark]
    B --> C[Latex Collection Cup]
    C --> D[Field Latex - 30 to 40 percent solids]
    D --> E{Processing Route}
    E --> F[Acid Coagulation]
    E --> G[Centrifugation]
    E --> H[Smoked Sheet]
    F --> I[Crepe Rubber]
    F --> J[TSR Block Rubber]
    G --> K[Concentrated Latex - 60 percent DRC]
    H --> L[RSS Grade NR]
    I --> M[Crepe Grade NR]
    J --> N[TSR 5, TSR 10, TSR 20]
    K --> O[Dipped Goods - Gloves, Balloons]
    L --> P[Compounding and Vulcanisation]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Final Rubber Product - Tyre, Gasket, Seal]
    style A fill:#e6f7ff
    style D fill:#fff7e6
    style Q fill:#d9f7be
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Chemical Structure & Reactive Sites of cis-1,4-Polyisoprene

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MonomerUnit[Monomer: Isoprene C5H8]
        A1[CH2 double bond C CH3] --> A2[CH double bond CH2]
    end
    MonomerUnit --> PolyReaction[1,4-Polymerisation]
    PolyReaction --> RepeatUnit[Repeat Unit: -CH2-C CH3 equal CH-CH2-]
    subgraph ReactiveSites[Reactive Sites in Repeat Unit]
        R1[A: Carbon Carbon Double Bond]
        R2[B: Allylic Methylene CH2]
        R3[C: Pendant Methyl CH3 - Inert]
    end
    RepeatUnit --> ReactiveSites
    R1 --> P1[Vulcanisation with Sulphur]
    R1 --> P2[Halogenation - Cl2, Br2]
    R1 --> P3[Epoxidation with mCPBA]
    R2 --> P4[Oxidation - O2 Attack]
    R2 --> P5[Ozonolysis - O3 Attack]
    R1 --> P6[Free Radical Grafting]
    style RepeatUnit fill:#ffe7ba
    style R1 fill:#ffccc7
    style R2 fill:#ffccc7
    style R3 fill:#d9d9d9
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Auto-Oxidation Mechanism of NR (Bolland-Gee Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Init[Initiation: RH equal R dot + H dot] --> P1[Propagation 1: R dot + O2 equal ROO dot]
    P1 --> P2[Propagation 2: ROO dot + RH equal ROOH + R dot]
    P2 --> Branch[Hydroperoxide Decomposition: ROOH equal RO dot + OH dot]
    Branch --> Attack1[RO dot attacks RH - chain scission]
    Branch --> Attack2[OH dot attacks RH - chain scission]
    Attack1 --> Term[Termination]
    Attack2 --> Term
    P2 -.feedback.-> Branch
    Term --> T1[R dot + R dot equal R-R]
    Term --> T2[ROO dot + ROO dot equal ROOR + O2]
    Term --> T3[ROO dot + R dot equal ROOR]
    T1 --> Out[Result: Cross-linking, Hardening, Embrittlement]
    T2 --> Out
    T3 --> Out
    Attack1 --> Out2[Result: Chain Scission, Softening, Tackiness]
    style Init fill:#ffd6d6
    style P1 fill:#fff7e6
    style P2 fill:#fff7e6
    style Branch fill:#ffe7ba
    style Out fill:#d9f7be
    style Out2 fill:#ffccc7
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Structure-Property Causality in Natural Rubber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Structure[Structural Features]
        S1[98 percent cis 1,4]
        S2[Linear Chain]
        S3[High MW - 10 power 6]
        S4[Pure Hydrocarbon]
        S5[Allylic CH2]
    end
    subgraph Property[Resulting Property]
        P1[Strain Induced Crystallisation]
        P2[High Tensile Strength]
        P3[Low Hysteresis]
        P4[High Green Strength]
        P5[Electrical Insulator]
        P6[Easy Vulcanisation]
        P7[Oxidation Sensitive]
    end
    subgraph Application[Engineering Use]
        U1[Heavy Duty Tyres]
        U2[Engine Mounts]
        U3[Cable Insulation]
        U4[Earthquake Bearings]
    end
    S1 --> P1
    S1 --> P3
    S2 --> P1
    S2 --> P4
    S3 --> P4
    S4 --> P5
    S5 --> P6
    S5 --> P7
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> U1
    P3 --> U2
    P5 --> U3
    P4 --> U1
    P4 --> U4
    style S1 fill:#bae7ff
    style P1 fill:#ffe7ba
    style U1 fill:#d9f7be
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Crystallisation Modes in NR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NR[Amorphous NR at Rest] --> Cond1{Condition}
    Cond1 -->|T minus 25 °C, t several hours| LTC[Low Temperature Crystallisation]
    Cond1 -->|Strain greater than 200 percent| SIC[Strain Induced Crystallisation]
    Cond1 -->|Storage at RT long time| Slow[Slow Age Hardening]
    LTC --> Cryst1[Spherulitic Crystals - alpha and beta forms]
    SIC --> Cryst2[Nano Fibrils along Stretch Axis]
    Slow --> Cryst3[Mixed Morphology]
    Cryst1 --> Melt1[Melts at 28 to 30 °C]
    Cryst2 --> Melt2[Melts on Stress Release]
    Cryst3 --> Melt3[Melts on Warming]
    Melt1 --> NR
    Melt2 --> NR
    Melt3 --> NR
    style NR fill:#e6f7ff
    style SIC fill:#ffd6d6
    style LTC fill:#fff7e6
    style Cryst2 fill:#d9f7be
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define natural rubber. Write its chemical formula and the biological source.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Natural rubber (NR) is a high-molecular-weight, stereoregular polymer of isoprene (2-methyl-1,3-butadiene), with a predominantly **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** configuration (≥ 98% cis content), obtained as a latex from the **Hevea brasiliensis** tree (family *Euphorbiaceae*).

**Chemical formula of repeat unit:** $\text{C}_5\text{H}_8$ (molar mass = 68.12 g/mol)

**Polymer structure:** $\left[-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2-\right]_n$

**Biological source:** The latex (milky sap) contained in the laticiferous vessels of the bark of *Hevea brasiliensis*, a tree native to the Amazon basin and cultivated extensively in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Sri Lanka, Thailand, Indonesia, and Malaysia.

---

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **Differentiate between number-average and weight-average molecular weight. State typical NR values.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Number-Average MW (M̄ₙ) | Weight-Average MW (M̄w) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | $\overline{M_n} = \sum N_i M_i \big/ \sum N_i$ | $\overline{M_w} = \sum N_i M_i^2 \big/ \sum N_i M_i$ |
| Bias | Sensitive to **low-MW chains** (small molecules count equally) | Sensitive to **high-MW chains** (heavy chains dominate) |
| Determination | Osmometry, end-group analysis, vapour pressure | Light scattering, sedimentation, GPC with MALLS |
| Typical NR value | 1.5 × 10⁵ – 4.0 × 10⁵ g/mol | 5.0 × 10⁵ – 1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol |
| Physical meaning | Average chain length per molecule | Average chain length per unit mass |

> M̄w ≥ M̄ₙ always; for monodisperse polymer M̄w = M̄ₙ, and for NR the ratio (PDI) is typically **1.5 – 3.0**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **(a)** Describe the **source, extraction, and chemical structure** of natural rubber. Discuss how the **cis-1,4 configuration** controls its elastic behaviour. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **molecular weight distribution** of natural rubber. Derive the expressions for **M̄ₙ, M̄w, and PDI**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Source:** Natural rubber is obtained from the latex of *Hevea brasiliensis* (Para rubber tree), cultivated in tropical regions (Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Sri Lanka, Thailand, Indonesia, Malaysia). The tree is tapped by spiral or V-cut incisions on the bark, releasing a milky white **latex** that is a colloidal suspension of rubber particles (0.05 – 3 µm diameter) in water.

**Extraction:**
- **Concentration:** Field latex (30 – 40% DRC) is centrifuged to 60% concentrated latex.
- **Coagulation:** Acetic or formic acid is added → rubber particles coalesce → coagulum.
- **Sheeting:** Coagulum is passed through rollers → crepe or sheet.
- **Drying:** Air-dried or smoke-dried (RSS grades) at ~ 40 °C.
- **Baling:** Compressed into 33.33 kg or 50 kg bales for export.

**Chemical structure:** Each repeat unit is a **cis-1,4-isoprene** unit:
$$ \left[-\text{CH}_2-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2-\right]_n \quad \text{where } n \approx 3000 - 7000 $$

**How cis-1,4 controls elasticity:**
- The **Z (cis) configuration** forces the chain into a **distorted, kinked conformation** that prevents close packing at rest.
- This kinked chain has a **high conformational entropy** in the coiled state.
- On stretching, chains uncoil into a more ordered (crystalline) state with **lower entropy**.
- On release, entropy increases → chain recoils → **elastomeric recovery**.
- A *trans-1,4* configuration (e.g., gutta-percha) would be **stiff and crystalline** at room temperature — hence not an elastomer.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

Natural rubber is a *polydisperse* polymer — its chains have a distribution of molecular weights, typically characterised by the **number-average (M̄ₙ)** and **weight-average (M̄w)** molecular weights.

**Derivation of M̄ₙ:** Let a polymer sample be fractionated into *i* slices, each containing Nᵢ molecules of molecular weight Mᵢ.

Total number of molecules:
$$ N_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i $$

Total mass of sample:
$$ m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i M_i $$

By definition:
$$ \boxed{\overline{M_n} = \frac{m_{total}}{N_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}} $$

**Derivation of M̄w:** Mass fraction in slice *i*:
$$ w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} N_j M_j} $$

Weight-average is mass-fraction-weighted Mᵢ:
$$ \boxed{\overline{M_w} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i M_i = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i M_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i M_i}} $$

**Polydispersity Index:**
$$ \boxed{PDI = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}} \geq 1} $$

**Typical NR values:** M̄ₙ ≈ 1.5 × 10⁵ – 4.0 × 10⁵ g/mol; M̄w ≈ 5.0 × 10⁵ – 1.5 × 10⁶ g/mol; PDI ≈ 1.5 – 3.0.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly (i) confuse the *formulas* of M̄ₙ and M̄w (writing M̄ₙ with M² in numerator), (ii) forget that PDI ≥ 1 always, (iii) fail to state the *units* (g/mol), and (iv) miss stating that NR is a *polydisperse* polymer. Deduct 1 mark for each omission in KTU valuation.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** What is **strain-induced crystallisation** in natural rubber? Explain with reference to the **structure–property relationship** and discuss its **engineering significance**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **oxidation mechanism** of natural rubber with suitable chemical equations. Explain the **role of antioxidants** in retarding the degradation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Definition:** Strain-induced crystallisation (SIC) is the *reversible formation of nano-crystallites* in natural rubber when it is subjected to mechanical deformation (stretching) above a critical strain (typically 200 – 300% elongation).

**Mechanism:**
- The **cis-1,4, stereoregular** chain backbone has no bulky substituents and is geometrically uniform.
- Upon stretching, the chains align along the stretch axis, allowing the **methyl groups** to pack into an ordered **orthorhombic crystal lattice**.
- Crystallisation is **thermodynamically driven** by the reduction in entropy difference between stretched and unstretched states (Flory's theory).
- The crystals act as **physical cross-links** and **nano-fibril reinforcements**, dramatically increasing modulus and strength.

**Structure–property relationship:**

| Structural feature | Resulting property |
|---|---|
| Cis-1,4 stereoregularity | Chain alignment under strain |
| Linear (no branches) | Crystallite perfection |
| Pendant methyl on every 4th carbon | Lattice packing |
| Absence of bulky side groups | Low steric hindrance |

**Engineering significance:**
1. **Tyre technology:** SIC gives NR its legendary cut-growth resistance and chipping resistance in truck/bus radial tyres. Crystals blunt crack tips.
2. **Earthquake-resistant bearings:** SIC allows NR to absorb seismic energy with low permanent set.
3. **High tensile strength unfilled:** Unfilled NR gum has tensile strength of 25 – 35 MPa, comparable to engineering plastics.
4. **Fatigue resistance:** Reversible crystals = no permanent damage from cyclic loading.
5. **Self-reinforcement:** Eliminates the need for high carbon-black loading in some applications.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Auto-oxidation of NR (Bolland–Gee mechanism):**

**Initiation:**
$$ \text{RH} \xrightarrow{\Delta,\,h\nu,\,\text{mech}} \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{H}^{\cdot} $$

**Propagation:**
$$ \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\cdot} $$
$$ \text{ROO}^{\cdot} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\cdot} $$

**Branching (hydroperoxide decomposition):**
$$ \text{ROOH} \rightarrow \text{RO}^{\cdot} + {}^{\cdot}\text{OH} $$

**Termination (radical combination):**
$$ \text{R}^{\cdot} + \text{R}^{\cdot} \rightarrow \text{R-R} $$
$$ \text{ROO}^{\cdot} + \text{ROO}^{\cdot} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2 $$
$$ \text{ROO}^{\cdot} + \text{R}^{\cdot} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} $$

> where R = NR backbone (with allylic H abstracted) and ROOH is the rubber hydroperoxide.

**Role of antioxidants:**

1. **Primary (chain-breaking) antioxidants — H-donors:** e.g., hindered phenols (BHT, Irganox 1010), aromatic amines (6PPD, IPPD).
$$ \text{ROO}^{\cdot} + \text{AH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{A}^{\cdot} $$
The antioxidant radical A· is resonance-stabilised and does *not* propagate the chain.

2. **Secondary (preventive) antioxidants — hydroperoxide decomposers:** e.g., phosphites (TNPP), thioethers (DLTDP), zinc dialkyldithiocarbamates.
$$ \text{ROOH} + \text{P}(\text{OR})_3 \rightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{O=P}(\text{OR})_3 $$

3. **UV stabilisers / antiozonants:** Waxes (migrate to surface), nickel dithiocarbamates, paraphenylenediamines (6PPD provides both AO and antiozonant action).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In KTU valuation, students often (i) forget to show the **allylic H** (the C–H α to the C=C) which is the most easily abstracted H in NR, (ii) miss the *branching* step (the auto-catalytic nature of the cycle), and (iii) fail to classify antioxidants as *primary* vs *secondary*. Deduct 1–2 marks for each omission.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorise This Entire Block:**

- ☐ NR = **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** (≥ 98% cis); repeat unit = C₅H₈; M₀ = **68.12 g/mol**.
- ☐ Source = *Hevea brasiliensis* latex; tapped, coagulated, dried → RSS, TSR, or crepe.
- ☐ M̄ₙ formula = ΣNᵢMᵢ / ΣNᵢ; M̄w formula = ΣNᵢMᵢ² / ΣNᵢMᵢ; PDI = M̄w / M̄ₙ.
- ☐ Typical NR: M̄ₙ ≈ 10⁵, M̄w ≈ 10⁶, PDI ≈ 1.5 – 3.0 (broad polydisperse).
- ☐ **Xₙ = M̄ₙ / 68.12** → typical Xₙ = 3 000 – 7 000.
- ☐ **Strain-induced crystallisation** is unique to NR → high tensile strength unfilled.
- ☐ Crystallisation requires **stereoregularity, linear chains, no bulky groups**.
- ☐ Glass transition Tg = **– 72 °C**; melting Tm = **28 – 30 °C** (unstrained).
- ☐ **Avrami equation:** $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ — use n ≈ 1 for SIC, n ≈ 3–4 for 3-D bulk crystallisation.
- ☐ **Auto-oxidation** is a free-radical chain (Bolland–Gee) with initiation, propagation, branching, and termination.
- ☐ **Activation energy** for NR oxidation ≈ 90–105 kJ/mol (uninhibited); antioxidants raise this to > 130 kJ/mol.
- ☐ **Electrical properties:** ρᵥ = 10¹⁴–10¹⁶ Ω·cm; ε' = 2.4–2.6; tan δ = 0.002–0.005; dielectric strength = 18–25 kV/mm.
- ☐ **Chemical reactivity** is centred on the C=C double bond (vulcanisation, halogenation, epoxidation, hydrogenation, maleinisation, cyclisation).
- ☐ **Structure–property mantra:** "Cis-1,4 + linear + stereoregular + pure hydrocarbon + high MW = high-strength, self-reinforcing, hydrophobic, electrically insulating, easily vulcanisable, but easily oxidised elastomer."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various forms of natural rubber: crumb, sheet, crepe, SP rubber, DPNR, liquid rubber, powdered rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT501 - RUBBERS-SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Various forms of natural rubber: crumb, sheet, crepe, SP rubber, DPNR, liquid rubber, powdered rubber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Various Forms of Natural Rubber"?

Natural Rubber (NR), as obtained from the latex of *Hevea brasiliensis*, is never used in its raw tapped form in industry. It must be converted into one of several **standardized commercial forms** that satisfy handling, storage, transportation, and processing requirements. Each commercial form has a unique processing pathway that alters the physical state, surface characteristics, protein content, or molecular weight of the base polymer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Commercial natural rubber is marketed in a variety of physical forms — namely sheet rubber, crepe rubber, crumb/granulated rubber, superior processing (SP) rubber, deproteinized natural rubber (DPNR), liquid natural rubber (LNR), and powdered natural rubber — each differing in method of coagulation, drying, preservation, and field of application.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Milk to Butter" Analogy

Think of fresh field latex as **raw cow's milk**:
- If you pour it into flat trays and let it dry → you get **Sheet Rubber** (like drying milk into a thin film).
- If you roll and crumple it repeatedly → you get **Crepe Rubber** (like churning milk into textured butter).
- If you break it into small granules and dry them → you get **Crumb Rubber** (like making milk powder granules).
- If you chemically chop up the long chains → you get **Liquid Rubber** (like turning thick cream into pouring cream).
- If you remove the proteins (the "impurities") → you get **DPNR** (like skimmed, purified milk).

### 1.3 Why Multiple Forms Exist?

| Need in Industry | Form that Solves It |
|---|---|
| Easy bulk transport | Crumb rubber (granules flow easily) |
| Cleaner factory hygiene | Pale crepe (washed, low impurity) |
| Specialized medical/electrical uses | DPNR (low protein, low water sensitivity) |
| Easy moulding & dispersion | Powdered rubber |
| Coatings & castable applications | Liquid natural rubber |
| Improved green strength | SP rubber (prevulcanized blend) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The form of NR you choose **directly determines** the compounding recipe, mixing energy, vulcanization kinetics, and final product properties. This is a frequently tested board concept.

### 1.4 Standard Classification Overview

The forms are broadly classified into:

- **Solid bulk forms**: Sheet (RSS), Crepe (PC, EPC), Crumb (TSR grades like SMR, SIR, ISNR)
- **Modified forms**: SP rubber, DPNR
- **Liquid/Low-viscosity forms**: Liquid Natural Rubber (LNR), Powdered NR

Each form is governed by international standards (ISO, ASTM, SMR/Malaysian, ISNR/Indian specifications).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Data Sheet

### 2.1 Sheet Rubber — Ribbed Smoked Sheet (RSS)

**Processing Logic:**
1. Field latex → diluted with water → filtered
2. Coagulated using **acetic acid or formic acid** in long aluminum tanks
3. Coagulum passed through series of rollers to form thin sheets (~**3 mm** thickness)
4. Sheets hung in **smokehouses** for 4–7 days at 40–50 °C
5. Smoke from burning wood imparts **natural antioxidants (phenols)** that prevent bacterial attack

**Grades (per Green Book International standards):**
- **RSS1 (highest quality, pale brown)**
- **RSS2, RSS3, RSS4, RSS5 (progressively darker, more impurities)**

**Key Property Range:**

$$ \text{Mooney Viscosity (ML}_{1+4}\text{ at 100 °C)} \approx 60 \text{–} 90 $$

> [!NOTE]
> The ribbed pattern on the sheet is created by grooved rollers — this **increases surface area**, accelerating smoke absorption and drying.

### 2.2 Crepe Rubber

**Processing Logic:**
1. Latex → coagulated using **sodium bisulphite + acetic acid**
2. Coagulum passed through **two-roll mills with differential speeds**
3. Crumpled (creped) repeatedly → produces characteristic **crinkled texture**
4. Washed thoroughly with water to remove serum impurities
5. **Air-dried** at ambient temperature (NOT smoked) → gives pale color

**Grades:**
- **PC (Pale Crepe)** – thinnest, whitest, premium grade
- **EPC (Estate Pale Crepe)** – slightly tinted
- **White Crepe, Thin Pale Crepe**

**Distinctive Feature:** Crepe contains fewer natural antioxidants than RSS because it is **not smoked**. Hence it requires **careful storage** and is typically used in **light-colored goods**.

### 2.3 Crumb Rubber — Technically Specified Rubber (TSR)

The most industrially important form today. Produced in standardized grades like **SMR (Standard Malaysian Rubber), SIR (Standard Indonesian Rubber), ISNR (Indian Standard Natural Rubber), TTR, NSR**.

**Production Methods:**

| Method | Mechanism | Typical Grade |
|---|---|---|
| Heveacrumb | Coagulation → granulation → drying | SMR |
| Mechano-chemical | Coagulation + mechanical crumb formation | SMR GP |
| Spray drying | Atomized latex → hot air | SMR LV |

**Key Specifications for SMR 5 (benchmark):**

$$ \text{Dirt content} \leq 0.05\% \text{ by mass} $$

$$ \text{Ash content} \leq 0.60\% \text{ by mass} $$

$$ \text{Volatile matter} \leq 1.00\% \text{ by mass} $$

$$ \text{Nitrogen content} \leq 0.60\% \text{ by mass} $$

$$ \text{Po (Plasticity Retention Index)} \geq 30 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRI (Plasticity Retention Index)** is a measure of **oxidation resistance**. A high PRI indicates the rubber will resist ageing during storage. This is a **guaranteed property** for TSR but NOT for sheet/crepe.

### 2.4 SP (Superior Processing) Rubber

A **physical blend** of:
- **20% prevulcanized latex** (latex already vulcanized before coagulation)
- **80% normally coagulated latex**

**Why this combination?**
The prevulcanized particles act as **pre-formed crosslinked nuclei**, improving:
- Green strength (uncured strength)
- Tack (stickiness between layers)
- Resistance to collapse during extrusion

**Applications:** Thin-walled extruded profiles, dipping goods, foam rubber.

### 2.5 DPNR (Deproteinized Natural Rubber)

**Process:** Latex treated with **proteolytic enzymes** (e.g., alcalase) + centrifugation, OR repeatedly washed with surfactant, OR irradiated.

**Effect on protein content:**

$$ \text{Protein content reduction: } \approx 0.3\% \rightarrow < 0.02\% $$

**Property Changes:**

| Property | Effect of Deproteinization |
|---|---|
| Water absorption | **Decreases significantly** |
| Dynamic hysteresis | **Lower heat build-up** |
| Allergic reactions (Latex allergy) | **Minimized** |
| Crystallization rate | **Increased** |
| Electrical resistivity | **Improved** |

> [!NOTE]
> DPNR is used in **medical gloves, condoms, catheters, electrical insulation** where protein-triggered allergies and water sensitivity must be avoided.

### 2.6 Liquid Natural Rubber (LNR)

**Molecular weight reduction:**

$$ M_w \text{ (solid NR)} \approx 10^6 \text{–} 10^7 $$

$$ M_w \text{ (LNR)} \approx 10^3 \text{–} 10^5 $$

**Production Routes:**
1. **Mastication (mechanical breakdown)** — oldest method, introduces uncontrolled chain scission
2. **Oxidative depolymerization using phenylhydrazine / air** — controlled
3. **Redox peptization** — modern controlled method
4. **Photolytic / ultrasonic degradation** — lab scale

**Applications:** Castable rubbers, adhesives, sealants, viscosity modifiers for bitumen/rubber blends, rocket propellant binder component.

### 2.7 Powdered Natural Rubber

**Concept:** NR in **free-flowing powder form** (particle size typically **0.1–1.0 mm**).

**Production Methods:**
- Spray drying of latex
- Coagulation under high shear with anti-caking agents (e.g., calcium carbonate, starch)
- Cryogenic grinding of solid rubber

**Advantage:** Eliminates the need for **mastication before mixing** — saves **30–40% mixing energy** in industrial Banbury mixers.

**Limitation:** Risk of agglomeration during storage; needs anti-caking additives.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Comparison Data Sheet

| Property | RSS Sheet | Crepe | TSR Crumb | SP | DPNR | LNR | Powdered |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Smoked sheet | Air-dried crinkled | Granules | Granules/sheet | Granules | Viscous liquid | Free-flowing powder |
| **Drying** | Smoke | Air | Hot air | Air/smoke | Hot air | — | Spray |
| **Color** | Brown | Pale yellow/white | Brown | Brown | Pale | Amber | Variable |
| **Protein %** | 0.3–0.5 | 0.2–0.3 | 0.3–0.5 | 0.3–0.5 | <0.05 | Variable | 0.3–0.5 |
| **Mooney ML(1+4)** | 60–90 | 50–80 | 50–80 | 60–90 | 50–80 | Liquid | 50–80 |
| **PRI (min)** | Not specified | Not specified | ≥30 | ≥30 | ≥60 | — | Variable |
| **Main use** | Tyre carcasses | Light-coloured goods | General purpose | Extrusions, foam | Medical, electrical | Adhesives, castables | Direct mixing |
| **Standard** | Green Book | Green Book | ISO 2322 | ISO | ASTM | — | ISO |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **SP rubber with prevulcanized latex rubber**. SP is a *blend* of prevulcanized + fresh latex coagulated together. Plain prevulcanized rubber is made by vulcanizing the latex *before* coagulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Plasticity Retention Index (PRI) — Used in TSR Grading

PRI is the **quantitative indicator of oxidation resistance** of NR. This is the most frequently asked derivation-type question in KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Measure initial plasticity (P₀)**

A standardized disc of rubber is compressed to a fixed thickness (1 mm) under a load of 100 N for 15 seconds. The thickness in 0.01 mm units = **P₀**.

$$ P_0 = \text{Initial Wallace plasticity (in 0.01 mm units)} $$

**Step 2 — Aged plasticity (P₃₀)**

An identical disc is aged in a **circulating air oven at 140 °C for 30 minutes**, then measured similarly.

$$ P_{30} = \text{Wallace plasticity after ageing at 140 °C for 30 min} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate PRI**

$$ \text{PRI} = \frac{P_{30}}{P_0} \times 100 $$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example for SMR 5:**

Given: P₀ = 40, P₃₀ = 36

$$ \text{PRI} = \frac{36}{40} \times 100 = 90 $$

Since 90 ≥ 30, the rubber **meets SMR 5 specification**.

**Step 5 — Interpretation Logic:**

$$ \text{If PRI} \geq 60 \rightarrow \text{Excellent oxidation resistance (Special grade)} $$

$$ \text{If 30} \leq \text{PRI} < 60 \rightarrow \text{Acceptable (Standard grades)} $$

$$ \text{If PRI} < 30 \rightarrow \text{Reject (poor ageing behaviour)} $$

### 3.2 Derivation: Mooney Viscosity to Molecular Weight Correlation (Empirical)

For raw natural rubber, Mooney viscosity correlates with weight-average molecular weight via the empirical Mark–Houwink-type relation:

$$ [\eta] = K \cdot M_w^{\alpha} $$

Where:
- $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (related to Mooney)
- $K, \alpha$ = empirical constants for NR in toluene at 25 °C: $K \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$, $\alpha \approx 0.667$
- $M_w$ = weight-average molecular weight

**Step 1 — Estimate Mooney → intrinsic viscosity (approximate empirical):**

$$ \log_{10} M_w = 4.98 + 0.025 \times (\text{Mooney ML}_{1+4}) $$

**Step 2 — Numerical Example:**

For Mooney ML(1+4) at 100 °C = **70**:

$$ \log_{10} M_w = 4.98 + 0.025 \times 70 $$

$$ \log_{10} M_w = 4.98 + 1.75 = 6.73 $$

$$ M_w = 10^{6.73} \approx 5.37 \times 10^{6} \text{ g/mol} $$

This value sits within the expected range of **10⁶–10⁷ g/mol** for solid NR.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection Logic

| Selection Criterion | Recommended Form |
|---|---|
| Tyre manufacturing, bulk use, low cost | **TSR Crumb (SMR/ISNR)** |
| Medical gloves, low allergenicity | **DPNR** |
| Castable / sprayable application | **LNR** |
| High green strength, no cold flow | **SP rubber** |
| Light-colored goods, no smoke odor | **Crepe (PC/EPC)** |
| Direct powder mixing, no mastication | **Powdered NR** |
| Traditional craft / cottage industry | **RSS Sheet** |

### 3.4 Derivation: Protein Removal Efficiency in DPNR

Let $C_0$ = initial protein concentration in field latex (≈ 0.3% w/w in dry rubber).

After enzymatic treatment:

$$ C = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t} $$

Where $k$ = pseudo-first-order rate constant for enzymatic hydrolysis (≈ 0.05 min⁻¹), $t$ = treatment time (min).

**Example:** For $t = 120$ min:

$$ C = 0.3 \cdot e^{-0.05 \times 120} = 0.3 \cdot e^{-6} $$

$$ C = 0.3 \times 0.00248 \approx 0.00074\% \text{ w/w} $$

This confirms that DPNR achieves protein content **below 0.02%**, satisfying medical-grade specifications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Conversion Pathways from Field Latex to Commercial Forms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Latex from Hevea brasiliensis] --> B{Form of processing?}
    A --> C[Centrifugation]
    C --> D[Concentrated Latex 60% DRC]

    B --> E[Acid Coagulation in Tanks]
    E --> F[Rolling into Sheets]
    F --> G[Smoke Drying 4-7 days]
    G --> H[RSS Ribbed Smoked Sheet]

    B --> I[Acid Coagulation plus Sodium Bisulphite]
    I --> J[Roller Milling Crumpled]
    J --> K[Air Drying]
    K --> L[Pale Crepe / EPC]

    B --> M[Controlled Coagulation under Shear]
    M --> N[Granule Formation]
    N --> O[Hot Air Drying 100-120 °C]
    O --> P[TSR Crumb SMR SIR ISNR]

    A --> Q[Enzymatic Treatment]
    Q --> R[Centrifugation plus Washing]
    R --> S[Deproteinized NR DPNR]

    A --> T[Mechanical Mastication]
    T --> U[Oxidative Peptization]
    U --> V[Liquid Natural Rubber LNR]

    A --> W[Spray Drying of Latex]
    W --> X[Powdered Natural Rubber]

    A --> Y[Blend 80 percent Fresh plus 20 percent Prevulcanized Latex]
    Y --> Z[SP Superior Processing Rubber]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of TSR Crumb Production Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Receiving Bay] --> P2[Quality Check Lab]
    P2 --> P3[Bulking Tanks]
    P3 --> P4[Coagulation Vats with Formic Acid]
    P4 --> P5[Maturation Tank]
    P5 --> P6[Granulator / Hammer Mill]
    P6 --> P7[Shredder / Creper]
    P7 --> P8[Deep Bed Dryer 100 to 120 °C]
    P8 --> P9[Cleaner / Magnetic Separator]
    P9 --> P10[Auto Baling Press 33.3 kg Bales]
    P10 --> P11[Wrapping in Polyethylene]
    P11 --> P12[Storage Warehouse]
    P12 --> P13[Quality Assurance Lab PRI Dirt Ash Volatile]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Form Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0{End Application} --> S1[Tyre Tread or Carcass]
    S0 --> S2[Medical or Electrical]
    S0 --> S3[Adhesive or Sealant]
    S0 --> S4[Light Colored Product]
    S0 --> S5[Powder Mixing Operation]

    S1 --> R1[TSR Crumb SMR 10 or SMR 20]
    S2 --> R2[DPNR Grade]
    S3 --> R3[Liquid Natural Rubber LNR]
    S4 --> R4[Pale Crepe PC]
    S5 --> R5[Powdered NR]

    S0 --> S6[High Green Strength Extrusion]
    S6 --> R6[SP Rubber]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Property Modification Map Across Forms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Natural Rubber Base Polymer] --> Q1[Sheet RSS: Adds Smoke Antioxidants]
    Q --> Q2[Crepe: Removes Serum Impurities]
    Q --> Q3[Crumb TSR: Standardizes Properties]
    Q --> Q4[DPNR: Removes Proteins]
    Q --> Q5[LNR: Reduces Molecular Weight]
    Q --> Q6[Powdered: Adds Anti-caking Agent]
    Q --> Q7[SP: Adds Prevulcanized Domains]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "Technically Specified Rubber" (TSR)? List any four guaranteed properties of TSR 20 grade.*

**Model Answer:**
TSR is a form of natural rubber produced in **granulated (crumb) form** under tightly controlled specifications set by international standards (e.g., SMR, SIR, ISNR). The rubber is coagulated under controlled conditions, granulated, hot-air dried, and baled. It replaces traditional sheet/crepe in most bulk industrial applications due to its uniformity.

Four guaranteed properties of TSR 20:
1. **Dirt content** ≤ 0.16% by mass
2. **Ash content** ≤ 1.00% by mass
3. **Volatile matter** ≤ 1.00% by mass
4. **Nitrogen content** ≤ 0.60% by mass
5. **Plasticity Retention Index (PRI)** ≥ 30

(Any four points – ½ mark each; introduction 1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between RSS sheet rubber and Pale Crepe rubber in terms of (a) drying method, (b) color, (c) antioxidant content.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | RSS Sheet | Pale Crepe |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Drying | **Smoke drying** in smokehouse at 40–50 °C for 4–7 days | **Air drying** at ambient temperature |
| (b) Color | **Brown** (due to smoke deposition) | **Pale yellow to white** |
| (c) Antioxidant | **High** (phenols from wood smoke absorbed) | **Low** (not exposed to smoke, hence more prone to oxidation) |

(Each correct comparison = 1 mark; total 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

> **Choose either Question A or Question B.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Describe with a neat flow diagram the **manufacture of Ribbed Smoked Sheet (RSS) rubber**. Highlight the role of smoking. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Latex Preparation (1 mark):**
Field latex is collected into cups, **bulked in tanks**, diluted to ~12–15% DRC, and **filtered** to remove dirt, leaves, bark particles.

**Step 2 — Coagulation (1 mark):**
The filtered latex is run into long **aluminum coagulating tanks** containing dilute **acetic acid or formic acid** (≈ 0.5–1.0% v/v). Coagulation occurs in 1–4 hours.

**Step 3 — Sheet Formation (1.5 marks):**
The coagulum is passed through a series of **smooth and grooved rollers** to compress it into thin sheets of about **3 mm thickness**. The grooved rollers impart the characteristic **ribbed pattern** on one surface.

**Step 4 — Smoking (2.5 marks):**
Sheets are hung on wooden poles inside **smokehouses** for **4 to 7 days** at **40–50 °C**. Smoke from **slow-burning wood** (without resin) is circulated.
*Role of smoking:*
- Imparts **natural phenolic antioxidants** (e.g., guaiacol, cresols) that protect NR from bacterial and oxidative degradation
- Reduces moisture content to safe storage level (~**1%**)
- Gives the characteristic **brown color**

**Step 5 — Grading and Baling (1 mark):**
Sheets are visually graded into RSS1, RSS2, RSS3, RSS4, RSS5 based on color, transparency, and freedom from blemishes, then baled for export.

**(b)** Explain the **manufacture of TSR (Crumb) rubber**. Compare its advantages over sheet rubber. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Manufacture of TSR Crumb (5 marks):**
1. Field latex → **bulked** and **filtered** → **standardized DRC** (~12–20%)
2. Coagulated using **formic acid** under controlled pH (≈ 4.5–5.0)
3. Coagulum **matured** for 1–16 hours → undergoes controlled bacterial/chemical action to reduce non-rubber content
4. Passed through **granulators / hammer mills** → crumb particles (≈ 1–10 mm)
5. **Washed** to remove residual serum
6. **Deep-bed hot air drying** at 100–120 °C for 4–8 hours → reduces moisture to < 1%
7. **Cleaned** (magnetic separation, sieving)
8. **Baled** in 33.3 kg or 35 kg polythene-wrapped bales
9. Tested for **dirt, ash, N₂, VM, PRI, Mooney** before release

**Advantages over Sheet Rubber (2 marks):**
- Standardized guaranteed specifications → **batch-to-batch uniformity**
- Lower cost due to mechanization
- Easier storage, transport, and dosing (granules flow automatically)
- Better hygiene and less manual labour
- PRI guarantee ensures predictable ageing

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** What is **DPNR (Deproteinized Natural Rubber)**? Describe the **enzymatic method** of its preparation and list its applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
DPNR is a form of natural rubber in which the **nitrogenous proteins** (responsible for allergic reactions and moisture sensitivity) are reduced from the natural level of ~0.3% to **less than 0.02% w/w**.

**Enzymatic Method of Preparation (4.5 marks):**

1. **Latex collection:** Fresh field latex is collected and **stabilized** with ammonia and surfactant (to prevent premature coagulation).
2. **Enzyme addition:** **Alcalase** (or other alkaline proteases) is added at a concentration of ~0.1–0.5% on total protein basis. The latex is incubated at **30–40 °C for 1–2 hours** with gentle stirring.
3. **Reaction:** Proteolytic enzymes **hydrolyze peptide bonds**, breaking proteins into water-soluble peptides and amino acids.
4. **Centrifugation / Creaming:** The treated latex is **centrifuged** (similar to latex concentration) to separate the cream fraction (rich in rubber). The protein hydrolysates remain in the aqueous serum and are removed.
5. **Washing:** The cream is **repeatedly diluted and re-concentrated** to further reduce residual protein.
6. **Coagulation and Drying:** Final DPNR is coagulated, granulated, and hot-air dried.

**Applications (1.5 marks):**
- **Medical gloves, catheters, condoms** (low allergenicity)
- **Electrical insulation** (low water absorption, stable dielectric properties)
- **Engineering vibration dampers** (low hysteresis, low heat build-up)
- **Pharmaceutical and food-contact articles**

**(b)** Define **Liquid Natural Rubber (LNR)**. Explain the **oxidative depolymerization method** for its production and list two major applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
LNR is a **low-molecular-weight natural rubber** with $M_w$ in the range **10³–10⁵ g/mol**, existing as a **viscous liquid** at room temperature, in contrast to solid NR ($M_w$ ≈ 10⁶–10⁷ g/mol).

**Oxidative Depolymerization Method (4.5 marks):**

1. **NR dissolution:** Solid NR (usually pale crepe) is dissolved in an aromatic solvent (e.g., toluene or xylene) at 5–10% concentration.
2. **Initiator addition:** A free-radical initiator such as **benzoyl peroxide** OR **phenylhydrazine + air** is added.
3. **Controlled oxidation:** Air or oxygen is bubbled through the solution at **60–80 °C** for several hours. Chain scission occurs preferentially at the **trans-1,4 isoprene double bonds**, generating shorter chains with **carbonyl and hydroxyl end groups**.
4. **Termination:** Reaction stopped by cooling and adding antioxidant.
5. **Solvent removal:** LNR is recovered by **steam stripping or vacuum distillation**.
6. **Characterization:** $M_w$ is confirmed by GPC; viscosity typically **1–1000 Pa·s** depending on grade.

**Two Major Applications (1.5 marks):**
1. **Adhesives and pressure-sensitive tapes** (good wetting and tack)
2. **Castable elastomers and sealants** (no mastication needed, pours into moulds)
3. (Bonus) **Bitumen/rubber blend modifier** for road construction

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Frequently deducted marks):**
> 1. Students confuse **smoke drying (RSS)** with **air drying (Crepe)** — a 1-mark deduction every time.
> 2. Many write **SMR 5** specifications but misquote the **Nitrogen** value (correct: ≤ 0.60%, not 0.06%).
> 3. For DPNR, students forget to mention **centrifugation** as the separation step — examiners expect the process chain, not just enzyme addition.
> 4. For LNR, do NOT write that it is made by "dissolving solid rubber" alone — mention **chain scission / depolymerization** to earn full marks.
> 5. In TSR questions, students forget **PRI ≥ 30**; it is a guaranteed property and frequently asked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Natural rubber comes in 7 main commercial forms**: Sheet (RSS), Crepe, Crumb (TSR), SP, DPNR, LNR, Powdered.
- **RSS** is **smoke-dried** (gets phenolic antioxidants from wood smoke); **Crepe** is **air-dried** (pale but low antioxidants).
- **TSR / Crumb rubber** is the **most industrially important** form today, governed by guaranteed specifications: dirt, ash, VM, N₂, PRI.
- **PRI** is calculated as $(P_{30}/P_0) \times 100$ and must be **≥ 30** for SMR 20.
- **SP rubber** = 20% prevulcanized latex + 80% fresh latex coagulated together → improves green strength.
- **DPNR** has protein content **< 0.02%** (vs ~0.3% in normal NR) → used in **medical/electrical** applications.
- **LNR** has $M_w$ in range **10³–10⁵** g/mol; produced by mastication OR oxidative peptization; used in **adhesives**.
- **Powdered NR** eliminates mastication, saves **30–40% mixing energy**, requires anti-caking agents.
- **Mooney viscosity (ML 1+4 at 100 °C)** of solid NR ≈ **60–90**.
- Always remember to draw **neat labelled flow diagrams** in 14-mark questions — examiners award **1–2 marks** specifically for the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulcanisation: Chemical and Physical Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT501 - RUBBERS-SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vulcanisation: Chemical and Physical Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus-Standard Definition (KTU PCPOT501, Module 2)**
> **Vulcanisation** is a **chemical process of cross-linking** the long, coiled, plastic polymer chains of raw (unvulcanised) natural or synthetic rubber with adjacent polymer chains by means of **sulphur, peroxides, metal oxides, or other curatives**, thereby converting the soft, tacky, and thermoplastic raw elastomer into a strong, elastic, thermoset material with vastly improved mechanical strength, elasticity, thermal stability, and solvent resistance.

The process is essentially the formation of a **three-dimensional network** of covalent crosslinks (primarily **polysulphidic bridges — C—Sₓ—C**, where $x$ typically ranges from **1 to 8**) between the $\alpha$-methylene carbons of the polyisoprene (or other elastomer) backbone.

> [!NOTE] **Discovery Context (Historical Anchor)**
> Vulcanisation was accidentally discovered by **Charles Goodyear in 1839** when he dropped a rubber–sulphur mixture onto a hot stove. The invention was later patented by **Thomas Hancock in 1843**. The term honours the Roman god *Vulcan* (god of fire and forge).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti noodles lying loose in a plate — each noodle slides freely past the next; the system is soft, sticky, and has no shape memory. This is **raw rubber**.

Now imagine taking a thin thread and tying every noodle to its **four nearest neighbours** at random points along their length, forming a **3-D fishing-net of noodles**. The mass is now:
- No longer sticky,
- Springs back when stretched (elastic),
- Retains shape after deformation,
- Cannot be melted and re-shaped easily.

That "tying thread" is the **crosslink**, and the action of creating it is **vulcanisation**. The "fishing-net" is the **elastomeric network**.

> [!NOTE] **Crosslink Analogy in One Line**
> Raw rubber = bowl of loose noodles → Vulcanised rubber = a **noodle fishing-net** tied together with chemical bridges.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Sulphur loading (conventional) | **1.5 – 3.0 phr** | Efficient vulcanisation (EV) systems |
| Sulphur loading (traditional) | **2.5 – 3.5 phr** | Conventional (CV) systems |
| Accelerator (MBT/MBS) | **0.5 – 1.5 phr** | Rate controller |
| Zinc oxide (activator) | **3 – 5 phr** | Activator |
| Stearic acid | **1 – 2 phr** | Activator / dispersant |
| Vulcanisation temperature | **140 – 160 °C** | Press cure range |
| Vulcanisation pressure | **10 – 20 MPa (press)** | Moulding pressure |
| Optimum cure time ($t_{90}$) | **5 – 30 minutes** | Time to 90% cure |
| Scorch time ($t_{s1}$ or $t_2$) | **1 – 5 minutes** | Safety margin |
| Crosslink density ($\nu$) | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵ mol/cm³** | Network density |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Mnemonic: "ZAS"**
> The activator system is remembered as **Z**inc + **A**cid (stearic) = **ZAS** — together they form the active zinc complex that enables accelerator chemistry.

## 1.4 The Need for Vulcanisation — Why It Cannot Be Skipped

Raw natural rubber exhibits:
1. **Cold flow** (creep under own weight) — thermodynamically unstable.
2. **Tackiness** — sticks to itself and processing equipment.
3. **Poor tensile strength** (~**0.3 MPa** unvulcanised vs **20–30 MPa** vulcanised).
4. **High temperature sensitivity** — softens above 60 °C.
5. **Solubility** in many organic solvents.
6. **Poor abrasion resistance** and **low elastic recovery**.

Vulcanisation fixes **all six problems** in a single chemical step, making rubber the **engineering material of choice** for tyres, seals, dampers, and medical devices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Aspects of Vulcanisation

### 2.1.1 Sulphur Vulcanisation — The Dominant Industrial System

The reaction occurs between **allylic hydrogens** of the rubber chain and **elemental sulphur (S₈)** in the presence of accelerators and activators.

**General stoichiometry (idealised):**

$$2 \text{—CH}_2\text{—C(CH}_3\text{)=CH—CH}_2\text{—} + \text{S}_x \longrightarrow \text{—CH}_2\text{—C(CH}_3\text{)=CH—CH}_2\text{—S}_x\text{—CH}_2\text{—CH=C(CH}_3)\text{—CH}_2\text{—} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$$

Where $x$ = **number of sulphur atoms in the crosslink bridge** (typically 1–8).

### 2.1.2 Mechanism of Sulphur Vulcanisation (Multi-Stage)

**Stage 1 — Activation of Sulphur**
The accelerator (e.g., **2-mercaptobenzothiazole, MBT**) reacts with ZnO and stearic acid to form a **zinc-accelerator complex** (e.g., zinc-MBT):

$$\text{ZnO} + 2 \text{ StH (Stearic acid)} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(St)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{Zn(St)}_2 + \text{MBT} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(MBT)}_2 + \text{StH}$$

This zinc complex cleaves the S₈ ring to form a **sulphurating agent** — an active linear polysulphide (Zn—Sₓ—MBT).

**Stage 2 — Reaction with Rubber (Allylic Substitution)**

The active sulphurating species abstracts the **allylic hydrogen** of the polyisoprene chain, producing a **rubber-bound polysulphidic intermediate**:

$$\text{RH} + \text{ZnS}_x\text{MBT} \longrightarrow \text{R}\text{—S}_x\text{—MBT} + \text{ZnH(MBT)}$$

**Stage 3 — Crosslink Formation**

Two rubber-bound intermediates combine to yield the final **C—Sₓ—C crosslink**:

$$\text{R}\text{—S}_x\text{—MBT} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{R}\text{—S}_x\text{—R} + \text{HMBT}$$

The crosslink length ($x$) depends on the **accelerator-to-sulphur ratio (A/S ratio)**:

| A/S Ratio | System Type | Crosslink Type | Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0.1 – 0.6** | Conventional (CV) | Polysulphidic (Sₓ, $x$ = 3–8) | Good mechanical, poor ageing |
| **0.7 – 2.5** | Semi-EV (SEV) | Mixed (mono- + polysulphidic) | Balanced |
| **2.5 – 12** | Efficient (EV) | Predominantly monosulphidic | Excellent ageing |

### 2.1.3 Accelerators — Classification

> [!NOTE] **Why accelerators?**
> Without accelerators, sulphur vulcanisation at 140 °C would take **5–6 hours**. With accelerators, cure time drops to **5–20 minutes** — a 30-fold productivity gain.

| Class | Example | Relative Speed |
|---|---|---|
| **Guanidines** | DPG (Diphenyl guanidine) | Slow |
| **Thiazoles** | MBT, MBTS | Medium-fast |
| **Sulphenamides** | CBS, MBS, TBBS | Delayed-action (best scorch safety) |
| **Thiurams** | TMTD, TMTM | Very fast (often used as boosters) |
| **Dithiocarbamates** | ZDBC, ZDEC | Ultra-fast (room-temp cure with S) |

### 2.1.4 Non-Sulphur Vulcanisation Systems

**1. Peroxide Vulcanisation (most important non-sulphur system)**

Peroxides (e.g., **dicumyl peroxide, DCP**) generate **free radicals** that abstract allylic hydrogens and crosslink:

$$\text{RO—OR} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet}$$

$$2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet} + 2\,\text{RH} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{R}^{\bullet} + 2\,\text{ROH}$$

$$2\,\text{R}^{\bullet} \longrightarrow \text{R—R} \text{ (C—C crosslink)}$$

C—C crosslinks are **short, stable, and ageing-resistant** — ideal for **silicone, EPDM, HNBR**.

**2. Metal Oxide Vulcanisation**
Used for **chloroprene rubber (CR/Neoprene)**. ZnO + MgO crosslink via **allylic chlorine displacement**.

**3. Phenolic Resin Vulcanisation**
Used for **butyl rubber (IIR)**. Resin (e.g., PF resin) crosslinks via **Friedel–Crafts alkylation**.

## 2.2 Physical Aspects of Vulcanisation

### 2.2.1 Effect on Properties

| Property | Before Vulcanisation | After Vulcanisation | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | 0.3 MPa | 20–30 MPa | ↑ 100× |
| Elongation at break | 800–1000% | 400–700% | ↓ (optimum) |
| Hardness (Shore A) | ~ 10 | 30–90 | ↑ 3–9× |
| Elastic recovery | Poor | Excellent (>90%) | ↑ |
| Solubility | Soluble in toluene | Swells only | ↓ |
| Glass transition ($T_g$) | ~ −72 °C | ~ −72 °C | Unchanged |
| Heat resistance | Melts/flows | Thermoset | ✓ |
| Elastic modulus ($E$) | 0.5–1 MPa | 2–10 MPa | ↑ 5–20× |

### 2.2.2 Stages of Vulcanisation (Rheometer Curve)

The cure curve (from an **oscillating disc rheometer, ODR**) shows three regions:

> [!NOTE] **Mnemonic: "S-C-R" (Scorch – Cure – Reversion)**
> 1. **Scorch / Induction period** — delayed action, processing safety.
> 2. **Curing / Crosslinking period** — rapid modulus rise, network forms.
> 3. **Reversion / Maturity / Plateau** — overcure, depending on system.

**Key rheometer parameters:**
- $M_L$ = minimum torque (lowest viscosity before cure)
- $M_H$ = maximum torque (fully cured)
- $t_{s1}$ or $t_2$ = **scorch time** (1 or 2 units above $M_L$)
- $t_{90}$ = **optimum cure time** (90% of $M_H$ achieved)
- $t_{95}$ = **technical cure time** (95% of $M_H$)
- $\Delta M = M_H - M_L$ ∝ **crosslink density**
- **CRI (Cure Rate Index)** $= \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s1}}$ (in min⁻¹)

### 2.2.3 Crosslink Density & Network Theory (Flory–Rehner)

The **crosslink density ($\nu$)** is the concentration of effective network chains per unit volume, measured in **mol/m³** (or **mol/cm³**).

**Swelling-based determination (Flory–Rehner Equation):**

$$\nu = -\frac{\ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2}{2 V_s (v_r^{1/3} - \frac{v_r}{2})}$$

Where:
- $v_r$ = **volume fraction of rubber in swollen gel**
- $V_s$ = **molar volume of swelling solvent** (e.g., toluene = 106.3 cm³/mol)
- $\chi$ = **Flory–Huggins polymer–solvent interaction parameter** (for NR-toluene, $\chi \approx 0.39$)

**Shear modulus (rubber elasticity theory):**

$$G = \nu R T$$

Where $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** and $T$ = absolute temperature (K). Therefore:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu_{\text{Poisson}}) \approx 3G \quad \text{(for incompressible rubber)}$$

**Average molecular weight between crosslinks:**

$$M_c = \frac{\rho}{\nu}$$

Where $\rho$ = density of rubber (~**0.92 g/cm³** for NR).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbols | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = \nu R T$ | $G$ = shear modulus, $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ = K | Network property calculation |
| 2 | $E \approx 3G$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $G$ = shear modulus | For incompressible rubber |
| 3 | $M_c = \rho / \nu$ | $\rho$ = density, $\nu$ = crosslink density | Find $M_c$ from $\nu$ |
| 4 | $\nu = \rho / (2 M_c)$ (per-network chain) | — | Equivalent form |
| 5 | $\text{CRI} = \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s1}}$ | Cure rate index, min⁻¹ | Compare vulcanisation rates |
| 6 | Flory–Rehner (swelling) | See above | Find $\nu$ from swelling |
| 7 | $\Delta M \propto \nu$ | Rheometer torque rise | Estimate crosslink density |
| 8 | $\text{A/S ratio} = \dfrac{\text{phr accelerator}}{\text{phr sulphur}}$ | — | Classify CV/EV system |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility**
> The $G = \nu R T$ relation is the **master equation of rubber elasticity** — every tyre, O-ring, bridge bearing, and conveyor belt is designed using it. Crosslink density controls **hardness, modulus, rebound resilience, and fatigue life** simultaneously, making it the single most important design parameter in rubber engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crosslink Density from Modulus (Rubber Elasticity)

**Starting Point:** Affine network model of an ideal rubber (Flory, 1942; Treloar, 1975).

For a phantom network of $N$ chains in a volume $V$, the entropy change on deformation is:

$$\Delta S = -\frac{1}{2} N k ( \lambda_1^2 + \lambda_2^2 + \lambda_3^2 - 3)$$

Where $\lambda_i$ are the principal stretch ratios and $k$ is Boltzmann's constant.

For a **simple shear deformation** of magnitude $\gamma$ in an incompressible solid ($\lambda_1 = \lambda$, $\lambda_2 = 1/\lambda$, $\lambda_3 = 1$, with $\lambda = 1 + \gamma$):

The shear stress is $\tau = \dfrac{\partial A}{\partial \gamma}$ where $A = U - TS$ is the Helmholtz free energy.

Computing $\partial A / \partial \gamma$ at $\gamma = 0$ (small strain limit) yields the **shear modulus**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \nu R T \,\gamma \\
\Rightarrow G &= \nu R T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion to Young's Modulus ($E$)** using the Poisson's ratio $\nu_p$ (use $\nu_p$ to avoid confusion with crosslink density):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 2G(1 + \nu_p) \\
\nu_p &= 0.5 \quad \text{(incompressible rubber)} \\
\Rightarrow E &= 2G(1 + 0.5) = 2G \cdot 1.5 = 3G
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the **final compact forms:**

$$
G = \nu R T \qquad\qquad E = 3\nu R T
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Flory–Rehner Equation (Crosslink Density by Swelling)

**Starting Point:** Total Gibbs free energy change on swelling = elastic (network) + mixing (polymer-solvent):

$$
\Delta G_{\text{swell}} = \Delta G_{\text{elastic}} + \Delta G_{\text{mixing}}
$$

**Step 1 — Free energy of mixing (Flory–Huggins):**

$$
\Delta G_{\text{mix}} = RT \left[ n_1 \ln v_1 + n_2 \ln v_2 + \chi \, n_1 v_2 \right]
$$

For the **polymer** (crosslinked rubber) as a single network chain ($n_2 = 1$ effectively), using volume fraction $v_r$ of rubber and $1 - v_r$ of solvent:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{mix,per vol}} = RT \left[ \frac{(1 - v_r)}{V_s} \ln(1 - v_r) + \frac{v_r}{V_r}\ln v_r + \frac{\chi (1 - v_r)}{V_s} v_r \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Elastic free energy (Flory):**

For an affine network:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{el}} = \frac{3 \nu R T}{2} \left( v_r^{2/3} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Total free energy and equilibrium condition:**

At equilibrium, $\dfrac{\partial \Delta G_{\text{swell}}}{\partial v_r} = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial \Delta G_{\text{mix}}}{\partial v_r} + \frac{\partial \Delta G_{\text{el}}}{\partial v_r} = 0
$$

Computing the derivatives explicitly and rearranging (substituting $-\ln(1-v_r) - v_r$ for the small-terms in the Flory–Huggins expression):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{\partial}{\partial v_r} \left[ RT \frac{V_s}{V_r} v_r^2 \cdot \chi + \dots \right] = \frac{\partial}{\partial v_r}\left[\frac{3 \nu R T}{2}(v_r^{2/3} - 1)\right] \\
&\Rightarrow -\left[ \ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2 \right] = V_s \nu (v_r^{1/3} - v_r / 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $\nu$:

$$
\boxed{\;\nu = -\,\frac{\ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi\, v_r^{\,2}}{2 V_s \left( v_r^{\,1/3} - \dfrac{v_r}{2} \right)}\;}
$$

This is the **Flory–Rehner equation (1943)** — the most commonly used equation in KTU numericals to find crosslink density from swelling data.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cure Rate Index (CRI)

**Starting Point:** Definition of CRI as a measure of the slope of the cure curve between scorch and optimum cure.

$$
\text{CRI} = \frac{\text{Normative modulus rise}}{\text{Time to achieve it}}
$$

The standard KTU convention uses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= t_{90} - t_{s2} \\
\text{CRI} &= \frac{100}{\Delta t} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Units: $\text{min}^{-1}$ (or $\%\text{ per min}$).

Higher CRI = **faster curing system**; lower CRI = **safer, slower curing** (better scorch safety).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE] **Problem:** A natural rubber vulcanisate has crosslink density $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³. Density $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³. Calculate (a) shear modulus $G$ at 25 °C, (b) Young's modulus $E$, (c) average molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$.

**Given:**
- $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³ $= 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6$ mol/m³ $= 150$ mol/m³
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³ $= 920$ kg/m³

### (a) Shear Modulus $G$

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \nu R T \\
&= 150 \, \text{mol/m}^3 \times 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} \times 298 \, \text{K} \\
G &= 150 \times 2477.572 \\
G &\approx 3.716 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} \\
G &\approx 0.372 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Young's Modulus $E$ (Incompressible Rubber)

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3G \\
&= 3 \times 0.372 \, \text{MPa} \\
E &= 1.116 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) Molecular Weight Between Crosslinks $M_c$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_c &= \frac{\rho}{\nu} \\
&= \frac{920 \, \text{kg/m}^3}{150 \, \text{mol/m}^3} \\
M_c &= 6.133 \, \text{kg/mol} = 6133 \, \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $G \approx 0.372$ MPa, $E \approx 1.116$ MPa, $M_c \approx 6133$ g/mol.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Vulcanisation Systems

| Parameter | Conventional (CV) | Semi-EV (SEV) | Efficient (EV) | Peroxide (DCP) | Metal Oxide (CR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sulphur (phr) | 2.5–3.5 | 1.5–2.0 | 0.3–0.8 | 0 | 0 |
| Accelerator (phr) | 0.3–0.5 | 0.7–1.0 | 2.5–6.0 | 0 | 0 (ZnO used) |
| Crosslink type | Polysulphidic | Mixed | Mono + Di | C–C | Ether / thioether |
| Crosslink length | 3–8 S | 1–4 S | 1–2 S | 1 atom | 1–2 atoms |
| Ageing resistance | Poor | Moderate | Excellent | Best | Good |
| Mechanical strength | Best | Very good | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Fatigue resistance | Best | Good | Lower | Lower | Moderate |
| Heat resistance | Low | Moderate | High | Very high | High |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Higher | High | Medium |
| Typical rubber | NR, SBR | NR, SBR | NR, EPDM, IIR | Silicone, EPDM, HNBR | CR (Neoprene) |
