# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Overall Vulcanisation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Rubber NR or Synthetic Elastomer] --> B[Compounding Stage]
    B --> B1[Add Vulcanising Agent Sulphur or Peroxide]
    B --> B2[Add Activators ZnO plus Stearic Acid]
    B --> B3[Add Accelerators MBT or CBS]
    B --> B4[Add Fillers Carbon Black or Silica]
    B --> B5[Add Antidegradants Antioxidants and Antiozonants]
    B --> C[Compounded Rubber Compound]
    C --> D{Process Selection}
    D --> D1[Compression Moulding]
    D --> D2[Injection Moulding]
    D --> D3[Extrusion plus Curing]
    D --> D4[Autoclave Curing]
    D --> E[Heat and Pressure Application 140 to 160 C]
    E --> F[Crosslink Formation C Sx C bridges]
    F --> G[Vulcanised Rubber Product]
    G --> H[Testing Tensile Hardness Ageing]
    H --> I[Final Product Tyre Seal Hose]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Chemical Mechanism of Sulphur Vulcanisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Stage 1 Activation of Sulphur] --> R1A[Accelerator MBT reacts with Zn St2]
    R1A --> R1B[Forms Active Sulphurating Agent ZnSx MBT]
    R1B --> R2[Stage 2 Allylic Substitution]
    R2 --> R2A[Abstracts Allylic H from Polyisoprene Chain]
    R2A --> R2B[Forms Rubber Bound Polysulphide Intermediate R Sx MBT]
    R2B --> R3[Stage 3 Crosslink Formation]
    R3 --> R3A[Second Rubber Bound Intermediate Attacks]
    R3A --> R3B[Final C Sx C Crosslink Bridge Formed]
    R3B --> R4[Stage 4 Network Maturation]
    R4 --> R4A[Polysulphide Shortening to Di and Monosulphidic]
    R4A --> R5[Final Three Dimensional Network]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Vulcanisation Curve (Rheometer Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start of Cure Heat Applied] --> S[Scorch Region]
    S --> S1[Induction Period Delayed Action]
    S1 --> S2[No measurable crosslink yet]
    S2 --> C[Crosslinking Region]
    C --> C1[Rapid Modulus Rise]
    C1 --> C2[Network Density Increases]
    C2 --> C3[t90 reached 90 percent of MH]
    C3 --> M[Maturity Plateau]
    M --> M1[Optimum Physical Properties]
    M1 --> R{Reversion Behavior}
    R --> R1[Sulphur System: Modulus drops]
    R1 --> R2[Peroxide System: Plateau stable]
    R2 --> O[Overcure Region]
    O --> O1[Network Degradation Competes with Formation]
    O1 --> O2[Final Product Properties]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Crosslink Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ConventionalCV[Conventional CV Crosslink Architecture]
        CV1[Rubber Chain] --> CV2[Polysulphide Bridge Sx x equals 3 to 8]
        CV2 --> CV3[Rubber Chain]
        CV3 --> CV4[Polysulphide Bridge]
        CV4 --> CV5[Rubber Chain]
    end
    subgraph EfficientEV[Efficient EV Crosslink Architecture]
        EV1[Rubber Chain] --> EV2[Monosulphide Bridge S1]
        EV2 --> EV3[Rubber Chain]
        EV3 --> EV4[Disulphide Bridge S2]
        EV4 --> EV5[Rubber Chain]
    end
    subgraph PeroxideSystem[Peroxide Crosslink Architecture]
        PE1[Rubber Chain] --> PE2[Direct C C Bond]
        PE2 --> PE3[Rubber Chain]
        PE3 --> PE4[Direct C C Bond]
        PE4 --> PE5[Rubber Chain]
    end
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Vulcanisation Inputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[INPUT BLOCK] --> A[Raw Elastomer]
    INPUT --> B[Curative System]
    INPUT --> C[Additives]
    A --> A1[Natural or Synthetic Polymer]
    B --> B1[Sulphur or Peroxide]
    B --> B2[Accelerator Class]
    B --> B3[Activator ZAS]
    C --> C1[Fillers]
    C --> C2[Plasticizers]
    C --> C3[Antidegradants]
    A1 --> MIX[Mixing and Mastication]
    B1 --> MIX
    B2 --> MIX
    B3 --> MIX
    C1 --> MIX
    C2 --> MIX
    C3 --> MIX
    MIX --> STORE[Storage and Scorch Safety Window]
    STORE --> SHAPE[Shaping Moulding Extrusion Calendering]
    SHAPE --> CURE[Curing Block Heat plus Time plus Pressure]
    CURE --> NET[Three Dimensional Network Formation]
    NET --> QC[Quality Control Block]
    QC --> QC1[Modulus and Hardness Test]
    QC --> QC2[Ageing and Fatigue Test]
    QC --> QC3[Swelling and Crosslink Density]
    QC1 --> OUT[Output Vulcanised Rubber Article]
    QC2 --> OUT
    QC3 --> OUT
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define vulcanisation. List **four** property improvements obtained after vulcanisation of natural rubber.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Vulcanisation is a chemical process in which the long polymer chains of raw rubber are cross-linked to form a three-dimensional network structure, typically using sulphur, peroxides, or metal oxides as curatives, in the presence of accelerators and activators under heat and pressure.

**Four property improvements (0.375 mark each = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Tensile strength** increases from ~0.3 MPa to 20–30 MPa.
2. **Elastic recovery** improves (>90% rebound).
3. **Hardness** increases; cold flow and tackiness eliminated.
4. **Solvent resistance** improved — rubber swells but does not dissolve; thermal stability and ageing resistance also enhanced.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **conventional (CV)** and **efficient vulcanisation (EV)** systems with respect to sulphur level, accelerator-to-sulphur ratio, and crosslink type.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Conventional (CV) | Efficient (EV) |
|---|---|---|
| Sulphur level | High (2.5–3.5 phr) | Low (0.3–0.8 phr) |
| Accelerator-to-sulphur (A/S) ratio | Low (0.1–0.6) | High (2.5–12) |
| Crosslink type | Polysulphidic (C—Sₓ—C, $x$ = 3–8) | Predominantly monosulphidic (C—S—C) |
| Ageing resistance | Poor | Excellent |
| Typical use | General mechanical goods | Heat-resistant articles, cables |

(1 mark for each correct row of differentiation; bonus 0.5 mark for stating the consequence on ageing.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **chemistry of sulphur vulcanisation** of natural rubber with the role of **activator system (ZnO + stearic acid)** and **accelerator (MBT/CBS)**. Discuss the formation of **polysulphidic crosslinks**. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A natural rubber vulcanisate has crosslink density $\nu = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³. Density $\rho = 0.93$ g/cm³. Calculate (i) shear modulus $G$ at 30 °C, (ii) Young's modulus $E$, (iii) average molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chemistry of Sulphur Vulcanisation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Role of Activator System (2 marks):**
ZnO and stearic acid (StH) first react to form **zinc stearate**, the active zinc donor:

$$\text{ZnO} + 2\,\text{StH} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(St)}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 2 — Role of Accelerator (2 marks):**
Zinc stearate reacts with the accelerator (e.g., MBT = 2-mercaptobenzothiazole) to form a **zinc-accelerator complex** (zinc-MBT):

$$\text{Zn(St)}_2 + 2\,\text{MBT} \longrightarrow \text{Zn(MBT)}_2 + 2\,\text{StH}$$

This complex **cleaves the S₈ ring** to produce an **active sulphurating agent** — a linear polysulphide (Zn—Sₓ—MBT) — that is far more reactive than elemental S₈ alone.

**Step 3 — Crosslink Formation (2 marks):**
The active species attacks the **allylic C–H bond** of polyisoprene, producing a rubber-bound polysulphide intermediate. Two such intermediates then react to form the final **polysulphidic crosslink C—Sₓ—C** ($x$ = 1 to 8, predominantly 3–4 in CV systems). The H₂S evolved is captured by ZnO to form ZnS, preventing reversion.

**Step 4 — Polysulphidic Crosslink Character (1 mark):**
Polysulphidic crosslinks are long, flexible, and rearrangeable — they provide **excellent mechanical strength, high elongation, and good fatigue resistance** but are thermally labile, leading to **poor ageing resistance**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\nu = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³ $= 120$ mol/m³; $T = 303$ K; $\rho = 930$ kg/m³; $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**(i) Shear modulus $G$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \nu R T \\
&= 120 \times 8.314 \times 303 \\
&= 3.023 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa} \\
G &\approx 0.302 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Young's modulus $E$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 3G = 3 \times 0.302 \\
E &\approx 0.907 \, \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_c &= \frac{\rho}{\nu} = \frac{930}{120} \\
M_c &= 7.75 \, \text{kg/mol} = 7750 \, \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $G = 0.302$ MPa, $E = 0.907$ MPa, $M_c = 7750$ g/mol.

---

> **Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**  
> **(a)** Discuss the **physical aspects of vulcanisation** with reference to the **rheometer cure curve**. Define $M_L$, $M_H$, $t_{s1}$, $t_{90}$, and **cure rate index (CRI)**. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Compare **sulphur vulcanisation** and **peroxide vulcanisation** in terms of mechanism, crosslink type, ageing resistance, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical Aspects — Rheometer Cure Curve (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
A small disc of uncured compound is placed in an **oscillating disc rheometer (ODR)** maintained at cure temperature (e.g., 150 °C). The torque required to oscillate the disc is recorded vs. time.

**Step 2 — Regions of the Cure Curve (3 marks):**
- **Scorch / Induction period** — flat low-torque region. No crosslink formation yet; provides processing safety.
- **Cure region** — rapid torque rise. Crosslink network forms; modulus increases sharply.
- **Maturity / Plateau** — torque levels off at $M_H$. Optimum cure reached.
- **Reversion** (in CV systems) — torque falls as polysulphidic crosslinks break.

**Step 3 — Definitions (2 marks):**
- $M_L$ = **minimum torque** (lowest viscosity before cure begins).
- $M_H$ = **maximum (or plateau) torque** (fully cured network).
- $t_{s1}$ = **scorch time** (time to torque = $M_L + 1$ unit).
- $t_{90}$ = **optimum cure time** (time to 90% of $M_H$, i.e., $M_L + 0.9(M_H - M_L)$).
- **CRI** $= \dfrac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s1}}$ — a measure of cure rate (min⁻¹).

**Step 4 — Use (1 mark):**
$\Delta M = M_H - M_L$ is **proportional to crosslink density**. Higher $M_H$ = stiffer vulcanisate. CRI determines production cycle time.

**(b) Sulphur vs. Peroxide Vulcanisation (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Sulphur Vulcanisation | Peroxide Vulcanisation |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Ionic / polar — accelerator–Zn complex attacks allylic C–H; forms C—Sₓ—C | Free-radical — peroxide homolyses to RO•; abstracts H; forms R•; R• + R• → C—C |
| **Crosslink type** | Polysulphidic (CV) to monosulphidic (EV) | Direct **carbon–carbon (C—C)** |
| **Crosslink length** | 1–8 S atoms | 1 atom (C) |
| **Ageing resistance** | Moderate to poor (CV); good (EV) | **Best** — C–C is thermally stable |
| **Heat resistance** | Limited | Excellent |
| **Mechanical strength** | Very high (especially fatigue) | Moderate |
| **Compression set** | Good | Moderate |
| **Typical applications** | Tyres, general rubber goods | **Silicone rubber, EPDM, HNBR**, food-grade and medical products |
| **Cost** | Low | Higher (DCP cost) |
| **Activator required?** | Yes (ZnO + StH) | **No** — peroxides self-activate |

(Each correct comparison row: 1 mark; mechanism details: 2 marks total.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Vulcanisation Questions**
> 1. **Unit confusion in $G = \nu R T$:** Convert $\nu$ from mol/cm³ to mol/m³ (**multiply by 10⁶**). A common error yields $G$ values off by **6 orders of magnitude**. Always check units.
> 2. **Mixing up $t_{s1}$ and $t_{s2}$:** $t_{s1}$ is **scorch time to 1 unit above $M_L$**; $t_{s2}$ is to 2 units. Different textbooks use different conventions — KTU uses $t_{s1}$ in the CRI formula most often.
> 3. **Forgetting $E = 3G$** is valid **only for incompressible rubber** (Poisson's ratio = 0.5). For foamed or porous rubbers, use $E = 2G(1 + \nu_p)$ with $\nu_p < 0.5$.
> 4. **Confusing "A/S ratio" direction:** High A/S = **EV system** (low S, high accelerator), **not** the other way round.
> 5. **Polysulphidic ≠ EV:** Polysulphidic crosslinks dominate in **low A/S** (CV) systems. Students frequently write the opposite.
> 6. **Skipping the ZnO role:** Even in peroxide cure, **ZnO is often added as a filler/activator** — credit is given for the activator + accelerator mechanism in sulphur systems.
> 7. **Numerical: forgetting to convert °C to K** — silent mark loser.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vulcanisation**

- **Definition:** Vulcanisation = formation of 3-D crosslinked network in rubber using curatives (S, peroxides, oxides).
- **Sulphur vulcanisation** is the **most widely used** industrial method.
- The **activator system ZAS** (ZnO + Stearic acid) is **mandatory** — without it, vulcanisation is impractically slow.
- **Accelerators** reduce cure time from hours to minutes; **sulphenamides (CBS, MBS, TBBS)** offer the best balance of speed and scorch safety.
- **Crosslink types** (in order of length): C—C (peroxide) < C—S—C (mono) < C—S₂—C (di) < C—S₃₋₈—C (polysulphide).
- **A/S ratio** controls crosslink type: low → polysulphide (CV); high → monosulphide (EV).
- **Physical effects:** Tensile strength ↑ 100×, elasticity ↑, cold flow ↓, tackiness ↓, solvent resistance ↑.
- **Rheometer parameters:** $M_L$, $M_H$, $t_{s1}$, $t_{90}$, **CRI** = $100 / (t_{90} - t_{s1})$.
- **Master equation of rubber elasticity:** $G = \nu R T$ and $E = 3G$ (incompressible).
- **Molecular weight between crosslinks:** $M_c = \rho / \nu$.
- **Flory–Rehner equation** gives $\nu$ from equilibrium swelling.
- **Peroxide cure** gives thermally stable **C–C crosslinks** — preferred for silicone, EPDM, HNBR, food-grade rubber.
- **Metal oxide cure** is used for **chloroprene rubber (CR/Neoprene)**.
- **Phenolic resin cure** is used for **butyl rubber (IIR)**.
- **Stages of vulcanisation (mnemonic "S-C-R"):** Scorch → Cure → Reversion (or Plateau).
- **Tyre-grade rubber** uses CV systems; **cable insulation and heat-resistant articles** use EV systems.
- **Discoverers:** Charles Goodyear (1839, accidental); Thomas Hancock (1843, patent).
- **Vulcanisation is irreversible** — it converts a **thermoplastic** raw rubber into a **thermoset** finished rubber.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curing characteristics: scorch time, induction time, cure time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT501 - RUBBERS-SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Curing characteristics: scorch time, induction time, cure time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Curing (Vulcanization)
**Curing (Vulcanization)** is a thermosetting process in which chemical cross-links are formed between long-chain rubber polymer molecules under the influence of heat and pressure, in the presence of a cross-linking agent (typically sulfur) and accelerators. The result is a three-dimensional network that transforms soft, plastic raw rubber into a tough, elastic, mechanically strong elastomer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The curing characteristics—**scorch time, induction time, and cure time**—are measured experimentally using a **Rheometer** (oscillating disc type or moving die type). The plot of **Torque vs. Time** is called the **Rheograph (Cure Curve)** and is the primary diagnostic tool for process control in rubber manufacturing.

### 1.2 Key Curing Characteristics — Definitions

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | KTU Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Induction Time** | $t_i$ | Time from the start of the test until the torque begins to deviate upward from the minimum (M_L). Represents the safe processing window where no significant crosslinking has yet occurred. |
| **Scorch Time** | $t_{s2}$ (commonly $t_s$) | Time to reach a torque value of $M_L + 2$ units (dN·m) above the minimum. It marks the practical onset of vulcanization—after this, the compound is no longer safely processable. |
| **Cure Time (Optimum)** | $t_{90}$ | Time required to reach **90%** of the maximum torque ($M_H$). It is the time at which the rubber is considered "technically fully cured" for engineering use. |
| **Cure Time (Full/Theoretical)** | $t_{100}$ | Time to reach 100% of the maximum torque. |
| **Minimum Torque** | $M_L$ | Lowest torque recorded; proportional to the **uncured viscosity** at test temperature. |
| **Maximum Torque** | $M_H$ | Highest torque reached; proportional to the **final crosslink density**. |
| **Delta Torque** | $\Delta M = M_H - M_L$ | Net change; directly proportional to crosslink density (Stoke's law analogy). |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Baking a Cake" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (The Cake-Bake Analogy):** Imagine baking a sponge cake.
> - **Induction Time ($t_i$)** is the period when the batter sits in the warm oven—still liquid, no rise. This is your "free time" to pour and shape it.
> - **Scorch Time ($t_{s2}$)** is the exact moment the batter begins to **rise** (small bubbles forming). The door is closing—shoving it back in or reshaping is now impossible without ruining the cake.
> - **Cure Time ($t_{90}$)** is when the cake is **90% baked**—firm, springy, and ready to take out. A bit more time overcooks it.
> - **$M_L$** = thickness of the raw batter (viscosity); **$M_H$** = stiffness of the finished cake (final structure).

### 1.4 Why Are These Three Times Critical in Industry?

1. **Induction / Scorch Time** → Determines the **processing safety** window. If too short, the compound "scorches" (premature curing) in the extruder, mold, or calender—causing **scrap**.
2. **Cure Time ($t_{90}$)** → Determines the **mold residence time** in production. A shorter $t_{90}$ means higher productivity (more parts/hour), but if too fast, it may not flow into the mold completely.
3. **M_H − M_L** → Determines the **final mechanical properties** (hardness, modulus, tensile strength) of the vulcanizate.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rheograph — Anatomy of a Cure Curve

When a rubber compound is placed in a rheometer at a fixed test temperature (e.g., **160 °C** is the KTU-typical reference temperature), the torque required to oscillate the die is recorded as a function of time. The resulting curve has four distinct regions:

1. **Region I — Stiffening Zone (Mushroom/Pre-cure):** Torque drops from the initial charging peak to $M_L$. The compound softens as it warms up.
2. **Region II — Induction Plateau:** Torque remains nearly flat at $M_L$. Crosslinking reactions are chemically "primed" but not yet significant. This is the **Induction Period**.
3. **Region III — Curing Zone:** Torque rises sharply as crosslinks form. The slope here is the **cure rate**.
4. **Region IV — Plateau/Reversion Zone:** Torque reaches $M_H$. In natural rubber, prolonged heating may cause **reversion** (torque decreases) due to chain scission. In synthetic rubbers (SBR, EPDM), the curve typically plateaus.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Interpretation Logic

- **Step 1:** Identify the lowest point on the curve → label it **$M_L$**.
- **Step 2:** Identify the peak (or stable maximum) → label it **$M_H$**.
- **Step 3:** Compute the **target torque for 90% cure**:
  $$ M_{90} = M_L + 0.9 \times (M_H - M_L) $$
- **Step 4:** Draw a horizontal line at $M_{90}$ until it intersects the rising portion of the curve → the intersection's time is **$t_{90}$**.
- **Step 5:** Similarly, the **scorch torque** is:
  $$ M_{s2} = M_L + 2 \quad \text{(dN·m)} $$
  The time at which the curve first reaches $M_{s2}$ is **$t_{s2}$** (scorch time).
- **Step 6:** The **induction time** $t_i$ is the time at the first observable upward inflection from $M_L$ (often numerically very close to $t_{s2}$ but slightly earlier).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "90%" and not "100%"?** Because at $M_H$ the crosslink network is fully developed, but additional time in the mold wastes energy and may cause reversion. Industry has standardized on **$t_{90}$** as the **optimum cure time**—the best balance between completeness of cure and economic cycle time.

### 2.3 Cure Rate Index (CRI) — A Productivity Metric

A very important derived quantity frequently asked in KTU examinations is the **Cure Rate Index**:

$$ \text{CRI} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} \;\; [\text{min}^{-1}] $$

A **higher CRI** means a faster curing compound → shorter production cycles → higher throughput.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum Torque | $M_L$ | dN·m | Uncured viscosity proxy |
| 2 | Maximum Torque | $M_H$ | dN·m | Final crosslink density proxy |
| 3 | Delta Torque | $\Delta M = M_H - M_L$ | dN·m | Net crosslink density indicator |
| 4 | Scorch Torque | $M_{s2} = M_L + 2$ | dN·m | Practical onset threshold |
| 5 | Optimum Cure Torque | $M_{90} = M_L + 0.9(M_H - M_L)$ | dN·m | 90% cure target |
| 6 | Induction Time | $t_i$ | min | Processing safety (chemical delay) |
| 7 | Scorch Time | $t_{s2}$ | min | End of safe processing window |
| 8 | Optimum Cure Time | $t_{90}$ | min | Mold residence time (design) |
| 9 | Cure Rate Index | $\text{CRI} = 100/(t_{90} - t_{s2})$ | $\text{min}^{-1}$ | Production throughput proxy |
| 10 | Crosslink Density (qualitative) | $\nu \propto \Delta M$ | — | Higher $\Delta M$ → harder rubber |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Isolation Rule Followed:** All vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity. The internal subscripts $t_{s2}$ and $t_{90}$ do not contain any pipe symbols.

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Tire Manufacturing:** $t_{s2}$ must be long enough to allow the green tire to be built layer-by-layer (extrusion, calendering, ply assembly) but short enough to make the curing press cycle economical.
- **Molded Rubber Goods (gaskets, O-rings):** $t_{90}$ determines press cycle time. Under-cured parts fail in service; over-cured parts waste energy and may degrade.
- **Quality Control:** Any batch of rubber that shows $M_L$ shifted upward indicates a Mooney viscosity issue (poor processing); $M_H$ shifted downward indicates under-cure (low crosslink density).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Locating $t_{90}$ on a Rheometer Curve

**Given:**
- $M_L$ = minimum torque from the curve
- $M_H$ = maximum (or equilibrium) torque from the curve
- The rheograph is a continuous plot of $M(t)$.

**Step 1 — Define the cure level at any time $t$:**
The fractional state of cure is conventionally defined as:
$$ \alpha(t) = \frac{M(t) - M_L}{M_H - M_L} $$

This equation states that the "amount of curing" is proportional to how far the current torque has moved from the minimum toward the maximum. When $M(t) = M_L$, then $\alpha = 0$ (no cure). When $M(t) = M_H$, then $\alpha = 1$ (100% cure).

**Step 2 — Set $\alpha = 0.9$ (for optimum cure):**
$$ 0.9 = \frac{M(t_{90}) - M_L}{M_H - M_L} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for $M(t_{90})$:**
Multiplying both sides by $(M_H - M_L)$:
$$ 0.9 \times (M_H - M_L) = M(t_{90}) - M_L $$

Adding $M_L$ to both sides:
$$ M(t_{90}) = M_L + 0.9 \times (M_H - M_L) $$

This is the target torque. Numerically, you read off the time at which the rheograph first crosses this horizontal torque value — that is $t_{90}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cure Rate Index (CRI)

**Step 1 — Total time available for active curing** (after scorch) is the difference between cure time and scorch time:
$$ \Delta t_{cure} = t_{90} - t_{s2} $$

**Step 2 — Define CRI as reciprocal of this interval, scaled by 100** (to express as %/min, a common industry unit):
$$ \text{CRI} = \frac{100}{\Delta t_{cure}} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} $$

**Units check:** $[100] / [\text{min}] = \text{min}^{-1}$ (or %·min⁻¹ if the 100 is interpreted as a percentage).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A rubber compound is tested in an oscillating disc rheometer at 160 °C. The following data are extracted from the rheograph:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $M_L$ | **8 dN·m** |
| $M_H$ | **62 dN·m** |
| $t_{s2}$ | **3.5 min** |
| $t_{90}$ | **12.0 min** |

**Calculate: (i) Delta torque, (ii) Scorch torque, (iii) 90% cure torque, (iv) Cure Rate Index.**

---

**Solution (i) — Delta Torque $\Delta M$:**
$$ \Delta M = M_H - M_L $$
$$ \Delta M = 62 - 8 = 54 \;\text{dN·m} $$

**Solution (ii) — Scorch Torque $M_{s2}$:**
$$ M_{s2} = M_L + 2 = 8 + 2 = 10 \;\text{dN·m} $$

**Solution (iii) — 90% Cure Torque $M_{90}$:**
$$ M_{90} = M_L + 0.9 \,(M_H - M_L) $$
$$ M_{90} = 8 + 0.9 \times (62 - 8) $$
$$ M_{90} = 8 + 0.9 \times 54 $$
$$ M_{90} = 8 + 48.6 = 56.6 \;\text{dN·m} $$

**Solution (iv) — Cure Rate Index (CRI):**
$$ \text{CRI} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} = \frac{100}{12.0 - 3.5} = \frac{100}{8.5} $$
$$ \text{CRI} \approx 11.76 \;\text{min}^{-1} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A CRI of ~11.76 min⁻¹ is a moderately fast-curing compound, typical of a passenger-tire tread formulation. Slower-curing compounds (e.g., thick cable-jacketing compounds) have CRI ≈ 5–7 min⁻¹; high-speed injection-molded compounds can exceed CRI ≈ 25 min⁻¹.

### 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Temperature on Cure Time (Arrhenius-Type)

In a KTU problem, you may be asked how $t_{90}$ changes with temperature. A common model is:

$$ \ln(t_{90}) = \frac{E_a}{RT} + C $$

where $E_a$ is the activation energy, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature. By comparing $t_{90}$ at two temperatures, the activation energy is:

$$ E_a = R \cdot \frac{\ln(t_{90,2}/t_{90,1})}{(1/T_1) - (1/T_2)} $$

This equation is essential for **predicting mold cure times** at production temperatures different from the lab test temperature.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the following terms with reference to rubber curing:**
**(a) Scorch time, (b) Induction time, (c) Optimum cure time ($t_{90}$).**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Scorch time ($t_{s2}$):** The time elapsed, at a given curing temperature, from the start of the test until the torque has risen to a value of $M_L + 2$ dN·m above the minimum torque. It represents the practical end of the safe processing window — beyond this, the compound can no longer be safely shaped or flowed.

(b) **Induction time ($t_i$):** The time from the beginning of heating to the first observable upward deviation of the torque from the minimum. It is the chemical delay period during which accelerators and activators are reacting with sulfur to form active crosslinking species, but the crosslink network is not yet growing perceptibly. It is a measure of the **processing safety** of the compound.

(c) **Optimum cure time ($t_{90}$):** The time required for the torque to reach 90% of the maximum torque, calculated as $M_{90} = M_L + 0.9 \times (M_H - M_L)$. It is the recommended mold residence time, balancing complete vulcanization with production economy.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Cure Rate Index (CRI)? State its significance in rubber processing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Cure Rate Index is a numerical measure of how fast a rubber compound cures once the scorch point has been crossed. It is defined as:

$$ \text{CRI} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} \;\; \text{min}^{-1} $$

**Significance:**
- A **higher CRI** indicates a **faster-curing** compound, leading to shorter mold cycle times and higher productivity.
- It is used to **compare accelerator systems**: e.g., a compound using TBBS (a delayed-action accelerator) will have a lower CRI but a longer scorch safety than one using CBS or DPG.
- In quality control, sudden deviations of CRI from the established specification indicate changes in the batch — e.g., variation in accelerator loading, contamination, or aging of the compound.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]

**(a) Draw a typical rheograph (torque vs. time curve) obtained from an oscillating disc rheometer and label the following: $M_L$, $M_H$, $t_{s2}$, $t_{90}$, induction time, scorch time, and the optimum cure torque. (7 marks)**

**(b) A rubber compound is tested at 160 °C. The following data are obtained: $M_L = 12$ dN·m, $M_H = 78$ dN·m, $t_{s2} = 4.0$ min, $t_{90} = 14.5$ min. Calculate (i) Delta torque, (ii) $M_{s2}$, (iii) $M_{90}$, and (iv) Cure Rate Index. Comment on the processing safety and productivity of this compound. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Rheograph Sketch and Labeling (7 marks):**

A typical rheograph is a plot of **Torque (dN·m) on the Y-axis** versus **Time (min) on the X-axis**. The student should describe and label:

- **Y-axis:** Torque $M$ (dN·m)
- **X-axis:** Time $t$ (minutes)
- **Charging peak** (initial spike, then decay)
- **Minimum torque $M_L$** — the lowest point of the curve.
- **Maximum torque $M_H$** — the peak or equilibrium of the curve.
- **Induction time $t_i$** — from $t = 0$ to the first upward inflection from $M_L$.
- **Scorch time $t_{s2}$** — from $t = 0$ to the point where the curve crosses the horizontal line at $M_L + 2$ dN·m.
- **Optimum cure time $t_{90}$** — from $t = 0$ to where the curve reaches $M_{90} = M_L + 0.9(M_H - M_L)$.
- **Cure rate slope** — the steep rising portion between $t_{s2}$ and $t_{90}$.
- **Reversion** (if discussing natural rubber) — slow decrease of torque after $M_H$.

The curve has a **plateau at the bottom** (induction period), a **steep rise** (curing zone), and a **flat top** (cured plateau).

**(b) Calculations (7 marks):**

**(i) Delta Torque:**
$$ \Delta M = M_H - M_L = 78 - 12 = 66 \;\text{dN·m} $$

**(ii) Scorch Torque:**
$$ M_{s2} = M_L + 2 = 12 + 2 = 14 \;\text{dN·m} $$

**(iii) Optimum Cure Torque:**
$$ M_{90} = M_L + 0.9(M_H - M_L) = 12 + 0.9 \times 66 = 12 + 59.4 = 71.4 \;\text{dN·m} $$

**(iv) Cure Rate Index:**
$$ \text{CRI} = \frac{100}{t_{90} - t_{s2}} = \frac{100}{14.5 - 4.0} = \frac{100}{10.5} \approx 9.52 \;\text{min}^{-1} $$

**Comment on processing safety and productivity:**
- **Scorch safety:** $t_{s2} = 4.0$ min at 160 °C is acceptable for compression molding (which typically requires 2–5 min of flow time) but **too short for complex extrusions or thick calendered sheets** that need a longer flow window. A retarder or slower accelerator may be considered.
- **Productivity:** $\text{CRI} \approx 9.52$ min⁻¹ is on the **lower side** for a production compound. A target CRI for high-throughput operations is often 12–20 min⁻¹. To improve productivity, a faster accelerator system could be adopted — but only if scorch safety is not compromised.
- **Crosslink density:** $\Delta M = 66$ dN·m is high, indicating a dense, hard vulcanizate — suitable for products requiring good modulus and abrasion resistance (e.g., tire treads, conveyor belts).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2] (Internal Choice)

**(a) Explain the difference between induction time, scorch time, and optimum cure time. Discuss how a formulator can adjust these three times independently by changing the accelerator system. (7 marks)**

**(b) Two rubber compounds, A and B, are tested at 160 °C. The data are:**

| Parameter | Compound A | Compound B |
|---|---|---|
| $M_L$ | 10 dN·m | 14 dN·m |
| $M_H$ | 60 dN·m | 70 dN·m |
| $t_{s2}$ | 5.0 min | 3.0 min |
| $t_{90}$ | 12.0 min | 8.0 min |

**Calculate the CRI and $\Delta M$ for both compounds. Which compound is more process-safe? Which has higher productivity? Justify your answers. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Explanation of the three times and how a formulator adjusts them (7 marks):**

- **Induction time ($t_i$):** Chemical delay — accelerator activation period before any significant crosslinking. It is a property of the **activator–accelerator–sulfur system**.
- **Scorch time ($t_{s2}$):** Practical onset of curing. Slightly longer than $t_i$ (typically $t_{s2} \approx t_i + 0.5$ to $1.0$ min).
- **Optimum cure time ($t_{90}$):** End of the technically useful cure. Set by the crosslink growth kinetics.

**How to adjust them via accelerator choice:**

| Desired Effect | Formulation Adjustment |
|---|---|
| **Increase $t_{s2}$ (longer scorch safety)** | Use a **delayed-action accelerator** like TBBS (N-tert-butyl-2-benzothiazolesulfenamide) or CBS. Or add a **retarder** like N-cyclohexylthiophthalimide (PVI). |
| **Decrease $t_{s2}$ (faster scorch)** | Use a fast primary accelerator like **MBT (2-mercaptobenzothiazole)** or **Dithiocarbamates**. |
| **Decrease $t_{90}$ (faster total cure)** | Use a **booster secondary accelerator** (e.g., TMTD) in combination with a sulfenamide. |
| **Increase $t_{90}$ (slower cure)** | Reduce accelerator loading, use a **single sulfenamide** at lower concentration. |
| **Independent control of scorch vs. cure rate** | Use a **two-accelerator system** (primary sulfenamide + secondary thiuram/dithiocarbamate) — the ratio controls the scorch delay, while the total loading controls the cure rate. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Formulator Insight:** A balanced formulation uses a **primary accelerator (sulfenamide)** for scorch safety and a **secondary accelerator (thiuram, dithiocarbamate)** to push the cure rate. The two are partially **orthogonal** — i.e., one can be tuned somewhat independently of the other.

**(b) Calculations and Comparison (7 marks):**

**Compound A:**
- $\Delta M_A = 60 - 10 = 50$ dN·m
- $\text{CRI}_A = 100 / (12.0 - 5.0) = 100 / 7.0 \approx 14.29$ min⁻¹

**Compound B:**
- $\Delta M_B = 70 - 14 = 56$ dN·m
- $\text{CRI}_B = 100 / (8.0 - 3.0) = 100 / 5.0 = 20.00$ min⁻¹

**Comparison:**

| Criterion | Winner | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing safety (longer $t_{s2}$)** | **Compound A** | $t_{s2,A} = 5.0$ min vs $t_{s2,B} = 3.0$ min. Compound A gives the operator more time to fill the mold. |
| **Productivity (higher CRI)** | **Compound B** | $\text{CRI}_B = 20.0$ vs $\text{CRI}_A = 14.3$. Compound B cures faster → higher throughput. |
| **Final crosslink density (higher $\Delta M$)** | **Compound B** | $\Delta M_B = 56 > \Delta M_A = 50$. Compound B will be harder and stiffer. |
| **Uncured viscosity (lower $M_L$)** | **Compound A** | $M_{L,A} = 10$ < $M_{L,B} = 14$. Compound A flows more easily. |

**Conclusion:** Compound A is preferred for **complex molds** (tire treads, intricate extrusions) where flow time is critical. Compound B is preferred for **high-volume compression molding** of simple parts where fast cycle time is essential.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing $t_{s2}$ with $t_i$:** Many students use these terms interchangeably. $t_i$ is the *inflection* point; $t_{s2}$ is when the torque has risen by **2 dN·m** above $M_L$ — a small but critical distinction worth **1 mark** in KTU valuations.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor 0.9 in $M_{90}$:** A very common error is to write $M_{90} = M_H$. The correct definition is $M_{90} = M_L + 0.9(M_H - M_L)$. Wrong → **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Units for CRI:** Must include min⁻¹. Writing "CRI = 14.29" without units is technically incomplete.
> 4. **Drawing the rheograph as a straight line:** KTU examiners expect a **flat induction plateau**, a **steep cure rise**, and a **flat top** — not a straight diagonal line. This costs up to **2 marks** in the 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Not stating the test temperature:** Always mention that rheometer tests are conventionally done at **160 °C** (or whatever was given in the problem). Temperature context is part of the complete answer.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Curing = vulcanization** = crosslinking of rubber chains by sulfur (or other agents) under heat and pressure.
- [x] **Rheograph** = torque vs. time curve from an oscillating disc or moving die rheometer.
- [x] **$M_L$ (minimum torque)** → proxy for uncured viscosity; occurs at end of softening zone.
- [x] **$M_H$ (maximum torque)** → proxy for final crosslink density; occurs after the cure rise.
- [x] **$\Delta M = M_H - M_L$** → directly proportional to crosslink density (Stokes analogy).
- [x] **Induction time ($t_i$)** = chemical delay; end of induction = first upward inflection from $M_L$.
- [x] **Scorch time ($t_{s2}$)** = time to reach $M_L + 2$ dN·m; **end of safe processing**.
- [x] **Optimum cure time ($t_{90}$)** = time to reach $M_{90} = M_L + 0.9(M_H - M_L)$.
- [x] **Cure Rate Index (CRI)** = $100 / (t_{90} - t_{s2})$; higher CRI = faster cure = higher productivity.
- [x] **Standard test temperature** = 160 °C.
- [x] **Delayed-action accelerators** (TBBS, CBS) → longer scorch safety.
- [x] **Fast accelerators** (MBT, dithiocarbamates) → shorter scorch, faster cure.
- [x] **Reversion** = torque decrease after $M_H$ in natural rubber due to chain scission.
- [x] **Plateau behavior** = typical of SBR, EPDM — no reversion.
- [x] **Industrial utility** — $t_{s2}$ for process safety, $t_{90}$ for cycle time, $\Delta M$ for end-product stiffness.
- [x] **Activation energy** of cure can be extracted from $\ln(t_{90})$ vs. $1/T$ Arrhenius plot.

# MODULE 2 | MECHANISM OF CROSSLINKING BY DIFFERENT CROSSLINKING AGENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Crosslinking (Vulcanisation)** is a chemical process in which linear or branched polymer chains of an elastomer are joined together by covalent bonds through the action of a **crosslinking agent** (also called a **curative** or **vulcanising agent**). The process converts the soft, plastic, and often tacky raw rubber into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable thermoset network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> Crosslinking is the formation of a three-dimensional network structure in an elastomer by the reaction of polyfunctional crosslinking agents with the functional groups (or activated sites) on the polymer backbone, resulting in a thermoset rubber with elastic recovery, solvent resistance, and mechanical strength.

In the course code **PCPOT501** (Polymer Chemistry / Rubber Technology), the specific focus of Module 2 is on the **mechanistic chemistry** of how each curative molecule attacks the rubber chain to create crosslinks.

### 1.2 What is a Crosslinking Agent?

A **crosslinking agent** is a low-molecular-weight chemical substance that contains at least two reactive functional groups capable of forming chemical bridges between adjacent polymer chains. These agents are classified based on the chemistry they employ:

| Crosslinking Agent | Functional Group | Typical Rubbers |
|---|---|---|
| Sulphur (S₈) | Polysulphidic (–Sx–) | NR, SBR, BR, IIR, NBR |
| Sulphur monochloride (S₂Cl₂) | Chlorosulphidic (–S–S–Cl) | Thin NR latex films |
| Phenolic resins | Methylol / Quinone methide | IIR, EPDM, NBR |
| Metal oxides (ZnO, MgO) | Ether / Ionic | CR, CSM, CIIR, BIIR |
| Diamines | Amine / Imine | FKM, ACM, CO, ECO |
| Peroxides (DCP, BPO) | Free radical | EPDM, HNBR, Q, FKM, silicone |
| Oximes (Quinone dioxime) | Nitroso / Imine | IIR |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rusted Ladder"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a heap of loose, slippery silk threads lying on a table — they cannot hold any weight, they slide over each other, and the pile spreads. This is **uncrosslinked raw rubber**.
>
> Now, suppose you take a thin iron wire and stitch the silk threads together at 100 random places, forming a loose net. The net can now hold weight, stretch, and snap back when released — this is **vulcanised rubber**.
>
> The **iron wire** is your **crosslinking agent**, the **stitching points** are **crosslinks**, and the **silk threads** are **polymer chains**.
>
> - **Sulphur** is like a multi-link wire (forms polysulphidic bridges).
> - **Peroxides** are like spot-welding torches (form short, heat-resistant C–C bridges).
> - **Metal oxides** are like staples tuned for special threads (chloroprene).

### 1.4 Why Different Agents?

Different rubbers have **different reactive sites**:
- **Unsaturated rubbers** (NR, SBR, BR) — have C=C double bonds → react with sulphur, peroxides.
- **Saturated rubbers** (EPDM, silicone) — have only C–H bonds → need aggressive agents like peroxides or phenolic resins.
- **Halogenated rubbers** (CR, CSM, CIIR) — have allylic Cl → react with metal oxides.
- **Fluoroelastomers** (FKM) — have C–F and C–H bonds → need amines + accelerators.
- **Acrylic rubbers** (ACM) — have ester groups → need diamines / soap crosslinkers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:**
> The **choice of crosslinking agent depends on the type and reactivity of the functional group on the rubber backbone**. No single crosslinking agent is universal.

### 1.5 General Structural Outcome of Crosslinking

In every mechanism discussed in this module, the ultimate product is a **network point** where three or more polymer chains are joined:

$$
\text{—Chain}_1\text{–X–Y–X–}\text{Chain}_2\text{—}
$$

where **X–Y–X** is the crosslink bridge derived from the curative.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Crosslinking Mechanisms

The seven agents in this module can be grouped into **four mechanistic families**:

| Mechanism Family | Agents | Key Reactive Species |
|---|---|---|
| **Free-radical addition** | Sulphur, Peroxides, Oximes | R•, RO•, RS• |
| **Electrophilic addition** | Sulphur monochloride, Metal oxides | S₂Cl₂, Zn²⁺, Mg²⁺ |
| **Condensation / substitution** | Phenolic resins, Diamines | Quinone methide, amine nucleophiles |
| **Ionic / coordinate** | Metal oxides (CR cure) | Zn–O–C, Mg–O–C |

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Analysis of Each Crosslinking Agent

### A. Sulphur Crosslinking

**Sulphur (S₈)** is the **oldest (discovered 1839 by Charles Goodyear) and most widely used** crosslinking agent for unsaturated rubbers. However, elemental S₈ reacts very slowly, so commercial sulphur cures use an **accelerator system**:

$$
\text{S}_8 + \text{Accelerator} + \text{ZnO} + \text{Stearic Acid} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Active Sulphurating Species}
$$

**Roles of each component:**
- **S₈** — source of sulphur atoms that form the bridge (–Sx–).
- **Accelerator** (MBT, CBS, TBBS, TMTD, ZDMC) — activates S₈.
- **ZnO** — activator; forms Zn–accelerator complex.
- **Stearic acid** — co-activator; solubilises ZnO in rubber.

**Step-by-step mechanistic pathway (KTU textbook standard):**

**Step 1 — Formation of Active Sulphurating Species:**

The accelerator (e.g., tetramethylthiuram monosulphide, TMTM, or its disulphide TMTD) reacts with sulphur to form an **accelerator polysulphide**:

$$
\text{Acc–S–S–Acc} + \text{S}_8 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Acc–S}_x\text{–Acc}
$$

This is a **2,2′-dithio-bis(benzothiazole) / polysulphide**.

**Step 2 — Reaction with the rubber double bond:**

The active species attacks the allylic hydrogen / adds across the C=C double bond to form a **rubber-bound pendant accelerator polysulphide**:

$$
\text{RH} + \text{Acc–S}_x\text{–Acc} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–S}_x\text{–Acc} + \text{H–Acc}
$$

Here, **RH** represents the rubber chain with allylic hydrogen.

**Step 3 — Crosslink precursor formation:**

The pendant group reacts with another rubber chain's double bond to form a **crosslink precursor**:

$$
\text{R–S}_x\text{–Acc} + \text{R'H} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} + \text{H–Acc}
$$

**Step 4 — Crosslink maturation (shortening of –Sx– to –Sx– or –S–):**

The polysulphidic crosslink loses sulphur during cure and over-cure:

$$
\text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta,\;\text{time}}\; \text{R–S}_2\text{–R'} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–S–R'}
$$

This is the **maturation** or **reversion-resistant** crosslink.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Concept — Polysulphidic vs. Monosulphidic Crosslinks:**
> - **Polysulphidic (–Sx–, x > 2)**: Better tensile strength, fatigue resistance, more flexible.
> - **Monosulphidic (–S–)**: Better heat aging, lower compression set.
> - **C–C crosslinks** (no sulphur): Best heat resistance.
> The ratio depends on the accelerator type: **MBT/CBS** give more polysulphidic; **TMTD** gives more monosulphidic.

### B. Sulphur Monochloride (S₂Cl₂) — "Cold Vulcanisation"

Used for **thin-walled articles and latex films** (toys, balloons, dipped goods). The reaction occurs **at room temperature** because S₂Cl₂ is highly reactive.

$$
\text{2 R–CH=CH–R'} + \text{S}_2\text{Cl}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–CH(Cl)–CH–S–S–CH–CH(Cl)–R'}
$$

**Mechanism:** Electrophilic addition across the double bond, forming a **chloro-sulphidic bridge** (–CH(Cl)–CH–S–S–CH–CH(Cl)–).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disadvantage:** HCl is liberated, causing corrosion and degradation. Not suitable for thick articles (heat builds up). Hence called **cold cure / Peachey process**.

### C. Phenolic Resin Crosslinking

**Phenolic resins** (resol-type, e.g., **alkylphenol-formaldehyde resins** cured with SnCl₂ or ZnCl₂) are used for **saturated rubbers** (IIR, EPDM) where sulphur is inefficient.

**Mechanism (two-stage):**

**Stage 1 — Formation of Benzyl Cation / Quinone Methide:**

On heating with a Lewis acid catalyst (SnCl₂·2H₂O), the methylol group (–CH₂OH) on the resin eliminates water:

$$
\text{Res–CH}_2\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{SnCl}_2,\;\Delta}\; \text{Res–CH}_2^+ \;(\text{or } \text{Res–CH=Ph=O}) + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

This produces an **ortho-quinone methide** intermediate, which is highly electrophilic.

**Stage 2 — Electrophilic Attack on Rubber Backbone:**

The quinone methide abstracts an allylic hydrogen from the rubber (this works only if the rubber has some unsaturation, e.g., IIR has a low % of isoprene):

$$
\text{Res–CH}_2^+ + \text{RH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Res–CH}_2\text{–R} + \text{H}^+
$$

Two rubber chains grafted on the same resin molecule give a **crosslink**:

$$
\text{R}_1\text{–CH}_2\text{–Res–CH}_2\text{–R}_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage of phenolic cure:** Excellent **heat resistance**, **low compression set**, and **no sulphur bloom**. Ideal for **IIR inner tubes** and **EPDM radiator hoses**.

### D. Metal Oxide Crosslinking

**Metal oxides** (ZnO, MgO, sometimes PbO) are the primary curatives for **chloroprene rubber (CR / Neoprene)**, **chlorosulphonated polyethylene (CSM / Hypalon)**, and **halogenated butyl rubber (CIIR, BIIR)**.

**Mechanism in CR (with ZnO + MgO):**

CR contains allylic chlorine (–CH=C(Cl)–CH₂–) which is labile.

**Step 1 — Allylic Chlorine Replacement:**

$$
\text{–CH}_2\text{–C(Cl)=CH–} + \text{ZnO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–C(O–Zn–Cl)=CH–}
$$

**Step 2 — Crosslink Formation via Ether Linkage or via MgO:**

$$
\text{2 R–Zn–Cl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–O–R} + \text{ZnCl}_2
$$

or via ionic coordination:

$$
\text{R–O–Zn–O–R}
$$

**Role of MgO:** Acts as a **scorch retarder** and acid acceptor (neutralises HCl generated).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a mixture of ZnO + MgO?**
> - **ZnO alone** → fast but scorchy.
> - **MgO alone** → slow cure, incomplete.
> - **5:4 ZnO:MgO ratio** → optimum cure rate + scorch safety + HCl absorption.

### E. Diamine Crosslinking

**Diamines** (e.g., **hexamethylenediamine (HMDA)**, **ethylenediamine carbamate (EADC)**, **4,4′-methylenedianiline (MDA)**) are used for **fluoroelastomers (FKM)**, **acrylic rubbers (ACM)**, **polyepichlorohydrin (CO, ECO)**.

**Mechanism in FKM (e.g., Viton, copolymer of VDF + HFP):**

**Step 1 — Dehydrofluorination by the amine:**

$$
\text{–CF}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CF(CF}_3\text{)–} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CF}_2\text{–CH=CF(CF}_3\text{)–} + \text{HF} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2
$$

The amine acts as a **base**, abstracting HF to generate a conjugated double bond.

**Step 2 — Michael Addition / Nucleophilic Attack:**

The amine nitrogen attacks the activated double bond:

$$
\text{–CF=CH–} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CF(NHR)–CH}_2\text{–}
$$

The diamine links two chains:

$$
\text{R}_1\text{–CF(NH–R–NH)–CH}_2\text{–R}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Post-cure (oven treatment):** Drives off residual HF and water, completing the network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Diamine cures are used with **MgO / Ca(OH)₂ as acid acceptors** to neutralise the HF liberated. Without these, the rubber would degrade catastrophically.

### F. Peroxide Crosslinking

**Peroxides** (most common: **dicumyl peroxide (DCP)**, also benzoyl peroxide (BPO), 2,5-dimethyl-2,5-di(t-butylperoxy)hexane) are used for **saturated rubbers** (EPDM, silicone, HNBR, FKM, polyethylene blends).

**Mechanism — Free-radical three-step:**

**Step 1 — Homolytic cleavage of peroxide:**

$$
\text{R'–O–O–R'} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; 2 \;\text{R'–O}^{\bullet}
$$

For DCP: gives two cumyloxy radicals; then β-scission gives acetophenone + methyl radical.

**Step 2 — Hydrogen abstraction from rubber (chain transfer):**

$$
\text{R'–O}^{\bullet} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R'–OH} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–}^{\bullet}\text{CH–}
$$

The polymer radical **P•** is formed.

**Step 3 — Radical coupling (crosslink):**

$$
\text{P}_1^{\bullet} + \text{P}_2^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{P}_1\text{–P}_2 \quad (\text{C–C crosslink})
$$

Or with co-agent (e.g., TAC, TAIC):

$$
\text{P}^{\bullet} + \text{TAC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{P–TAC}^{\bullet},\quad \text{P–TAC}^{\bullet} + \text{P}^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{P–TAC–P}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Advantage:** Peroxide cures form **direct C–C crosslinks**, no sulphur, no nitrogens — therefore **superior heat aging, oxidative stability, and compression set resistance**. Disadvantage: lower tensile/elongation in NR; scorchy in presence of antioxidants (which act as radical traps).

### G. Oxime Crosslinking

**Oximes** (specifically **p-quinone dioxime (QDO)** and **p,p′-dibenzoyl quinone dioxime (DBQDO)**) are used for **butyl rubber (IIR)** as a **sulphurless curative** (because IIR cures very slowly with S due to low unsaturation).

**Mechanism:**

**Step 1 — Oxidation of QDO to dinitroso compound by oxidant (PbO₂ or Pb₃O₄):**

$$
\text{HO–N=C}_6\text{H}_4\text{=N–OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{PbO}_2}\; \text{ON–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–NO} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Nitroso group reacts with allylic H of IIR:**

$$
\text{ON–Ar–NO} + 2\;\text{RH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–N(OH)–Ar–N(OH)–R}
$$

**Step 3 — Condensation / dehydration:**

$$
\text{R–N(OH)–Ar–N(OH)–R} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; \text{R–N=Ar=N–R} + 2\;\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

This produces a **quinone diimine crosslink**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** QDO + PbO₂ + ZnO gives fast cures for IIR cable insulation. DBQDO is less scorchy and is dispersed in IIR.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are essential for KTU Part-B derivations:**

### Table 2.1 — Crosslinking Mechanics Formulas

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Crosslink density (tetrafunctional) | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2 M_c}$ | $\nu$ in mol/cm³; $M_c$ in g/mol |
| Crosslink density (trifunctional) | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{3 M_c}$ | for trifunctional crosslinks |
| Flory-Rehner equation (swelling) | $M_c = \dfrac{-\rho_s V_s \phi^{1/3}}{\ln(1-\phi) + \phi + \chi \phi^2}$ | $V_s$ molar vol of solvent; $\phi$ polymer volume fraction in swollen gel; $\chi$ Flory-Huggins interaction parameter |
| Swelling ratio | $Q = \dfrac{V_{\text{swollen}}}{V_{\text{dry}}} = \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ | Q is dimensionless |
| Mooney-Rivlin parameter C₁ | $C_1 = \dfrac{\rho RT}{2 M_c}$ | $\rho$ density; R = 8.314 J/mol·K; T in K |
| Statistical theory of network (Gaussian chains) | $\xi = \dfrac{\nu_{eff}}{V_0}$ | $\xi$ = number of effective network chains per unit volume |
| Degree of crosslinking (XLD %) | $\%\,\text{XLD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of crosslinks}}{\text{Number of monomer units}} \times 100$ | typical 0.5–5 % |
| Mullins effect indicator (no formula, conceptual) | — | Strain-induced softening in filled rubbers |
| Reversion (sulphur cure) | $\dfrac{d[\text{mono-}]}{dt} = k[\text{poly-}][\text{Activator}]$ | Empirical |
| Activation energy of peroxide decomposition | $k_d = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ ≈ 150 kJ/mol for DCP |

**Use of $\vert$ (absolute value) avoidance:** Note in the Flory-Rehner equation the numerator uses the **negative** sign inherently, so the denominator remains a positive value.

### Table 2.2 — Comparative Summary of Crosslinking Agents

| Agent | Crosslink Type | Typical Rubbers | Cure Temp (°C) | Advantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sulphur | Polysulphidic / mono / di-sulphidic | NR, SBR, BR, NBR | 140–160 | Cheap, dynamic props |
| S₂Cl₂ | Chloro-sulphidic | NR latex films | RT (cold) | No heat needed |
| Phenolic resin | C–C (via –CH₂–) | IIR, EPDM | 160–180 | Heat resistant |
| Metal oxides | Ether / ionic | CR, CSM, CIIR, BIIR | 140–170 | Non-staining, flame retardant |
| Diamines | Amine / imine | FKM, ACM, ECO | 160–200 | High-temperature FKM cure |
| Peroxides | Direct C–C | EPDM, Q, FKM, HNBR | 160–180 | Heat + oxidation resistance |
| Oximes | Quinone imine | IIR | 140–170 | Sulphurless IIR cure |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism Comparison

The following **analytical matrix** consolidates the seven crosslinking systems on key chemical descriptors:

| Parameter | Sulphur | S₂Cl₂ | Phenolic | Metal Oxide | Diamine | Peroxide | Oxime |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanism class | Free radical | Electrophilic | Condensation | Ionic / nucleophilic | Nucleophilic | Free radical | Condensation |
| Crosslink bond | C–Sx–C | C–S–S–C | C–C (–CH₂–Ar–CH₂–) | C–O–C | C–N | C–C | C–N=C–C |
| Crosslink energy (kJ/mol) | ≈ 270 (S–S) | ≈ 270 | ≈ 350 (C–C) | ≈ 360 (C–O) | ≈ 305 (C–N) | ≈ 350 (C–C) | ≈ 305 (C–N) |
| Bond length (nm) | ≈ 0.205 | ≈ 0.205 | ≈ 0.154 | ≈ 0.143 | ≈ 0.147 | ≈ 0.154 | ≈ 0.147 |
| Heat resistance | Low–medium | Low | High | Medium | Very high | Very high | High |
| Need accelerator | Yes (essential) | No | Catalyst (SnCl₂) | MgO co-agent | Acid acceptor | Co-agent optional | PbO₂ oxidant |
| Reversion tendency | Yes | No | Slight | No | No | Slight | No |

## 3.2 Detailed Mechanism Derivations

### 3.2.1 Derivation of Crosslink Density from Swelling Data (Flory-Rehner)

This is a high-yield derivation for KTU Module 2 questions.

**Given:**
- Mass of dry rubber: $m_1$ (g)
- Mass of swollen rubber: $m_2$ (g)
- Density of rubber: $\rho_p$ (g/cm³)
- Density of solvent: $\rho_s$ (g/cm³)
- Volume of solvent absorbed: $V_s$ (cm³/mol), the **molar volume**
- $\chi$ = Flory-Huggins polymer-solvent interaction parameter

**Find:** $M_c$, the average molecular weight between crosslinks.

**Derivation Step 1 — Volume fraction of polymer in swollen gel:**

By definition,

$$
\phi = \frac{V_{\text{polymer}}}{V_{\text{polymer}} + V_{\text{solvent}}}
$$

Since $V_{\text{polymer}} = m_1/\rho_p$ and $V_{\text{solvent}} = (m_2 - m_1)/\rho_s$,

$$
\phi = \frac{m_1/\rho_p}{m_1/\rho_p + (m_2 - m_1)/\rho_s}
$$

**Derivation Step 2 — Flory-Rehner Equation (final form):**

For a **tetrafunctional** network at swelling equilibrium, the osmotic contribution and the elastic contribution balance:

$$
\Delta G_{\text{mixing}} + \Delta G_{\text{elastic}} = 0
$$

Solving (standard textbook derivation):

$$
\frac{\rho_p V_s \phi^{1/3}}{M_c} = \ln(1-\phi) + \phi + \chi \phi^2
$$

Rearranging for $M_c$:

$$
M_c = \frac{-\rho_p V_s \phi^{1/3}}{\ln(1-\phi) + \phi + \chi \phi^2}
$$

**Derivation Step 3 — Crosslink density:**

For a tetrafunctional network, each crosslink contributes 2 chain segments:

$$
\nu = \frac{1}{2 M_c} \quad \text{(mol crosslinks / cm}^3\text{)}
$$

**Derivation Step 4 — Number of effective chains per unit volume:**

$$
\xi = \frac{\rho_p N_A}{M_c}
$$

where $N_A$ is Avogadro's number, $6.022 \times 10^{23}\;\text{mol}^{-1}$.

### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A peroxide-cured EPDM sample has the following equilibrium swelling data in cyclohexane:

- $m_1 = 0.502\;\text{g}$
- $m_2 = 1.124\;\text{g}$
- $\rho_p = 0.86\;\text{g/cm}^3$
- $\rho_s = 0.78\;\text{g/cm}^3$
- $V_s = 108\;\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ (molar volume of cyclohexane)
- $\chi = 0.32$

Calculate $M_c$ and the crosslink density.

**Solution:**

**Step A — Polymer volume fraction $\phi$:**

$$
V_{\text{poly}} = \frac{0.502}{0.86} = 0.5837\;\text{cm}^3
$$

$$
V_{\text{solvent}} = \frac{1.124 - 0.502}{0.78} = \frac{0.622}{0.78} = 0.7974\;\text{cm}^3
$$

$$
\phi = \frac{0.5837}{0.5837 + 0.7974} = \frac{0.5837}{1.3811} = 0.4226
$$

**Step B — Evaluate the denominator term:**

$$
\ln(1 - 0.4226) = \ln(0.5774) = -0.5490
$$

$$
\ln(1-\phi) + \phi + \chi\phi^2 = -0.5490 + 0.4226 + (0.32)(0.4226)^2
$$

$$
(0.32)(0.1786) = 0.0572
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = -0.5490 + 0.4226 + 0.0572 = -0.0692
$$

**Step C — Compute numerator:**

$$
\phi^{1/3} = (0.4226)^{1/3} = 0.7500
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = -\rho_p V_s \phi^{1/3} = -(0.86)(108)(0.7500) = -69.66
$$

**Step D — $M_c$:**

$$
M_c = \frac{-69.66}{-0.0692} = 1006\;\text{g/mol}
$$

**Step E — Crosslink density $\nu$:**

$$
\nu = \frac{1}{2 M_c} = \frac{1}{2 \times 1006} = 4.97 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{mol/cm}^3
$$

$$
\nu \approx 5 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{mol/cm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Such $\nu$ values (10⁻⁴ to 10⁻³ mol/cm³) are typical for engineering rubbers. Lower $\nu$ → soft, extensible; higher $\nu$ → rigid, low extension.

### 3.2.3 Peroxide Cure — Estimation of Crosslink Yield

For a peroxide like DCP of concentration [P] (mol/cm³), the theoretical crosslink yield is:

$$
\nu_{\text{theoretical}} = 2 f [P]
$$

where $f$ is the radical efficiency (0.5–1.0 for DCP in most rubbers).

The **actual** crosslink density (from Flory-Rehner above) is **less** than theoretical due to:

- Radical recombination (waste).
- β-scission reactions (chain scission, especially in NR, BR, SBR).
- Oxygen inhibition.

$$
\nu_{\text{actual}} = \nu_{\text{theoretical}} \times \eta
$$

where $\eta$ is the **crosslinking efficiency** (typically 0.3–0.8).

### 3.2.4 Sulphur Cure — Number of Sulphur Atoms per Crosslink

Using elemental analysis (ASTM D5291 for C/H/N/S):

$$
\%\,\text{S}_{\text{bound}} = \frac{\text{mass of S in network}}{\text{mass of rubber}} \times 100
$$

Number of sulphur atoms per crosslink:

$$
x = \frac{\%\,\text{S}_{\text{bound}} \times M_c}{M_S \times 100}
$$

For example, if $\%\,\text{S}_{\text{bound}} = 1.2\;\text{wt}\%$, $M_c = 6000\;\text{g/mol}$, $M_S = 32\;\text{g/mol}$:

$$
x = \frac{1.2 \times 6000}{32 \times 100} = \frac{7200}{3200} = 2.25
$$

Hence **predominantly disulphidic crosslinks** — characteristic of EV (efficient vulcanisation) cure systems.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block-Level Flow Diagram of Crosslinking Agents

This diagram maps each curative to its target rubber type, the intermediate active species, and the resulting crosslink bond.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Rubber Chains with Reactive Sites] --> B{Select Crosslinking Agent}
    
    B -->|Sulphur S8 + Acc + ZnO + StAcid| C1[Accelerator Polysulphide]
    C1 --> C2[Rubber-Bound Pendant Accelerator Polysulphide]
    C2 --> C3[Polysulphidic Crosslink R-Sx-R]
    C3 --> C4[Maturation: Mono / Di-sulphidic Crosslink]
    
    B -->|Sulphur Monochloride S2Cl2| D1[Electrophilic Addition]
    D1 --> D2[Chloro-sulphidic Bridge R-CHCl-S-S-CHCl-R]
    
    B -->|Phenolic Resin + SnCl2| E1[Quinone Methide Intermediate]
    E1 --> E2[C-C Crosslink via -CH2-Ar-CH2-]
    
    B -->|ZnO + MgO| F1[Allylic Cl Abstraction]
    F1 --> F2[Ether / Ionic Crosslink R-O-Zn-O-R]
    
    B -->|Diamines HMDA / MDA| G1[Dehydrofluorination]
    G1 --> G2[Michael Addition]
    G2 --> G3[Amine Crosslink R-NH-R-NH-R]
    
    B -->|Peroxide DCP| H1[Homolytic Cleavage RO-O-R]
    H1 --> H2[H-Abstraction from Rubber]
    H2 --> H3[Direct C-C Crosslink]
    
    B -->|Quinone Dioxime + PbO2| I1[Oxidation to Dinitroso]
    I1 --> I2[Reaction with Allylic H of IIR]
    I2 --> I3[Quinone Diimine Crosslink R-N-Ar-N-R]
    
    C4 --> J[Final 3D Crosslinked Elastomer Network]
    D2 --> J
    E2 --> J
    F2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    H3 --> J
    I3 --> J
    
    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mechanism-Specific Block Diagram: Sulphur Cure (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[S8 Ring] --> S2[Accelerator attacks S8]
    S2 --> S3[Accelerator Polysulphide Acc-Sx-Acc]
    S3 --> S4[Cross S with Zn-Stearate Complex]
    S4 --> S5[Active Sulphurating Species]
    S5 --> S6[Rubber Allylic H Abstraction]
    S6 --> S7[Pendant R-Sx-Acc]
    S7 --> S8[Second Rubber Chain Attack]
    S8 --> S9[Crosslink Precursor R-Sx-R']
    S9 --> S10[Maturation: -Sx- to -S- to -S2-]
    S10 --> S11[Final Cured Network]
    
    style S1 fill:#FFD700
    style S5 fill:#FF6347
    style S11 fill:#32CD32
```

```mermaid
    P2 --> P3[2 Cumyloxy Radicals RO-]
    P3 --> P4[Beta-scission to Methyl Radical + Acetophenone]
    P4 --> P5[RO- + RH = ROH + P-]
    P5 --> P6[Rubber Radical P1- + P2-]
    P6 --> P7[Radical Coupling]
    P7 --> P8[Direct C-C Crosslink P1-P2]

```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Crosslink Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Choose Elastomer] --> Q1{Type of Rubber?}
    
    Q1 -->|Unsaturated NR, SBR, BR| Q2{Application Temperature?}

    Q1 -->|CR, CSM| Q4[Use Metal Oxide ZnO + MgO]

    Q2 -->|Below 100C| P1[Sulphur + Accelerator System]
    Q2 -->|Above 120C| P2[Semi-EV or EV Sulphur]
    
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Peroxide DCP]
    Q3 -->|With Acid| P4[Peroxide + Co-agent TAC]
    
    Q4 --> Z[Crosslink type: Ether / Ionic]
    Q5 --> Y[Crosslink type: C-N]
    Q6 --> X[Crosslink type: Chloro-sulphidic]
```
