<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Why is elemental sulphur not used alone in rubber vulcanisation? What is the role of an accelerator?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Elemental sulphur (S₈) alone reacts extremely slowly with rubber at typical vulcanisation temperatures (140–160 °C), requiring several hours and producing predominantly polysulphidic, thermally unstable crosslinks.

An **accelerator** (e.g., MBT, CBS, TBBS, TMTD) catalyses the S₈ ring opening, generating an **active sulphurating species** (accelerator polysulphide) that rapidly reacts with allylic hydrogens of the rubber. This reduces cure time from hours to minutes, lowers the activation energy, and allows better control over the polysulphidic vs. monosulphidic crosslink ratio.

Typical accelerator loadings are 0.5–2.0 phr, with ZnO and stearic acid as the activator/co-activator system.

**(3 marks: definition 1, accelerator role 1, benefits 1)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Distinguish between sulphur and peroxide vulcanisation systems. Mention any two advantages of peroxide cures."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sulphur Cure | Peroxide Cure |
|---|---|---|
| Crosslink type | C–Sx–C (polysulphidic) | Direct C–C |
| Heat resistance | Lower (Sx bonds break at 100–120 °C) | Higher (C–C stable to 200 °C) |
| Compression set | Higher | Lower |
| Required activator | Accelerator + ZnO + StAcid | Heat only; co-agent for efficiency |
| Reverted over cure | Yes (reversion) | No |

**Two advantages of peroxide cure:**
1. **Superior heat and oxidative aging resistance** due to absence of sulphur.
2. **Lower compression set** and **no reversion** — ideal for O-rings, gaskets.
3. (Bonus) Works on **saturated rubbers** (EPDM, silicone) where sulphur cannot.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Describe the **mechanism of sulphur vulcanisation** of natural rubber with the help of a four-step reaction pathway. Highlight the role of accelerator and ZnO–stearic acid activator system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **mechanism of peroxide crosslinking** of EPDM rubber. Discuss why peroxide cures are preferred for heat-resistant applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Sulphur Vulcanisation Mechanism (7 Marks)

The vulcanisation of NR with sulphur is a **free-radical / concerted mechanism** that occurs in four canonical steps.

**Step 1: Activation of S₈ by the accelerator–ZnO–stearic acid complex.**

The accelerator (e.g., MBT or its zinc salt Zn–MBT) reacts with elemental sulphur, opening the S₈ ring and forming a perthiomercaptide-type active species:

$$
\text{Zn(MBT)}_2 + \text{S}_8 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Zn(MBT)–S}_x\text{–MBT}
$$

This is the **active sulphurating species**. The reaction is catalysed by ZnO, which forms the soluble zinc accelerator complex with stearic acid (acting as a solubilising ligand).

**Step 2: Reaction with rubber — formation of rubber-bound accelerator polysulphide.**

The active species abstracts an **allylic hydrogen** from the isoprene unit of NR (or adds across the C=C):

$$
\text{RH} + \text{Zn(MBT)–S}_x\text{–MBT} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–S}_x\text{–MBT} + \text{Zn(MBT)}_2
$$

This creates a **pendant accelerator polysulphide** attached to the rubber backbone.

**Step 3: Formation of crosslink precursor — second chain attack.**

The pendant group attacks a second rubber chain at its allylic position:

$$
\text{R–S}_x\text{–MBT} + \text{R'H} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} + \text{HMBT}
$$

The **regenerated accelerator** (HMBT) re-enters the cycle. The product R–Sx–R′ is the **crosslink precursor**.

**Step 4: Maturation of the crosslink.**

During cure and post-cure, polysulphidic bridges (x = 4–8) desulphurise progressively:

$$
\text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; \text{R–S}_2\text{–R'} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–S–R'}
$$

The final network contains **a mixture of poly-, di-, and mono-sulphidic bridges**, with the ratio determined by accelerator type and cure temperature.

**(Marks distribution: Step 1 – 2, Step 2 – 2, Step 3 – 1, Step 4 – 1, role of ZnO – 1)**

#### Part (b) — Peroxide Crosslinking of EPDM (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Homolytic cleavage of the peroxide.**

Dicumyl peroxide (DCP) undergoes O–O bond homolysis at 160–170 °C:

$$
\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)–O–O–C(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)(CH}_3\text{)}_2 \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; 2\;\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)–O}^{\bullet}
$$

Each cumyloxy radical then undergoes β-scission:

$$
\text{(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{C(C}_6\text{H}_5\text{)–O}^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{acetophenone} + \text{CH}_3^{\bullet}
$$

**Step 2: Hydrogen abstraction from the rubber.**

The methyl (or cumyloxy) radical abstracts a hydrogen from the EPDM backbone:

$$
\text{CH}_3^{\bullet} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{CH}_4 + \text{–CH}_2\text{–}^{\bullet}\text{CH–}
$$

A **macroradical P•** is formed.

**Step 3: Radical coupling (crosslink).**

Two macroradicals combine to form a **direct C–C crosslink**:

$$
\text{P}_1^{\bullet} + \text{P}_2^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{P}_1\text{–P}_2
$$

**Why peroxide cures are preferred for heat-resistant applications:**

1. **C–C bond energy ≈ 350 kJ/mol**, much higher than **S–S bond ≈ 270 kJ/mol**, so the network does not break down at elevated temperatures.
2. **No sulphur or nitrogen in the network** → no oxidative degradation of crosslink sites.
3. **Excellent compression set resistance** and **low reversion**, vital for EPDM radiator hoses, silicone oven seals.
4. **Co-agents** like triallyl cyanurate (TAC) increase the crosslink efficiency and reduce radical waste.

**(Marks distribution: Step 1 – 1.5, Step 2 – 1.5, Step 3 – 1, heat resistance – 3)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** Discuss the **mechanism of metal oxide (ZnO/MgO) crosslinking** of chloroprene rubber (CR). Explain the role of both oxides. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of diamine crosslinking** of fluoroelastomers (FKM). Why is an acid acceptor essential in this system? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Metal Oxide Crosslinking of CR (7 Marks)

Chloroprene rubber (CR, Neoprene) contains **allylic chlorine** atoms (1,4-trans-polychloroprene unit: –CH₂–C(Cl)=CH–CH₂–) which are chemically labile due to resonance stabilisation.

**Step 1: Substitution of allylic chlorine by ZnO.**

ZnO attacks the allylic position, replacing Cl:

$$
\text{–CH}_2\text{–C(Cl)=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{ZnO} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CH}_2\text{–C(O–Zn–Cl)=CH–CH}_2\text{–}
$$

The resulting structure is a **zinc-chloro allyl ether**.

**Step 2: Crosslink formation via ether linkage.**

Two zinc-chloro allyl ether units condense to form a **di-allyl ether bridge** between two polymer chains:

$$
2\;\text{R–O–Zn–Cl} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–O–R} + \text{ZnCl}_2
$$

This is the principal crosslink. A secondary crosslink type is the **direct ether linkage (–C–O–C–)** without Zn.

**Roles:**
- **ZnO** — primary crosslinking agent; reacts with allylic Cl to form the crosslink.
- **MgO** — acts as (i) **scorch retarder** by absorbing HCl that would otherwise cause premature crosslinking, (ii) **acid acceptor** for HCl released during cure, and (iii) **vulcanisation activator** when used with ZnO.

A typical ratio is **5 phr ZnO : 4 phr MgO**, providing optimum cure rate and processing safety.

**Mark distribution: substitution – 2, crosslink – 2, ZnO role – 1.5, MgO role – 1.5**

#### Part (b) — Diamine Crosslinking of FKM (7 Marks)

Fluoroelastomers (e.g., Viton, copolymer of VDF and HFP) require diamine curatives such as **hexamethylenediamine carbamate (HMDA-C)** or **4,4′-methylenedianiline (MDA)**.

**Step 1: Dehydrofluorination.**

The diamine (or its carbamate salt) acts as a strong base, abstracting HF from the polymer backbone:

$$
\text{–CF}_2\text{–CH}_2\text{–CF(CF}_3\text{)–} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2 \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CF}_2\text{–CH=CF(CF}_3\text{)–} + \text{HF} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH}_2
$$

This generates an **activated double bond** (C=C with adjacent CF groups — Michael acceptor).

**Step 2: Michael-type nucleophilic addition.**

The amine nitrogen (free or regenerated) attacks the activated double bond on a second chain:

$$
\text{–CF=CH–} + \text{H}_2\text{N–R–NH–CF–CH}_2\text{–} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{–CF(NHR–NH)–CH}_2\text{–}
$$

This forms the **crosslinked amine bridge** of structure C–N–C.

**Step 3: Post-cure (oven treatment at 200–250 °C for 24 h).**

Drives off residual HF, completes network formation, and improves mechanical properties.

**Why acid acceptor is essential:**

The HF liberated during dehydrofluorination is highly corrosive and attacks the polymer backbone, causing chain scission and embrittlement. **MgO or Ca(OH)₂ (5–10 phr)** neutralises the HF:

$$
\text{MgO} + 2\;\text{HF} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{MgF}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

Without acid acceptor, the cure cannot proceed and the elastomer degrades.

**Mark distribution: dehydrofluorination – 2, Michael addition – 2, post-cure – 1, acid acceptor role – 2**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Students Must Avoid:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "polymer radical" with "ionic intermediate"** in peroxide mechanism. Peroxide cure is **strictly free-radical**.
> 2. **Do NOT state "sulphur forms C–C crosslinks"** — sulphur always forms **C–Sx–C**. Only peroxides (and a few other systems) give direct C–C.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the activator system** in sulphur mechanism (ZnO + stearic acid). Examiners specifically allocate marks for mentioning this.
> 4. **Do NOT generalise "metal oxide cure"** as a single mechanism. Specify **which rubber** (CR, CSM, etc.) because the mechanism differs.
> 5. **For diamine cure**, always mention the **acid acceptor** — without it, no marks for the crosslink step.
> 6. **For peroxide cure**, mention **β-scission** to acetophenone + methyl radical — this is the high-yield detail.
> 7. **Units in Flory-Rehner** — always express $M_c$ in g/mol and $\nu$ in mol/cm³. Mixed units lose a mark.
> 8. **Spelling**: "vulcanisation" (British) or "vulcanization" (American) — KTU accepts both, but be consistent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Crosslinking Agents**

### 🔑 Crosslinking Agents at a Glance

- **Sulphur (S₈):** Oldest, cheapest, polysulphidic crosslinks; **must use accelerator + ZnO + stearic acid**; cure temp 140–160 °C.
- **Sulphur monochloride (S₂Cl₂):** Cold vulcanisation for **thin NR latex films**; releases HCl; room temperature cure.
- **Phenolic resins:** Used for **saturated rubbers (IIR, EPDM)**; cure via **quinone methide**; require **Lewis acid catalyst** (SnCl₂); form stable **–CH₂–Ar–CH₂–** crosslinks.
- **Metal oxides (ZnO + MgO):** Used for **CR, CSM, CIIR, BIIR**; mechanism involves **allylic Cl substitution**; MgO = acid acceptor + scorch retarder.
- **Diamines (HMDA, MDA):** Used for **FKM, ACM, ECO**; mechanism = **dehydrofluorination + Michael addition**; require **MgO/Ca(OH)₂ acid acceptor**.
- **Peroxides (DCP, BPO):** Used for **saturated rubbers, EPDM, silicone, FKM**; mechanism = **homolysis → H-abstraction → radical coupling**; form **direct C–C crosslinks**; superior heat resistance.
- **Oximes (QDO, DBQDO):** Used for **IIR** as **sulphurless curative**; oxidised to **dinitroso** by **PbO₂**; form **quinone diimine crosslinks**.

### 📌 Key Formulas to Memorise

- $\nu = 1/(2M_c)$ — tetrafunctional
- Flory-Rehner: $M_c = -\rho V_s \phi^{1/3} / [\ln(1-\phi) + \phi + \chi \phi^2]$
- $C_1 = \rho RT/(2M_c)$ — Mooney-Rivlin
- $\nu = 2 f [P]$ — theoretical peroxide crosslink density

### 📊 Comparative Insights

- Crosslink bond strengths: **C–C (350) > C–O (360) ≈ C–N (305) > S–S (270)** kJ/mol.
- Heat resistance ranking: **Peroxide ≈ Diamine > Phenolic > Oxime > Sulphur > S₂Cl₂**.
- Saturated rubbers → only **peroxide / phenolic / diamine** work.
- Halogenated rubbers → **metal oxide** is primary cure.

### ✅ Examinable Quick-Facts

1. Why is MgO added in CR cure? → Acid acceptor + scorch retarder.
2. What is the active sulphurating species? → Accelerator polysulphide (Acc–Sx–Acc).
3. Why are peroxides scorchy with antioxidants? → Antioxidants are radical scavengers, competing with H-abstraction.
4. Difference between EV and CV cure? → EV (efficient vulcanisation) uses more accelerator + less S → more mono/disulphidic crosslinks → better aging.
5. Why is QDO used for IIR? → IIR has low unsaturation; sulphur cure is too slow; QDO is a fast, sulphurless alternative.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different curing systems: semi-EV, conventional and sulphurless cure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT501 - RUBBERS-SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Different curing systems: semi-EV, conventional and sulphurless cure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "Curing System" in Rubber Technology?

**Formal Definition:**
A **curing (vulcanization) system** is the specific combination and proportion of vulcanizing agents (sulphur, peroxides, metal oxides, etc.), accelerators, activators, and retarders formulated into a rubber compound to generate a controlled three-dimensional network of crosslinks between polymer chains during the curing reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **same base polymer** can yield radically different end-use properties (ageing resistance, dynamic fatigue life, compression set, heat build-up, scorch safety) simply by changing the **type and stoichiometry of the curing system**. The curing system is therefore the single most influential design lever in a rubber formulation.

**Curative Classification (KTU Syllabus Scope):**

| Class | Sulphur Present? | Typical Sulphur Level | Accelerator Level | Dominant Crosslink Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (CV)** | Yes (high) | 2.5 – 3.5 phr | 0.5 – 1.0 phr | Polysulphidic |
| **Semi-Efficient (Semi-EV)** | Yes (medium) | 1.0 – 1.7 phr | 1.2 – 2.5 phr | Mixed (mono/di/poly) |
| **Efficient Vulcanization (EV)** | Yes (low) | 0.3 – 0.7 phr | 2.5 – 5.0 phr | Mono + Disulphidic |
| **Sulphurless Cure** | No elemental S | 0 phr | High (TMTD/Donors) | C–C / Mono via donors |

*(phr = parts per hundred rubber, by mass)*

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bridge Builder"

Imagine each rubber polymer chain as a **long, slippery rope floating in water**.

- **No curing system** → ropes slide past each other → sticky, weak, plastic mass (raw gum rubber).
- **Conventional (CV) cure** → bridges are built using **long, flexible ropes of sulphur (S₄–S₈ polysulphide chains)**. They hold strongly, but if the river (heat/sun) gets turbulent, these long rope-bridges stretch, soften, and **may snap and re-tie incorrectly** → poor ageing.
- **EV cure** → bridges are built using **short, rigid, stainless-steel links (mono/disulphide)**. They do not stretch much, are very heat-stable, but the rubber feels stiffer and cracks earlier under repeated bending.
- **Semi-EV** → a **mix of long and short bridges** → best compromise.
- **Sulphurless cure** → bridges made of **epoxy glue / peroxide welds** with no sulphur at all → chemically inert, no sulphur bloom, but glue is brittle and costly.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bridge Material" is what defines the cure system.** Sulphur is the bridge; the accelerator decides whether the bridge is long (polysulphidic) or short (mono/di-sulphidic).

---

### 1.3 Why Multiple Systems Exist — Design Trade-off

Every rubber product demands a different balance of properties:

- **Tyre treads / dynamic parts** → require flexibility, hot-tear strength → **Conventional**.
- **Heat-resistant hoses, O-rings, seals** → require thermal stability → **EV**.
- **General mechanical rubber goods (MRG)** → require balanced properties → **Semi-EV**.
- **White / coloured products, food-grade articles** → no sulphur bloom → **Sulphurless / peroxide / donor systems**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Sulphur : Accelerator (S : Acc) Stoichiometric Compass

The **single most important numerical ratio** in cure-system design is the **Sulphur-to-Accelerator weight ratio (S/Acc)**. It dictates the crosslink type distribution:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\text{Sulphur (phr)}}{\text{Accelerator (phr)}} \;=\; \text{Ratio that determines crosslink type}}$$

| S : Acc Ratio | Cure System | Dominant Crosslink |
|---|---|---|
| ≥ 6 : 1 | Conventional | Polysulphidic (≥ 70 %) |
| 1.0 : 1 to 3 : 1 | Semi-EV | Mixed (≈ 50 % mono/di + 50 % poly) |
| ≤ 0.5 : 1 | EV | Mono + Disulphidic (≥ 80 %) |
| 0 : 1 (donor only) | Sulphurless | C–C / S-donor based |

---

### 2.2 Detailed Mechanics of Each Cure System

#### 2.2.1 Conventional Vulcanization (CV) System

**Composition:** Sulphur 2.5 – 3.5 phr + Accelerator 0.5 – 1.0 phr + ZnO 3 – 5 phr + Stearic Acid 1 – 2 phr.

**Mechanism (logical steps):**
1. Accelerator (e.g., MBT, MBTS) reacts with ZnO + Stearic Acid → active Zn-accelerator complex.
2. Complex activates S₈ ring → opens it into a linear polysulphide.
3. Polysulphide inserts between two allylic positions of rubber chains (allylic C–H abstraction).
4. Crosslinks of the form **C–Sₓ–C (where x = 4 – 8)** dominate.

**Crosslink Distribution:**
- Polysulphidic : ≈ 70 %
- Disulphidic : ≈ 20 %
- Monosulphidic : ≈ 10 %

**Property Profile:**
- High tensile strength, excellent flex / fatigue resistance, high elongation.
- Poor ageing (polysulphidic links break under heat).
- High compression set, prone to **reversion**.
- **Sulphur bloom** at high loadings (S recrystallizes on surface).

---

#### 2.2.2 Efficient Vulcanization (EV) System

**Composition:** Sulphur 0.3 – 0.7 phr + Accelerator 2.5 – 5.0 phr (often **sulphenamides** like CBS, TBBS, plus a **sulphur donor** such as TMTD or DTDM).

**Mechanism:**
1. High accelerator concentration decomposes S₈ directly into short sulphur fragments.
2. Crosslinks form predominantly as **C–S–C** (mono) and **C–S–S–C** (di).
3. Sulphur donors (e.g., TMTD → gives 1 S atom + Zn-dimethyldithiocarbamate) supply active sulphur *in situ* but no free S₈.

**Crosslink Distribution:**
- Monosulphidic : ≈ 50 – 60 %
- Disulphidic : ≈ 30 – 40 %
- Polysulphidic : ≤ 10 %

**Property Profile:**
- Excellent heat & ageing resistance.
- Low compression set, high resilience.
- **Low tensile, poor dynamic flex (crack growth).**
- Safe processing (less scorchy due to sulphenamide retarder effect).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EV systems are mandatory** for heat-resistant applications — e.g., radiator hoses, O-rings, engine mounts, steam-resistant seals.

---

#### 2.2.3 Semi-Efficient Vulcanization (Semi-EV) System

**Composition:** Sulphur 1.0 – 1.7 phr + Accelerator 1.2 – 2.5 phr (sulphenamide type).

**Mechanism:**
- Intermediate stoichiometry gives a **statistical mixture** of all three crosslink types.
- Acts as the *engineering compromise* between CV and EV.

**Crosslink Distribution:**
- Polysulphidic : ≈ 30 – 40 %
- Disulphidic : ≈ 30 – 35 %
- Monosulphidic : ≈ 25 – 30 %

**Property Profile:**
- Balanced ageing, tensile, fatigue.
- Moderate reversion resistance.
- **Most widely used system in industry** (tyre sidewalls, conveyor belts, general MRG).

---

#### 2.2.4 Sulphurless Cure System

**Definition:** Vulcanization carried out **without elemental sulphur**, using sulphur donors, peroxides, metal oxides, or urethane crosslinkers.

**Sub-types:**

**(a) Sulphur-Donor Cure (e.g., TMTD-based)**
- Replaces elemental S with donors such as **TMTD, DTDM, MBSS, TETD**.
- Reaction: 2 R–S–N → R–S–S–R + by-product.
- Crosslinks: mostly mono- and disulphidic (similar to EV).
- No free sulphur → **no bloom**.

**(b) Peroxide Cure**
- Uses organic peroxides: **Dicumyl Peroxide (DCP)**, Benzoyl Peroxide, Di-tert-butyl Peroxide.
- Mechanism: Peroxide homolyzes → **free radicals** → abstracts allylic H → two rubber radicals combine → **direct C–C crosslink**.
- Crosslinks: pure **C–C bonds**.
- **Advantages:** excellent heat & oxidative ageing, no sulphur bloom, low compression set, transparent compounds possible.
- **Disadvantages:** poor tear strength, poor flex, requires co-agents (TMPTMA, TAIC) for HNBR/EPDM, expensive.

**(c) Metal-Oxide Cure**
- Used for polychloroprene (CR), chlorosulphonated polyethylene (CSM), carboxylated nitrile (XNBR).
- ZnO + MgO → ether (–C–O–C–) and metal-ether crosslinks.

**(d) Urethane / Diol Cure**
- Used for millable polyurethane rubbers.
- Peroxide + diol/triol → urethane bridge crosslinks.

**Crosslink Type in Sulphurless Cure:**
- No polysulphidic bridges → predominantly **C–C** or **C–O–C** (ether) bonds.
- These are the **strongest and most heat-stable** bonds.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (LaTeX) instead of `|` inside markdown tables to prevent syntax breakage.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sulphur : Accelerator Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{Sulphur}}{\text{Acc}}$ (phr/phr) | dimensionless | Decides crosslink type |
| Crosslink Density (μ) | $\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{2 \, \bar{M_c}}$ | mol / cm³ or mol / m³ | ρ = rubber density, $N_A$ = Avogadro no. |
| Avg. Mol. Wt between crosslinks | $\bar{M_c} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \rho \, N_A}{\mu}$ | g/mol | Inverse of crosslink density |
| Number of Sulphur atoms per crosslink (e_x) | $e_x \;=\; \dfrac{\text{total S reacted}}{\text{crosslinks formed}}$ | atoms / crosslink | CV → 4–8 ; EV → 1–2 |
| Bond Dissociation Energy (BDE) | $E_{C\text{-}S} \approx \mathbf{272}$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | Monosulphidic bond |
| BDE Polysulphidic (S–S) | $E_{S\text{-}S} \approx \mathbf{227}$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | Per S–S link |
| BDE Carbon–Carbon | $E_{C\text{-}C} \approx \mathbf{348}$ kJ/mol | kJ/mol | Peroxide / Sulphurless |
| Optimum Cure Time (t₉₀) | Time to reach 90 % of max torque | minutes | From rheometer curve |
| Scorch Time (t₂ / t₅) | Time to 2 % / 5 % torque rise | minutes | Process safety |
| Reversion Index | $\dfrac{M_{H\text{(max)}} - M_{H\text{(end of cycle)}}}{M_{H\text{(max)}} - M_{L}}$ | dimensionless | CV > EV > Sulphurless |

**Constants to Memorize (bold in answer sheets):**
- **Avogadro Number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹**
- **Bond Energy hierarchy: C–C (348) > C–S (272) > S–S (227) kJ/mol**

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tyre Industry (≈ 70 % of world rubber consumption):** Tread → CV; Sidewall → Semi-EV; Inner liner → EV or Halobutyl.
- **Aerospace / Defence:** Peroxide-cured silicone and EPDM seals (sulphurless) withstand 200 – 300 °C.
- **Medical / Food-grade:** Peroxide + sulphur-donor EV systems (no toxic bloom).
- **Automotive under-bonnet:** EV and sulphur-donor systems (180 °C continuous).
- **White / coloured products:** Sulphurless / peroxide only — prevents the yellow-brown staining from sulphur bloom.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Crosslink Density Derivation (Theory of Rubber Elasticity)

The classical affine network model gives the **shear modulus** of a cured rubber:

$$\boxed{G \;=\; \nu \, k_B \, T \;=\; \nu \, R \, T}$$

where:
- $G$ = equilibrium shear modulus (Pa)
- $\nu$ = number density of elastically active network chains per unit volume (chains/m³)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $R$ = universal gas constant = 8.314 J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

For an ideal tetrafunctional crosslink (one crosslink joins 4 chain ends), each crosslink contributes **2 active network chains**.

$$\nu \;=\; 2 \, \mu$$

where $\mu$ is the crosslink density in **mol per unit volume**. Therefore:

$$G \;=\; 2 \, \mu \, R \, T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu \;=\; \dfrac{G}{2 R T}$$

Equivalently, using the **density–molecular weight relation**:

$$\boxed{\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{2 \, \bar{M_c}}}$$

with:
- $\rho$ = density of the cured rubber (kg/m³ or g/cm³)
- $\bar{M_c}$ = average molecular weight between successive crosslinks (g/mol)
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Expected Style)

**Problem:** A natural rubber compound (density $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³) is cured to an average molecular weight between crosslinks $\bar{M_c} = 5200$ g/mol. Determine:
(a) Crosslink density $\mu$ in mol/m³.
(b) Equilibrium shear modulus at $T = 300$ K.

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

**(a) Crosslink density:**

$$\mu \;=\; \dfrac{\rho \, N_A}{2 \, \bar{M_c}}$$

Substitute (use consistent units: $\rho = 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1.1 \times 10^6 \text{ g/m}^3$, $\bar{M_c} = 5200$ g/mol):

$$\mu \;=\; \dfrac{(1.1 \times 10^6) \times (6.022 \times 10^{23})}{2 \times 5200}$$

$$\mu \;=\; \dfrac{6.624 \times 10^{29}}{10400} \;=\; 6.37 \times 10^{25} \text{ chains/m}^3$$

In mol/m³:

$$\mu_{\text{mol}} \;=\; \dfrac{6.37 \times 10^{25}}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \;=\; \mathbf{105.8 \; mol/m^3}$$

**(b) Shear modulus:**

$$G \;=\; 2 \, \mu_{\text{mol}} \, R \, T \;=\; 2 \times 105.8 \times 8.314 \times 300$$

$$G \;=\; 2 \times 105.8 \times 2494.2 \;=\; \mathbf{5.28 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}} \;\approx\; \mathbf{0.528 \; MPa}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of "Sulphur Rank" $e_x$ in Crosslinks

The **sulphur rank** of a crosslink is defined as:

$$\boxed{e_x \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Moles of Sulphur that reacted}}{\text{Moles of crosslinks formed}}}$$

From elemental analysis of a cured rubber sample:
- Let $n_{S,\text{reacted}}$ = moles of sulphur chemically bound to polymer.
- Let $n_X$ = moles of crosslinks (from swelling modulus, $\mu$).

Then:

$$e_x \;=\; \dfrac{n_{S,\text{reacted}}}{n_X} \;=\; \dfrac{m_S / M_S}{\mu \cdot V / 2}$$

Typical values:
- CV : $e_x \in [4,\, 8]$
- Semi-EV : $e_x \in [2,\, 4]$
- EV : $e_x \in [1,\, 2]$
- Sulphurless donor : $e_x \approx 1$
- Peroxide : $e_x = 0$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Cure System

| Property | Conventional | Semi-EV | EV | Sulphurless (Peroxide) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | High | High | Moderate | Low–Moderate |
| Elongation at break | High | High | Moderate | Low |
| Heat ageing resistance | Poor | Good | Excellent | Outstanding |
| Compression set | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| Flex / Fatigue life | Excellent | Good | Poor | Poor |
| Reversion resistance | Poor | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| Sulphur Bloom | Yes | Mild | Very Low | None |
| Scorch Safety | Low | Moderate | High | High |
| Process Cost | Low | Moderate | Moderate–High | High |
| Staining | Yes | Mild | Low | None |
| Colour Compatibility | Poor (dark) | Fair | Fair | Excellent (white possible) |

---

### 3.5 Why Bond Energy Dictates Ageing — Logical Derivation

Thermal ageing proceeds via **homolytic cleavage** of the weakest bond adjacent to the crosslink. Using **Boltzmann energy distribution**, the fraction of bonds broken at temperature $T$ over time $t$ is governed by Arrhenius kinetics:

$$\boxed{k \;=\; A \, \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_a}{R T}\right)}$$

- **Polysulphidic** crosslinks: contain **S–S bonds (227 kJ/mol)** → lowest activation barrier → fastest breakage → **poor ageing**.
- **Monosulphidic** crosslinks: contain **C–S bonds (272 kJ/mol)** → higher barrier → slower breakage → **better ageing**.
- **C–C crosslinks (peroxide)**: **348 kJ/mol** → highest barrier → **best ageing**.

This single energy hierarchy explains the **complete ordering** of ageing performance:

$$\text{C–C} \;>\; \text{C–S} \;>\; \text{S–S} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{Peroxide} \;>\; \text{EV} \;>\; \text{Semi-EV} \;>\; \text{CV}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Crosslink Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Polymer Chains NR SBR EPDM etc] --> B[Activator System ZnO plus Stearic Acid]
    B --> C[Accelerator Activation forms Zn Acc Complex]
    C --> D{Sulphur Source Selection}
    D -- Elemental S plus low Acc --> E1[Conventional CV System high S 2.5 to 3.5 phr]
    D -- Elemental S plus medium Acc --> E2[Semi EV System medium S 1.0 to 1.7 phr]
    D -- Elemental S plus high Acc --> E3[EV System low S 0.3 to 0.7 phr]
    D -- Sulphur Donors TMTD DTDM only --> E4[Sulphurless Donor Cure]
    D -- Organic Peroxide DCP etc --> E5[Peroxide Sulphurless Cure]
    D -- Metal Oxide ZnO MgO --> E6[Metal Oxide Cure for CR CSM]
    E1 --> F1[Polysulphidic Crosslinks x equal 4 to 8]
    E2 --> F2[Mixed Crosslinks mono di poly]
    E3 --> F3[Monosulphidic plus Disulphidic x equal 1 to 2]
    E4 --> F4[Short Sulphur Crosslinks via donors]
    E5 --> F5[Direct Carbon Carbon Crosslinks]
    E6 --> F6[Ether and Metal Ether Crosslinks]
    F1 --> G[3D Rubber Network]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H[Final Vulcanizate Properties]
```

---

### 4.2 Process Flow — Decision Map for Selecting a Cure System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Start Application Requirement] --> Q{Operating Temperature}
    Q -- Below 80 deg C --> R{Dynamic Fatigue Required}
    Q -- Between 80 to 150 deg C --> S{Ageing Critical}
    Q -- Above 150 deg C --> T[Use EV or Sulphurless Peroxide]
    R -- Yes --> R1[Use Conventional CV Tyre tread]
    R -- No --> R2[Use Semi EV general MRG]
    S -- Yes --> S1[Use EV system]
    S -- No --> S2[Use Semi EV]
    T --> U{Colour Sensitive}
    U -- Yes --> V[Use Peroxide or Sulphur Donor]
    U -- No --> W[Use EV with TMTD donor]
```

---

### 4.3 Architecture — Bond Type and Energy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Weakest 227 kJ per mol S to S"
        A1[Polysulphidic Crosslink C to Sx to C x greater than or equal to 4]
    end
    subgraph "Medium 272 kJ per mol C to S"
        B1[Monosulphidic Crosslink C to S to C]
        B2[Disulphidic Crosslink C to S to S to C]
    end
    subgraph "Strongest 348 kJ per mol C to C"
        C1[Carbon Carbon Crosslink from Peroxide]
        C2[Ether Crosslink from Metal Oxide]
    end
    A1 -->|Poor ageing Best flex| D1[Conventional System]
    B1 -->|Balanced properties| D2[EV or Semi EV]
    B2 -->|Balanced properties| D2
    C1 -->|Best ageing Brittle| D3[Sulphurless Peroxide]
    C2 -->|Used in CR CSM| D4[Metal Oxide Cure]
```

---

### 4.4 Comparative Cure-Rheometer Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time axis minutes] --> B[Torque axis dNm]
    C[Conventional] --> C1[Long scorch t2 low]
    D[Semi EV] --> D1[Moderate scorch t2]
    E[EV] --> E1[Long scorch t2 highest safety]
    F[Peroxide] --> F1[Longest scorch then sharp cure]
    G[Max Torque MH] --> G1[Conventional highest due to polyS links]
    H[Reversion Drop] --> H1[Conventional shows strong drop EV none Peroxide flat]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Differentiate between Conventional and Efficient Vulcanization (EV) systems in terms of composition, crosslink type, and ageing resistance.**

**Model Answer (to be written in ~80 – 100 words):**

| Parameter | Conventional (CV) | EV |
|---|---|---|
| Sulphur level | High (2.5 – 3.5 phr) | Low (0.3 – 0.7 phr) |
| Accelerator level | Low (0.5 – 1.0 phr) | High (2.5 – 5.0 phr) |
| S : Acc ratio | ≥ 6 : 1 | ≤ 0.5 : 1 |
| Dominant crosslink | Polysulphidic (C–Sₓ–C, x = 4–8) | Mono & Disulphidic (x = 1–2) |
| Ageing resistance | Poor (weak S–S bonds) | Excellent (strong C–S bonds) |
| Sulphur bloom | Yes | Negligible |

CV systems give high tensile and flex life; EV systems give high heat resistance, low compression set, and no bloom.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is meant by "sulphurless cure"? Name any two sulphurless cure systems and state one advantage.**

**Model Answer (80 – 100 words):**
Sulphurless cure refers to vulcanization **without using elemental sulphur**. Crosslinks are formed either by peroxide radicals (giving direct C–C bonds) or by sulphur donors / metal oxides.

Two examples:
1. **Peroxide cure** (e.g., Dicumyl Peroxide in EPDM, silicone, HNBR).
2. **Sulphur-donor cure** (e.g., TMTD or DTDM in EV / thin-wall articles).

Advantage: **No sulphur bloom** and **excellent heat / oxidative ageing** due to absence of weak S–S bonds.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Conventional & Semi-EV Cure Systems

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Full Module Question Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled rheometer diagram, explain the **conventional vulcanization (CV) system** in detail. Discuss the composition, crosslink distribution, and **two advantages and two disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Semi-EV with EV** systems. Why is Semi-EV considered the most widely used system in industry? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Conventional Vulcanization (CV) System (7 marks)**

**Composition (2 marks):**
- Sulphur : **2.5 – 3.5 phr** (high)
- Accelerator : **0.5 – 1.0 phr** (low; MBT, MBTS, CBS)
- Activator : ZnO **3 – 5 phr** + Stearic Acid **1 – 2 phr**
- S : Acc ratio typically **6 : 1 to 12 : 1**

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. Activator complex (Zn–stearate–MBT) cleaves the S₈ ring.
2. Linear polysulphide fragments form **C–Sₓ–C** bridges via allylic H-abstraction.
3. Crosslinks are predominantly **polysulphidic (≈ 70 %)** with x = 4 – 8.

**Rheometer Behaviour (1 mark):**
- Short scorch time (t₂ low).
- Sharp cure rise to high M_H.
- Pronounced **reversion** (torque drops after optimum due to S–S bond scission).

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- High tensile strength, tear resistance, excellent dynamic flex/fatigue.
- Low cost, established technology for tyres.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Poor heat ageing (S–S bonds break at 227 kJ/mol).
- High compression set, sulphur bloom, reversion.

---

**(b) Semi-EV vs EV (7 marks)**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Semi-EV | EV |
|---|---|---|
| Sulphur (phr) | 1.0 – 1.7 | 0.3 – 0.7 |
| Accelerator (phr) | 1.2 – 2.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 |
| S : Acc | 0.4 – 0.7 | 0.05 – 0.15 |
| Crosslink | Mixed (mono, di, poly ≈ equal) | Mono + Disulphidic |
| Ageing | Good | Excellent |
| Tensile / Flex | High | Moderate |
| Compression Set | Moderate | Low |
| Bloom | Mild | Very Low |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher (donors + more Acc) |

**Why Semi-EV is Most Widely Used (4 marks):**

1. **Balanced property profile** — gives 80 – 90 % of the tensile/flex of CV while providing 70 – 80 % of the ageing resistance of EV. This is sufficient for most general mechanical rubber goods.
2. **Cost-effective** — uses less accelerator than EV and no expensive sulphur donors.
3. **Processable** — moderate scorch safety; no need for extreme retarder systems.
4. **Versatile** — works with NR, SBR, BR, NBR, EPDM, CR — a universal choice for items like conveyor belts, shoe soles, moulded MRG, and tyre sidewalls.
5. **Lower reversion than CV** — polysulphidic proportion is only 30 – 40 %, so the network is more thermally stable.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Sulphurless Cure Systems

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sessional Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **mechanism of peroxide cure** with suitable examples. How does it differ mechanistically from sulphur vulcanization? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write notes on: **(i)** Sulphur-donor cure, **(ii)** Metal-oxide cure (with example polymers). List the merits of sulphurless cure over sulphur cure. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Peroxide Cure Mechanism (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Vulcanization using **organic peroxides** that homolytically decompose to give free radicals, which then abstract allylic H-atoms from rubber chains; the resulting macro-radicals couple to form direct **C–C crosslinks**.

**Examples of Peroxides (1 mark):**
- Dicumyl Peroxide (DCP) — most common.
- Benzoyl Peroxide (BP).
- Di-*tert*-butyl peroxide.
- 2,5-Dimethyl-2,5-di(*tert*-butylperoxy)hexane.

**Mechanism (4 marks):**
1. **Initiation:** Peroxide undergoes homolysis on heating:
   $$\text{RO–OR} \;\xrightarrow{\Delta}\; 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet}$$
2. **H-Abstraction:** Alkoxy radical abstracts an allylic hydrogen from the polymer:
   $$\text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{ROH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$
3. **Crosslink Formation:** Two macro-radicals combine:
   $$2\,\text{R}^{\bullet} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{R–R (C–C crosslink)}$$
4. **Termination:** Radical disproportionation or recombination.

**Mechanistic Difference from Sulphur Cure (1 mark):**
- Sulphur cure requires an **accelerator–activator complex** to insert S atoms, producing **C–Sₓ–C** crosslinks.
- Peroxide cure is a **pure radical process** producing **C–C crosslinks** directly, with no S in the network.

---

**(b) Sulphur-Donor and Metal-Oxide Cures (7 marks)**

**(i) Sulphur-Donor Cure (2 marks):**
- Uses donors like **TMTD (Tetramethylthiuram Disulphide)**, **DTDM (4,4′-Dithiodimorpholine)**, **MBSS**, **TETD**.
- Donors release active S *in situ* during cure but **no elemental sulphur** is added.
- Typical crosslinks: **mono- and disulphidic** (similar to EV).
- **Application:** EV systems, thin-walled products, cable insulation (no bloom, no staining).

**(ii) Metal-Oxide Cure (2 marks):**
- Used in **chloroprene rubber (CR)** and **chlorosulphonated polyethylene (CSM)**.
- Active agents: **ZnO + MgO**.
- Mechanism: dehydrochlorination → formation of **ether (–C–O–C–)** or **metal-ether** crosslinks.
- CR example: Neoprene W is cured by 5 phr ZnO + 4 phr MgO.
- Advantages: excellent weathering, flame retardance.

**Merits of Sulphurless Cure over Sulphur Cure (3 marks):**

1. **No sulphur bloom** — ideal for white / coloured products.
2. **Excellent heat & oxidative ageing** — due to absence of weak S–S bonds.
3. **Lower compression set, better resilience.**
4. **Peroxide cures are possible for saturated rubbers (EPDM, silicone, HNBR, IIR)** where sulphur cure is very slow.
5. **No toxic / staining residues** — suitable for medical & food-grade articles.
6. **Wide processing window** — long scorch + sharp cure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong S : Acc numbers** — do NOT write CV as "0.5 : 1" or EV as "5 : 1". Memorize the bands given in the formula sheet.
> 2. **Confusing EV and Sulphurless** — EV *does* contain small amount of elemental S (0.3 – 0.7 phr); Sulphurless = 0 phr elemental S.
> 3. **Skipping the bond-energy argument** for ageing — without it, the CV vs EV ageing comparison looks magical. Always cite **227 vs 272 vs 348 kJ/mol**.
> 4. **Forgetting the activator system (ZnO + Stearic Acid)** — this is a frequent question and carries 1 – 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing peroxide with sulphur donors** — peroxides give C–C, donors give short C–S–C. Mechanism is different.
> 6. **No labelled diagram of cure curve** — for Part B 7-mark questions, always include a rheometer schematic (t₂, t₉₀, M_L, M_H, reversion).

---

### Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table and revise before any test.**

- [ ] **Curing system** = combination of vulcanizing agent + accelerator + activator + retarder.
- [ ] **Conventional (CV):** S = 2.5 – 3.5 phr; Acc = 0.5 – 1 phr; Polysulphidic (x = 4–8); Best flex/poor ageing.
- [ ] **Semi-EV:** S = 1.0 – 1.7 phr; Acc = 1.2 – 2.5 phr; Mixed crosslinks; Most widely used.
- [ ] **EV:** S = 0.3 – 0.7 phr; Acc = 2.5 – 5.0 phr; Mono + Disulphidic (x = 1–2); Best ageing among S-systems.
- [ ] **Sulphurless (donor):** 0 phr elemental S; TMTD / DTDM; no bloom.
- [ ] **Sulphurless (peroxide):** 0 phr S; DCP / BP; pure **C–C** crosslinks; best ageing.
- [ ] **Metal-oxide cure:** ZnO + MgO; for CR, CSM; ether crosslinks.
- [ ] **Bond Energy Order:** C–C (348) > C–S (272) > S–S (227) kJ/mol.
- [ ] **Ageing Order:** Peroxide > EV > Semi-EV > CV.
- [ ] **Reversion Order:** CV > Semi-EV > EV > Peroxide (lowest).
- [ ] **S : Acc ratio compass:** ≥ 6 → CV; 1–3 → Semi-EV; ≤ 0.5 → EV; 0 → Sulphurless.
- [ ] **Crosslink Density Formula:** $\mu = \rho N_A / (2 \bar{M_c})$.
- [ ] **Sulphur Rank $e_x$** = (S reacted) / (crosslinks formed).
- [ ] **Tyre tread → CV; Sidewall → Semi-EV; Hose → EV; Medical/Food → Peroxide / Donor.**
- [ ] **Always state activator system:** ZnO 3–5 phr + Stearic Acid 1–2 phr.
- [ ] **Draw a labelled rheometer curve** in 7-mark answers (ML, MH, ts2, t90, reversion drop).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulcanisation techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT501 - RUBBERS-SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vulcanisation techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vulcanisation** is an irreversible chemical process in which raw, plastic rubber (polymer) is converted into a tough, elastic, and dimensionally stable cross-linked elastomeric material through the formation of three-dimensional networks of cross-links between adjacent polymer chains. The term is broadly extended to **Vulcanisation Techniques**, which refer to the systematic industrial methodologies employed to achieve this transformation under controlled conditions of **temperature**, **pressure**, and **time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2):** *Vulcanisation techniques encompass the entire spectrum of compounding, shaping, curing, and post-curing operations used to introduce permanent elastic memory in elastomers. The techniques include both batch (moulding-based) and continuous (extrusion-based) processes.*

Mathematically, vulcanisation is represented as:

$$
\text{Linear Polymer Chains} \xrightarrow[\text{Time (t), Pressure (P)}]{\text{Cross-linker (Sulfur/Peroxide), Heat (T)}} \text{3D Network Elastomer}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Noodle Bridge"

Imagine a bowl of cooked spaghetti strands lying independently in water (this is your **raw, uncured rubber** — soft, sticky, weak). 

- The strands slide past each other easily, the structure has no rigidity.
- Now, imagine taking a fork and randomly tying knots between adjacent strands (**cross-links**).
- When you pick up the bundle, it now holds its shape, becomes springy, and resists permanent deformation.

This is **exactly** what vulcanisation does. The **sulfur atoms** (or peroxide radicals) act like molecular "knots" that tie together the long rubber chains, transforming a plastic mass into an elastic solid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key conceptual distinctions every KTU student must internalize:**
> - **Cross-link:** A covalent bond between two polymer chains.
> - **Scorch:** Premature vulcanisation that begins before the desired shaping is complete.
> - **Cure:** The deliberate, controlled vulcanisation reaction.
> - **Reversion:** The breaking of cross-links when cure time/temperature is excessive.
> - **Optimum Cure:** The point at which physical properties reach their peak.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following are the universally accepted industrial metrics:

- **Standard Cure Temperature for Natural Rubber (NR):** $T_c = 140^\circ C$ to $160^\circ C$
- **Sulfur Loading Range:** $0.5$ to $4.0$ parts per hundred rubber (phr)
- **Typical Cure Pressure for Moulding:** $P = 10$ to $20$ MPa
- **Standard Cure Time:** $t_{90}$ (time to reach 90% of maximum torque in rheometer)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Vulcanisation Techniques

Vulcanisation techniques are broadly classified into **two families** based on the production methodology:

### Family A: Batch / Discontinuous Processes (Mould-based)

1. **Compression Moulding Vulcanisation**
   - Pre-weighed rubber blank is placed in a heated mould cavity.
   - Mould is closed under hydraulic pressure; heat transfers through the rubber mass.
   - Process: $\text{Compound} \rightarrow \text{Loading} \rightarrow \text{Close Mould} \rightarrow \text{Cure} \rightarrow \text{Demould}$
   - Used for: Tyre treads, large gaskets, shoe soles.

2. **Transfer Moulding Vulcanisation**
   - Rubber is pre-heated in a transfer pot and forced through sprues into a closed mould.
   - Better dimensional accuracy than compression moulding.

3. **Injection Moulding Vulcanisation**
   - Rubber is plasticised in a screw barrel, then injected into a closed heated mould.
   - Faster cycle times, excellent for complex geometries.

### Family B: Continuous Processes (Extrusion-based)

1. **Steam Pan Curing (Autoclave)**
   - Extruded profiles cured in saturated steam at $\approx 150$ kPa ($T \approx 125^\circ C$).

2. **Hot Air Vulcanisation (HAV)**
   - Continuous tunnel oven with circulating hot air ($T = 200$ to $300^\circ C$).

3. **Fluid Bed (LCM/Salt Bath) Vulcanisation**
   - Profiles pass through a bed of molten salt or fluidised glass beads at $T = 200$ to $300^\circ C$.
   - High heat transfer coefficient.

4. **Microwave / UHF Vulcanisation**
   - Electromagnetic energy at $2450$ MHz heats the rubber volumetrically.
   - Followed by hot-air post-cure section.

5. **Continuous Vulcanisation (CV) Line**
   - Integrated extrusion + microwave pre-heat + hot-air tunnel.

## 2.2 Classification of Vulcanisation (Curing) Systems

| **System** | **Sulfur (phr)** | **Accelerator (phr)** | **Cross-link Type** | **Heat Resistance** |
|------------|------------------|----------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Conventional (CV) | 2.5 – 3.5 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Polysulfidic ($-S_x-$) | Low |
| Semi-Efficient (Semi-EV) | 1.0 – 1.7 | 1.0 – 1.5 | Mixed | Medium |
| Efficient Vulcanisation (EV) | 0.4 – 0.8 | 2.0 – 4.0 | Mono / Disulfidic | High |
| Peroxide Cure | 0 (DCP: 1–3 phr) | Co-agent optional | C–C bonds | Very High |

> [!NOTE]
> **EV systems are mandatory in KTU answers** whenever the question asks about "high heat resistance" or "ageing resistance" applications (e.g., radiator hoses, bearing seals).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Applicability** |
|-------------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Arrhenius Cure Rate | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | $k$: rate constant, $E_a$: activation energy (J/mol), $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $T$: absolute temperature (K) | Predicts cure rate vs temperature |
| Optimum Cure Time | $t_{90} = \dfrac{0.9 \cdot V}{Q_{max}}$ | $V$: mould cavity volume, $Q_{max}$: max heat flux | Mould design |
| Flory-Rehner Cross-link Density | $\nu = \dfrac{-\left[ \ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2 \right]}{V_s \left( v_r^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_r}{2} \right)}$ | $\nu$: cross-link density (mol/cm³), $v_r$: volume fraction of rubber in swollen gel, $\chi$: Flory-Huggins interaction parameter, $V_s$: molar volume of solvent | Swelling test for cure validation |
| Heat Transfer in Mould (1-D) | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ | $\alpha$: thermal diffusivity (m²/s), $x$: depth coordinate | Cure time prediction |
| Average Cure Temperature Rise | $\Delta T_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_{gen} \cdot t}{m \cdot C_p}$ | $Q_{gen}$: heat of vulcanisation (J), $m$: mass, $C_p$: specific heat | Process energy audit |
| Scorch Time Estimate | $t_s = t_0 \cdot e^{E_a / R \left( \dfrac{1}{T_0} - \dfrac{1}{T_c} \right)}$ | $t_0$: reference scorch time, $T_0$: reference temperature | Adjusting scorch for different mould temps |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** In the Flory-Rehner equation, students often write the polymer-solvent interaction term incorrectly. The expression $\ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2$ represents the **free energy of mixing**, not the elastic term. Practice this equation at least 5 times before the exam.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vulcanisation techniques are the **backbone of the global rubber industry** with direct applications in:

- **Automotive:** Tyres, hoses, engine mounts, wiper blades.
- **Aerospace:** Seals, gaskets, vibration dampers.
- **Medical:** Syringe plungers, catheters, surgical gloves.
- **Industrial:** Conveyor belts, bridge bearings, anti-vibration pads.

The choice of technique directly governs the **final product cost**, **cycle time**, and **performance envelope**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Arrhenius-Based Prediction of Cure Time

The vulcanisation reaction follows first-order kinetics with respect to the concentration of unreacted cross-linking sites $[C]$. The rate of disappearance of cross-linking sites is:

$$
-\dfrac{d[C]}{dt} = k \cdot [C]
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the Arrhenius equation for the temperature dependence of the rate constant:

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate the first-order rate equation from the initial concentration $[C]_0$ at $t = 0$ to concentration $[C]$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{[C]_0}^{[C]} \dfrac{d[C]}{[C]} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3:** Performing the integration:

$$
\ln\left( \dfrac{[C]}{[C]_0} \right) = -k \cdot t
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for time to reach a specific conversion (e.g., $90\%$ cure, i.e., $[C] = 0.1 \cdot [C]_0$):

$$
\ln(0.1) = -k \cdot t_{90}
$$

$$
t_{90} = \dfrac{\ln(10)}{k} = \dfrac{2.303}{k}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the Arrhenius expression for $k$:

$$
t_{90} = \dfrac{2.303}{A} \cdot e^{E_a / R \cdot T}
$$

**Step 6:** Take the natural logarithm to obtain a linearised form (useful for plotting $\ln t_{90}$ vs $1/T$):

$$
\ln(t_{90}) = \ln\left( \dfrac{2.303}{A} \right) + \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A plot of $\ln(t_{90})$ on the y-axis versus $1/T$ on the x-axis yields a **straight line** with slope $E_a / R$, allowing experimental determination of activation energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Expected):** A natural rubber compound has $E_a = 90$ kJ/mol. If $t_{90} = 10$ minutes at $150^\circ C$ ($T = 423.15$ K), what is $t_{90}$ at $170^\circ C$ ($T = 443.15$ K)?
>
> **Step 1:** Compute the ratio using the integrated form:
> $$
> \ln\left( \dfrac{t_{90,2}}{t_{90,1}} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right)
> $$
>
> **Step 2:** Plug in values:
> $$
> \ln\left( \dfrac{t_{90,2}}{10} \right) = \dfrac{90000}{8.314} \left( \dfrac{1}{443.15} - \dfrac{1}{423.15} \right)
> $$
>
> **Step 3:** Compute the temperature terms:
> $$
> \dfrac{1}{443.15} - \dfrac{1}{423.15} = 2.2568 \times 10^{-3} - 2.3634 \times 10^{-3} = -1.066 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{K}^{-1}
> $$
>
> **Step 4:** Compute the exponent:
> $$
> \dfrac{90000}{8.314} \times (-1.066 \times 10^{-4}) = 10825.6 \times (-1.066 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.154
> $$
>
> **Step 5:** Solve for $t_{90,2}$:
> $$
> t_{90,2} = 10 \cdot e^{-1.154} = 10 \times 0.3154 = 3.15 \ \text{minutes}
> $$
>
> **Conclusion:** A 20°C increase in cure temperature reduces cure time by a factor of $\approx 3.17$. This is the famous **"rule of thumb"** in rubber processing: *every 10°C rise roughly halves the cure time.*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cross-link Density from Equilibrium Swelling (Flory-Rehner)

When a cross-linked rubber is immersed in a good solvent, it swells until the **elastic retractive force** of the network balances the **osmotic driving force** of the solvent. Flory and Rehner derived the following relationship at equilibrium swelling.

**Step 1:** Define the volume fraction of rubber in the swollen gel:

$$
v_r = \dfrac{V_{polymer}}{V_{polymer} + V_{solvent}}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the free energy of mixing per unit volume of the swollen network:

$$
\Delta G_{mix} = \dfrac{RT}{V_s} \left[ \ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2 \right] \cdot v_r
$$

**Step 3:** Express the elastic free energy change due to swelling:

$$
\Delta G_{elastic} = R T \cdot \nu \cdot V_s \left( v_r^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_r}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** At equilibrium, the total derivative of free energy with respect to the number of solvent molecules equals zero:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \Delta G_{total}}{\partial n_s} = \dfrac{\partial \Delta G_{mix}}{\partial n_s} + \dfrac{\partial \Delta G_{elastic}}{\partial n_s} = 0
$$

**Step 5:** After differentiating and simplifying (this is the algebra-intensive step that examiners expect students to be familiar with), the final form emerges:

$$
\nu = \dfrac{-\left[ \ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2 \right]}{V_s \left( v_r^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_r}{2} \right)}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** 
- Higher $\nu$ $\rightarrow$ tighter network $\rightarrow$ harder, less swellable rubber.
- Lower $\nu$ $\rightarrow$ looser network $\rightarrow$ softer, more elastic rubber.
- This single parameter $\nu$ governs **modulus**, **swelling**, and **permeability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical $\nu$ values for KTU problems:**
> - Soft rubber: $\nu \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³
> - Tyre tread rubber: $\nu \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³
> - Hard ebonite: $\nu \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/cm³

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heat Conduction in a Slab Mould (Governing PDE)

For a rubber slab of thickness $L$ in a mould, heat transfer is governed by the **1-D transient heat conduction equation**:

$$
\rho C_p \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_{th} \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} + \dot{q}_{gen}
$$

where:

- $\rho$ = density of rubber (kg/m³)
- $C_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $k_{th}$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\dot{q}_{gen}$ = volumetric heat generation rate from exothermic cross-linking (W/m³)

**Step 1:** Divide both sides by $\rho C_p$ to obtain the standard form:

$$
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{\rho C_p}
$$

where $\alpha = k_{th} / (\rho C_p)$ is the **thermal diffusivity** (typical $\alpha$ for rubber $\approx 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s).

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions:
- At mould wall ($x = 0$ and $x = L$): $T = T_{mould}$ (constant).
- Initial condition: $T(x, 0) = T_{initial}$ (room temperature).

**Step 3:** For thin slabs, the lumped capacitance method is valid (Biot number $Bi < 0.1$). The temperature history at the centre becomes:

$$
T(t) = T_{mould} - (T_{mould} - T_{initial}) \cdot e^{-h A t / m C_p}
$$

where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient at the rubber–mould interface, and $A$ is the surface area.

**Step 4:** The minimum cure time to reach the centre-line temperature $T_{cure}$ is:

$$
t_{cure} = \dfrac{m C_p}{h A} \ln\left( \dfrac{T_{mould} - T_{initial}}{T_{mould} - T_{cure}} \right)
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is why **thick rubber articles** (e.g., solid tyres) require substantially longer cure times than thin ones (e.g., sheets). The cure is **heat-transfer limited**, not kinetically limited.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define vulcanisation. List any four vulcanisation techniques used in the rubber industry.**

**Model Answer:**

Vulcanisation is the process of converting raw, plastic rubber into a tough, elastic cross-linked material by forming three-dimensional networks of covalent cross-links between adjacent polymer chains, typically using sulfur, peroxides, or metal oxides under heat and pressure.

The four major vulcanisation techniques are:

1. **Compression Moulding** — Pre-weighed blanks cured in heated hydraulic press moulds.
2. **Injection Moulding** — Screw-injected rubber into closed moulds (fast cycle).
3. **Steam Autoclave Curing** — Extruded profiles cured in pressurised steam.
4. **Hot Air Tunnel Vulcanisation** — Continuous curing of extrudates in hot air ovens.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between Conventional Vulcanisation (CV) and Efficient Vulcanisation (EV) systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Conventional (CV)** | **Efficient (EV)** |
|---------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| Sulfur dosage | 2.5 – 3.5 phr | 0.4 – 0.8 phr |
| Accelerator dosage | 1.0 – 1.5 phr | 2.0 – 4.0 phr |
| Cross-link type | Predominantly polysulfidic | Predominantly mono/disulfidic |
| Heat resistance | Low | High |
| Flex resistance | High | Lower |
| Ageing resistance | Poor | Excellent |
| Applications | Tyres, general goods | Radiator hoses, seals |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **Compression Moulding Vulcanisation** technique with a neat sketch, process parameters, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Injection Moulding Vulcanisation** as a modern rubber processing technique. Compare its features with compression moulding. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**1. Introduction:**
Compression moulding is the oldest and most widely used vulcanisation technique, especially for large rubber components like tyre treads, bridge bearings, and industrial gaskets.

**2. Process Steps:**

1. **Compound preparation:** The compounded rubber is sheeted out on a calender or extruded into a pre-form of definite weight.
2. **Loading:** The pre-form is manually or automatically placed into the lower half of a heated mould cavity.
3. **Mould closing:** The heated upper platen descends under hydraulic pressure ($P = 10$ to $20$ MPa).
4. **Heat transfer:** Heat conducts from the mould wall into the rubber mass, raising the centre temperature gradually to the cure temperature.
5. **Cure:** Cross-linking occurs as the rubber reaches $t_{90}$, the time to reach 90% optimum cure.
6. **Mould opening and demoulding:** The press opens and the part is ejected.
7. **Post-processing:** Trimming of flash and inspection.

**3. Process Parameters:**

- **Cure Temperature:** $140^\circ C$ to $160^\circ C$ for NR; up to $180^\circ C$ for synthetics.
- **Cure Pressure:** $10$ to $20$ MPa (to close porosity and ensure heat transfer).
- **Cure Time:** Determined by rheometer ($t_{90}$ + safety factor).
- **Mould Material:** Tool steel (hardened and chrome-plated).

**4. Advantages:**

- Low tooling cost.
- Suitable for very large parts.
- Excellent for high-durometer, fibre-reinforced compounds.
- Minimal material waste (cold runner systems).

**5. Limitations:**

- Long cycle times due to heat-transfer limitation.
- Flash generation.
- Labour-intensive loading.
- Not economical for very small parts.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Injection Moulding Vulcanisation:**

In injection moulding, the rubber compound is **plasticised in a screw barrel** (similar to plastic injection moulding) and then **injected at high pressure** into a closed, heated mould through a narrow gate. The plasticising action pre-heats the rubber close to cure temperature, drastically reducing in-mould cure time.

**Key Features:**

- **Screw plasticisation** pre-heats rubber to $90$ – $110^\circ C$.
- **Injection pressure** $50$ – $150$ MPa.
- **Mould temperature** $180$ – $200^\circ C$ (higher than compression to compensate for shorter residence time).
- **Cycle time** as low as $30$ – $60$ seconds for small parts.

**Comparison with Compression Moulding:**

| **Parameter** | **Compression** | **Injection** |
|---------------|----------------|---------------|
| Pre-heating | None | Screw plasticisation |
| Cycle time | Long (5 – 30 min) | Short (30 s – 2 min) |
| Automation | Limited | Excellent |
| Part complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Tooling cost | Low | High |
| Flash | Significant | Minimal |
| Labour cost | High | Low |

**Conclusion:** Injection moulding dominates modern high-volume production of small-to-medium rubber articles, while compression moulding remains the choice for very large parts.

---

### Question B (Option 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Describe **Continuous Vulcanisation (CV) techniques** used for extruded rubber profiles. Explain the role of microwave pre-heating in such systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, derive the **Arrhenius relationship** for cure time and discuss its significance in selecting cure temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Continuous Vulcanisation (CV) Techniques:**

CV techniques are used for **extruded profiles** (hoses, cables, weather-stripping) where vulcanisation must occur in-line with extrusion. The main techniques are:

**1. Steam Pan (Autoclave) Curing:**
Extruded profiles are coiled on metal pans and placed in steam-heated autoclaves at $0.2$ – $0.5$ MPa saturated steam ($T \approx 125$ – $150^\circ C$). Cure times are typically $20$ – $60$ minutes.

**2. Hot Air Vulcanisation (HAV):**
Profiles pass through a long tunnel oven with circulating hot air at $200$ – $300^\circ C$. Residence time: $30$ seconds to several minutes.

**3. Fluid Bed / Salt Bath (LCM) Vulcanisation:**
Profiles pass through a tank of molten salt (e.g., nitrate/nitrite eutectic) or fluidised glass beads at $200$ – $300^\circ C$. The high heat transfer coefficient ($\approx 200$ W/m²K) enables very fast curing.

**4. Microwave / UHF Vulcanisation:**
Microwave energy at $2450$ MHz couples with polar groups in the rubber, generating heat **volumetrically** (not just at the surface). This provides:
- Uniform through-thickness heating.
- Heating rates up to $100^\circ C$/second.
- Reduced cure time by a factor of $3$ to $5$.

**Role of Microwave Pre-heating:**

A typical CV line uses an **integrated sequence**:
Extruder $\rightarrow$ Microwave tunnel $\rightarrow$ Hot air tunnel $\rightarrow$ Cooling bath.

The microwave section raises the rubber from $\approx 90^\circ C$ (extrudate temperature) to $\approx 150^\circ C$ uniformly across the cross-section, so that the **hot air section only needs to provide the final few degrees and hold the cure**. This dramatically increases line speed.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Derivation of Arrhenius Cure Relationship:**

[Same derivation as Section 3.1, summarised for exam writing:]

**Step 1:** For a first-order vulcanisation reaction:

$$
-\dfrac{d[C]}{dt} = k[C]
$$

**Step 2:** Integrating with boundary conditions $[C]_0$ at $t = 0$:

$$
[C] = [C]_0 \cdot e^{-k t}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting Arrhenius form $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$:

$$
[C] = [C]_0 \cdot e^{-A t \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}}
$$

**Step 4:** Time to reach 90% cure ($[C] = 0.1 [C]_0$):

$$
t_{90} = \dfrac{\ln 10}{A} \cdot e^{E_a / R T}
$$

**Step 5:** Logarithmic form for graphical analysis:

$$
\ln(t_{90}) = \ln\left( \dfrac{\ln 10}{A} \right) + \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T}
$$

**Significance in Cure Temperature Selection:**

- The equation is **exponentially sensitive** to temperature: small changes in $T$ produce large changes in $t_{90}$.
- Higher $E_a$ $\rightarrow$ more temperature sensitivity.
- A plot of $\ln t_{90}$ vs $1/T$ is **linear**; extrapolation to industrial cure temperatures is straightforward.
- **Practical limit:** Cure temperature is bounded by:
  - Scorch risk (lower bound).
  - Rubber degradation / reversion (upper bound).
  - Mould material limits (typically $\leq 200^\circ C$ for steel moulds).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "scorch time" ($t_{s2}$) with "optimum cure time" ($t_{90}$). Scorch is the **safety window** for processing; cure is the **required** cross-link formation.
> 2. **In the Arrhenius derivation**, many students forget the integration step and jump directly to the final form. This loses **2 marks** in KTU evaluations.
> 3. **In the Flory-Rehner equation**, do NOT drop the $\chi v_r^2$ term unless the examiner specifies "ideal network" — most real systems require the full expression.
> 4. **For CV line diagrams**, students often forget to mention the **cooling bath** at the end. Marks are awarded for the complete process sequence.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Vulcanisation** = cross-linking of polymer chains via sulfur/peroxide/other curatives.
- [x] **Techniques** fall into **Batch (Compression, Transfer, Injection)** and **Continuous (Steam, Hot Air, Fluid Bed, Microwave, LCM)**.
- [x] **Sulfur systems:** CV (2.5–3.5 phr S, polysulfidic, low heat resistance) vs EV (0.4–0.8 phr S, mono/disulfidic, high heat resistance).
- [x] **Peroxide cure** gives C–C cross-links → best heat & ageing resistance.
- [x] **Key temperatures:** Mould $140$ – $200^\circ C$; Hot air CV $200$ – $300^\circ C$; Salt bath $200$ – $300^\circ C$.
- [x] **Key pressures:** Moulding $10$ – $20$ MPa; Injection $50$ – $150$ MPa.
- [x] **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$ governs cure rate; cure time $t_{90} \propto e^{E_a / RT}$.
- [x] **Rule of thumb:** Every 10°C rise in cure temperature approximately halves the cure time.
- [x] **Cross-link density** ($\nu$) determined by **Flory-Rehner equation** from swelling data.
- [x] **Scorch time ($t_{s2}$)** = processing safety window; must exceed mould-filling time.
- [x] **Microwave vulcanisation** uses $2450$ MHz for volumetric heating; requires polar rubber or polar additives (carbon black helps).
- [x] **Salt bath (LCM)** gives highest heat transfer coefficient among continuous methods.
- [x] **Reversion** = breaking of cross-links at over-cure; more pronounced in NR with polysulfidic linkages.
- [x] **EV systems** are preferred for high-temperature service ($>100^\circ C$).
- [x] **Compression moulding** is the workhorse for large parts; **Injection moulding** for high-volume small parts.
- [x] **CV lines** integrate extrusion + microwave + hot air + cooling in a single continuous operation.
